Project_Title: Design, Modelling, and Validation of Microgrid Testbed(s)
Project_Description: This project will focus on designing, modelling, and validating the experimental and physical implementation of a hybrid microgrid platform using different power system technologies (e.g., AC/DC, DC/DC). The microgrid will incorporate renewable and non-renewable energy sources and various battery management and power system components. The system is intended to operate independently; islanded from any reliance on a main grid. The system will also support multiple loads to reduce reliance on existing grid infrastructures. The project is intended to systemically evaluate and investigate the economic benefit, environmental, and social impact of microgrids on constrained communities. This project will also support investigations into microgrid resilience, islanded operation, load prioritization, and hybrid power architectures.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
Students will be provided ample hands-on opportunities to explore the fundamentals of power electronics, control systems, renewable energy systems integration, and experimental power systems while contributing to the development of a reusable microgrid platform. Students will be engaged in both analytical and experimental research activities, including but not limited to: · Conducting literature review on emerging technologies and novel approaches · Developing, validating, and testing system level models in MATLAB/Simulink or other relevant simulation software

· Designing and implementing control logic for microgrid operation via C++ or Python · Validating simulated results against experimental testbed results (e.g., relying on energy management strategies, conducting hand calculations based on power models, gathering sensor data) · Document findings and produce technical reports · Construct physical microgrid models

Project_Title: UWill Discover Interactive Drama Research Conference
Project_Description: Select Drama students will do a workshop on how to present at a research conference. Students will also create interactive theatre to help student researchers network during UWill Discover events
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Create interactive drama sessions that create networking opportunities for conference participants.

Project_Title: Engineering Healthcare for Tomorrow
Project_Description: In the backdrop of aging population, transformation of healthcare is critical. To better engineer tomorrow's healthcare, a good understanding of the current situation is essential. This project aims to compare and contrast jurisdictions where healthcare is doing well with those that are not doing well. Through qualitative and quantitative analyses, factors that contribute to good healthcare may be deduced.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Study the literature. Analyze data from various sources. Perform statistical analyses.

Create/update a living document of lab procedures

Project_Title: Aspects of Calibration for the HyperKamiokande Far Detector
Project_Description: This term project will contribute to the calibration studies of the Hyper-Kamiokande far detector, a next-generation water Cherenkov neutrino observatory. The student will assist in the analysis of Monte Carlo simulations designed to evaluate the accuracy of photomultiplier tube (PMT) angular response and the intensity distribution of diffused light emitted from LED sources used in the calibration system. Through this work, the student will gain hands-on experience in particle detector development, apply and explore optimization algorithms, deepen their understanding of particle physics concepts, and strengthen their Python programming skills. These tasks will provide valuable training while supporting the development of reliable calibration strategies essential for precise neutrino measurements in Hyper-Kamiokande.

Project_Title: Connected Automated Driving
Project_Description:
The project is centered around leveraging machine learning (ML) and vehicle-to-everything (V2X) communication to enhance the safety, comfort, and efficiency of passengers in automated vehicles. The primary objective is to create an ML-driven controller for the vehicle, enabling specific maneuvers that enhance operational safety. These maneuvers encompass:
- Following a lead vehicle to establish a platoon of vehicles and regulating speed to maintain a safe distance.
- Executing a safe lane change when the current lane experiences reduced speed, identifying potential velocity or safety benefits in an adjacent lane.
- Merging onto a highway and implementing collision avoidance strategies with vehicles on the main road.
The development of learning models will rely on publicly available experimental datasets for training.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
Key responsibilities for the student involved in this project include:
- Surveying existing methods related to cooperative maneuvering and acquiring essential tools, such as Python, for their implementation.
- Learning data processing techniques to handle large datasets and extract relevant features for training the learning models.
- Gaining proficiency in feature engineering to distill crucial features from raw datasets.
- Acquiring knowledge in implementing fundamental reinforcement learning algorithms and employing experimental datasets to train and assess the accuracy of each model.

Project_Title: Quantum-inspired algorithms for simulating classical systems
Project_Description: Tensor network and matrix-product states based ansatzes have revolutionized our understanding of the physics of low-dimensional systems, particularly the entanglement structure of their ground state wave-functions. Recently, it has been shown that the representations have applications beyond quantum many-body physics and can efficiently represent a wide range of functions that are relevant in classical systems described by ordinary and partial differential equations that arise even in far flung areas like fluid mechanics or single-particle quantum mechanics. The goal of this project is to have the student explore using these quantum-inspired algorithms to simulate a range of systems, from classical spin dynamics to the single-particle Schrodinger equation, and evaluate their performance and efficiency relative to traditional methods.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: The student will implement Matrix Product State (MPS) algorithms to solve differential equations governing classical spin dynamics and the single-particle Schrödinger equation. This requires developing technical proficiency in tensor contraction and decomposition methods while translating physical states into compressed network representations. Both of these skills have applications in both advanced numerical methods in quantum many-body physics, as well as connections to neural network techniques used in modern machine learning. Finally, the student must rigorously benchmark these quantum-inspired solvers against traditional numerical methods to quantify their actual computational advantages and scaling limits.

Project_Title: Workplace Micro-Assertion: Covert Agency when Overt Challenge is Unsafe
Project_Description: This project examines the subtle, often overlooked ways employees engage in small acts of defiance or resistance in organizational life. These behaviours are typically informal and low-visibility, yet they can serve as meaningful expressions of agency, identity, and values at work. Drawing on research from organizational behaviour, industrial–organizational psychology, sociology, and critical management studies, the project builds on an existing literature review of related constructs to synthesize how different disciplines conceptualize and study these behaviours. In addition, the project incorporates internet-based research to document real-world examples of such behaviours as discussed by workers online (e.g., through hashtags or forums), with the goal of grounding theory development in lived workplace experiences and informing future conceptual and measurement work.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
- Continue and complete an existing interdisciplinary literature review on subtle forms of workplace resistance and related constructs
- Read, extract, and synthesize theoretical definitions, frameworks, and empirical approaches across organizational behaviour, psychology, sociology, and related fields
- Organize findings into structured summaries, comparison tables, and annotated documents to support theory development
- Conduct systematic internet-based research to identify real-world examples of everyday workplace behaviours aligned with the focal construct (e.g., behaviours discussed in online forums or via relevant hashtags)
- Catalogue and organize examples using a predefined coding template (observational only; no interaction with online users)
- Summarize emerging patterns in how workers describe, justify, or make sense of these behaviours

Project_Title: Experimental Battery Diagnostics and State-of-Health Monitoring
Project_Description:
This undergraduate research project is based in the Battery Management Systems (BMS) Laboratory and focuses on experimental battery diagnostics, safety monitoring, and data analysis for lithium-ion batteries. The student will gain hands-on experience with battery testing hardware, sensor systems, and data-driven analysis tools, while working closely with graduate students and researchers.

A central component of the project is the development of a real-time, multi-channel battery temperature monitoring system to support long-duration aging and cycling experiments. The system will enable continuous data acquisition, remote visualization, and automated safety alerts, contributing directly to improved laboratory safety and data integrity.

In parallel, the student will support Master’s-level research in Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS), a widely used technique for assessing battery state of health (SOH), internal resistance, and degradation mechanisms. The project also introduces the student to standards-based battery evaluation, including analysis of the UL 1974 standard for repurposed lithium-ion batteries. MATLAB will be used extensively for data visualization, trend analysis, and validation of experimental results.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
•	Design and implement a real-time, multi-channel temperature monitoring system for battery experiments
•	Develop workflows for continuous thermal data acquisition, visualization, and automated safety alerts
•	Participate in Electrochemical Impedance Spectroscopy (EIS) experiments alongside graduate-level research
•	Apply battery diagnostics concepts including SOC, SOH, internal resistance, C-rate, and aging behavior
•	Operate and interface with battery testing platforms and software (e.g., Gamry, Arbin, MITS Pro)
•	Study and interpret the UL 1974 standard for repurposed lithium-ion batteries
•	Create MATLAB-based visualizations of battery aging, resistance, and performance trends
•	Document experimental setups, testing procedures, and datasets following research best practices

Project_Title: Lab and data acquisition work in the Turbomachinery and Unsteady Flows lab

Project_Title: Origin of first continents on our blue planet
Project_Description:
I am looking for an Outstanding Scholar student to do an undergraduate thesis on an 18-million-year-old Icelandic rock association that consists of anorthosite, gabbro, leucogabbro, pyroxenite, and basalt. These rocks were sampled in June 2025. 
Iceland has a unique geological setting in which the Mid-Atlantic Ridge is exposed on land and the island is underlain by a mantle plume originating from deep Earth (>700 km). These unique geological characteristics make Iceland a natural laboratory to study the origin of early continental crust (older than 2.5 billion years). Several studies hypothesized that early continental crust formed in an Iceland-like geological setting, whereas opposing hypotheses proposed that early continental crust formed in oceanic island arc settings, such as the Philippines, the Marianas, the Caribbeans and so on. Similarly, anorthosite, gabbro, leucogabbro, pyroxenite, and basalt form a unique rock association, and this association has different chemical compositions in different tectonic settings. Thus, the chemical composition of the Icelandic anorthosite, gabbro, leucogabbro, pyroxenite, and basalt can be used to test the different hypotheses proposed for the origin of the early continental crust. 
I am looking for a student to study the chemistry of my Icelandic samples to test the hypothesis on the origin of the early continental crust. This study will have significant geological implications for the early history of our blue planet. 

Prerequisite ESCI-1111 Introduction to Earth Science
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Sample preparation, transmitted-light microscope study, Scanning Electron Mi8croscope (SEM) study.

Project_Title: Enhancing Stroke Rehabilitation Interdisciplinary Teams: Exploring Rehabilitation Nursing Practices and Perspectives of RPNs and RNs
Project_Description: Nurses are not treated as equal partners in stroke rehabilitation teams. They lack respect, specialized training in stroke rehabilitation, and control over their work environment. The role of nurses in rehabilitation teams is not clearly defined, and their contributions to rehabilitation outcomes are neither documented nor measured. In this research, we will document the practices of Registered Practical Nurses (RPNs) and Registered Nurses (RNs) in stroke rehabilitation units, explore the perceived roles of RPNs and RNs, and map nursing rehabilitation practices to internationally established rehabilitation frameworks. Identifying and labeling the work of nurses within the interdisciplinary team will increase understanding of their contributions to rehabilitation care and help identify better ways to document and measure nurses' contributions to stroke rehabilitation and recovery.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
Prepare Poster for presentation.
Search, sort, extract, and analyze literature
Draft section of manuscript if possible

Project_Description: In this project, the selected student reviews student leadership literature and student leadership practices to develop curriculum and activities for the University of Windsor student population. Project_Title: Research assistant
Project_Description: In this position, a student will conduct an extensive review of the extant literature on the potential impact of trauma and adverse childhood experiences on the development of emotion regulation, emotion dysregulation, and affective dynamics. The student will also participate in weekly lab meetings and laboratory knowledge mobilization projects, which will provide the student with additional background in ecological momentary assessment and other intensive longitudinal methodologies to assess emotion.

Project_Title: LLM-Aided Design of Hardware/Software Systems
Project_Description: This project explores the integration of Large Language Models (LLMs) into the design and development workflow of hardware/software (HW/SW) systems. The goal is to leverage the capabilities of LLMs—such as natural language understanding, code generation, and design synthesis—to enhance productivity, reduce development time, and improve the quality of complex embedded and computing systems.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
•	Fine-tune or prompt-engineer existing LLMs (e.g., GPT-4, Code Llama) for HW/SW design tasks.
•	Build a curated dataset of design examples, specifications, and annotated code to improve model performance.
•	Evaluate the system on real-world design problems, measuring improvements in speed, accuracy, and developer satisfaction.

Project_Title: Mushroom Product Creation
Project_Description: In partnership with Highline Mushrooms, Enactus Windsor students are seeking to use the discarded mushroom stems to create viable products to reduce waste. Enactus Windsor is a non-profit student organization with students in all faculties that uses entrepreneurship to help address social and environmental issues in our community. This specific problem deals with mushroom waste and the team is currently exploring products such as styrofoam replacements, filters, fruit labels, cosmetics and more. The OS student would work with the existing team to develop their own product, first creating a prototype, then potentially presenting their creation at an Enactus competition.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Collection of mushroom stems from Highline Mushrooms. Research and develop a viable product that addresses an environmental need (and possibly a social need) and makes use of the discarded mushroom stems. Market research to ensure there's a market for the product. Potential to produce the product on a larger scale and to sell it. The student would be helped by the faculty advisor, our two current project managers, and other students conducting research on their own potential products.

Project_Title: A Space of Agencies
Project_Description: There are different conceptions of what it is to be an agent. This project aims to develop an abstract space of the different dimensions of agency, especially the dimensions relevant to ethical agency. It also involves the assessment of where different types of AI may or may not be located in that space of agencies.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Finding sources, summarising work, proofreading, and offering suggestions.

Project_Title: Redwork: The Emperor of Atlantis – Visual Art
Project_Description: Redwork: The Emperor of Atlantis is a large-scale sculptural installation that reframes the collaborative traditions of embroidery and quilting as socially engaged art, referencing the revolutionary histories of textile practices, which trace back through the Women’s Liberation and Peace Movements of the 1960s and 1970s to the embroideries of the Suffragettes and avant-garde challenges to the traditional definitions dividing art and craft that were mounted in the early 20th century. It intersects with critical craft practices, an emergent field that brings together discourses from Women’s and Gender Studies with gendered questions of value in the arts. This interdisciplinary project integrates a participatory element mobilizing people from diverse communities to engage as co-creators in the making of the work. www.emperorofatlantis.com
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Students will gain hands-on experience as studio assistants, supporting a large-scale, multi-year project through active involvement in research, creative production, and documentation.

This position is filled for the fall semester.

The student will get experience in all aspects of the research process through designing, executing, and writing up their own study.

Project_Title: Staging Identity: Exploring How Drag and Queer Monologue Performances Shape Queer Migrants and Refugees' Sociocultural Integration in Windsor and Detroit
Project_Description:
"Staging Identity" is a sociological research project that explores how Drag and Queer Monologue Performances – two theatrical methods for subverting gender expression through humour and satire – shape the sociocultural integration of queer and trans migrants and refugees in Windsor and Detroit. This project's main goal is to comparatively examine the role and impact of these performances on how queer migrants and refugees integrate into Canada and the United States. 

This is a multi-year SSHRC supported project starting in January 2026 and ending in December 2028.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: The third or fourth-year undergraduate student will work as a research assistant for 24 weeks during the school year and 6 weeks during the summer. They will participate in all research training activities, and their responsibilities will focus on data collection and management in Windsor. Additionally, the RA will assist with updating literature and conducting ethnography in two queer community organizations, as well as with interviewing participants in Windsor. They will also assist preliminary coding, and knowledge mobilization in a queer community organization in Windsor.

Mesh generation for the complex geometry will be a critical focus. The students will use a mix of open-source and commercial mesh generation tools, and will carry out CFD setup and solutions in the open-source CFD software OpenFOAM.

Project_Title: Antimicrobial resistance surveillance in environmental systems
Project_Description:
Environmental dimensions of antimicrobial resistance are increasingly recognized as critical to
public health. Human-impacted environments, particularly wastewater and agricultural
runoff, are key but under-monitored reservoirs of antimicrobial resistance genes and
environmental stressors that drive their emergence and dissemination. In the Windsor-Essex region,
the resistome composition and ecological conditions within these matrices remain insufficiently
characterized. This limits the early detection of emerging antimicrobial resistance threats, hinders assessment of environmental contributions to resistance selection and spread, and constrains the development of effective mitigation strategies. This pilot study will contribute to developing a focused molecular surveillance framework to monitor antimicrobial resistance in environmental systems.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: The student will be trained in environmental sample collection and processing, DNA extraction, detection and quantification antimicrobial resistance genes using qPCR.

Project_Title: Sport Science Equipment Specialist
Project_Description: This position will involve the student to be trained in calibrating and utilizing sport science equipment within the Sport Science Laboratory in the Faculty of Human Kinetics. Project_Description: We plan to conduct a scoping review to look globally at undergraduate RN prescribing education - what are the courses required, how are RNs regulated, etc.

Project_Title: Understanding Bots and Frauds in Research
Project_Description: As research increasingly moves online and offers incentives to participants, a growing challenge has emerged: fraudulent participation. This includes individuals misrepresenting themselves, submitting duplicate entries, or providing false information, all of which can harm data quality and the overall integrity of a study. Despite growing awareness, there is limited research that brings together what we know about this issue and how to prevent it. This project involves conducting a literature review to synthesize existing evidence on how fraudulent participation is detected, reported, and addressed.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
As a student, you will get to:

-Assist in conducting literature searches using academic databases
-Screen articles based on eligibility criteria
-Extract key information from selected studies
-Organize findings
-Participate in research team meetings and knowledge-sharing activities

Project_Title: Lab Technicians (2) - Arctic invertebrate identification and quantification
Project_Description: The Canadian Arctic is changing at 4-6 times the global average due to multiple human-induced changes. Two of the primary drivers of change are climate change and increasing urban development of the north, and both are expected to impact the organisms living there with largely unknown consequences. We are studying the impacts of both of these modern pressures on a songbird population in Iqaluit, Nunavut's capital. Because the birds' breeding success is driven by the productivity of invertebrates (e.g., insects, spiders), we are assessing variation in the abundance and biodiversity of invertebrates across an urbanicity gradient and in response to varying environmental conditions. Up to two lab technicians will be fully trained by our team to identify (to the order) and quantify the abundance of invertebrates from samples recently collected by our team during our summer 2025 field season. Candidates will work alongside an honours thesis student and graduate student mentors during their time on our team. Work may be completed during the F25 semester, but could carry over to the W26 semester. No prior knowledge of invertebrate identification or quantification is necessary, but a willingness to work with alcohol-preserved samples is a must.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Students will gain experience in lab quantification of ecological samples using dissecting microscopes, under the context of human-induced change. Students will work to sort and then quantify invertebrates from samples collected from traps. Students will join a positive and very supportive team of diverse scientists and we strive to promote a productive, but balanced atmosphere of training and mentorship where all members are valued.

Project_Title: Understanding undergraduate nursing students’ perceptions of equity in the learning environment
Project_Description:
This is a collaboration with Dr. Gina Pittman and Dr. Sherry Morrell and funded through CLIF grant #322 from the CTL.

Nursing educators are called to purposefully address diversity, equity, decolonization, and inclusion (Shinners, 2021). To achieve a culture that promotes equity across all levels of learning and practice environments, nursing must start with a thorough assessment of the academic organization (DeWitty, 2019; Barrier, 2020). Significant attention has been directed toward addressing health inequities at the population and systems levels, yet little progress has been made in identifying approaches in nursing education to enhance equitable environments for undergraduate students to reach their fullest potential. 

Purpose: The purpose of this participatory action project is to understand undergraduate nursing students’ perceptions of equity in the learning environment through participation in a guided equity walk. An equity walk is a structured process that provides guidance on recognizing, designing, and implementing effective change strategies to establish more equity-minded and culturally safe environments. 

Nursing educators in collaboration with undergraduate nursing students will critically examine institutional policies, practices, and structures through an intersectional lens. Following the equity walk, focus groups will be facilitated by members of the research team. Data analysis will take place concurrently with data collection with the aim of data immersion to inform subsequent focus groups. Within the Faculty of Nursing, a significant shift to a concept-based curriculum identifies the third year of our program as Nurse as Change Agent. This project aids overall program evaluation to improve teaching and learning and aligns with the ongoing initiatives of the University of Windsor and is innovative for nursing education with the potential to make meaningful organizational change to create and sustain an inclusive and culturally safe environment where all nursing students feel they can succeed.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
Research team members will adapt and refine a structured equity walk guide and facilitate the equity walk. We have a draft that students need to review. 
We want to offer this equity assessment both in person and virtually so that students can provide feedback anonymously. 
A student member of the research team will take field notes throughout the equity walk as part of the data collection process. Following the equity walk, participants will engage in a semi-structured focus group co-facilitated by a student and educator member of the research team.  Aligned with participatory action principles, specific focus group questions will be co-created by members of the research team and participants.

Students can help with the actual equity assessment, data collection, transcription of focus groups, data analysis and even writing of the manuscript or presenting our findings back to the Equity Circle in the Faculty of Nursing or at presentations/conferences.

Project_Title: Exploring Key Predictors and Outcomes of Stigma Perception and Appraisal among Persons with a Mental Illness in Windsor
Project_Description: Stigma related to mental illness remains a major barrier to recovery, treatment access, and quality of life. This project will explore how people living with mental illness in Windsor-Essex perceive and interpret stigma, particularly in the context of structural violence. Using a mixed-methods approach, the study will investigate how these structural factors influence stigma appraisal and self-esteem. Data will be gathered through community-based surveys and in-depth interviews with individuals from diverse and marginalized backgrounds. Findings will support the development of policy and practice recommendations aimed at improving equity in mental health care.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
Assist with:
Literature review
Participant recruitment
Data collection/entry/transcription
Data validation
Report writing
Writing of a journal article

Project_Title: Exploration of new turbomachinery simulation capabilities in open-source CFD + hands-on experiment setup
Project_Description: In this project, students will split time between two different tasks. A recent release of the open-source computational fluid dynamics (CFD) software OpenFOAM incorporates new capabilities for turbomachinery simulations, and students working on this project will explore these capabilities to determine how suitable they are for integration into workflows of the Turbomachinery and Unsteady Flows Research Group (TUFRG). In parallel, especially from November onward, a major new turbomachinery lab will be being set up for the TUFRG. Students will also be able to work with graduate students to assemble and test the facility.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
Gaining familiarity with turbomachinery CFD workflows and simulation techniques
Communication with graduate students and the supervisor will strengthen technical communication capabilities
Hands-on work will drive familiarity with fluid mechanics experiments, instrumentation, data acquisition, and equipment

Project_Title: Understanding Vulnerable Code Changes
Project_Description: Vulnerabilities are the key threat to software security. Thus, it is important to understand code changes that are affected by vulnerabilities. This requires crawling the public Common Vulnerabilities and Exposure (CVE) database to collect descriptions of vulnerabilities and code repository links. We can leverage the repository links and vulnerability descriptions to collect the vulnerability related code changes. The goal of this project is to develop a framework to collect code vulnerability dataset from open source GitHub projects and understand how vulnerable code changes differ from non-vulnerable code changes. Such an understanding can help us to develop techniques to predict vulnerable code changes.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
1. Gain experience working with vulnerability database and source code analysis.
2. Learn data analytics techniques to analyze data and visualize findings.
3. Apply one or more methods to predict vulnerable code changes.

Project_Title: Identifying Breaking Changes in Python
Project_Description: Developers extensively copy-paste code snippets to reuse existing code instead of implementing everything from scratch. However, it is often difficult to reuse code snippets because those snippets can become out-of-date over time due to breaking changes in APIs (Application Programming Interfaces). Determining breaking changes can be difficult if such changes are not well documented and requires searching through code examples. This study focuses on understanding breaking changes in Python code and developing a technique to automatically detect such changes.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
1. Gain experience working with version control systems and source code analysis.
2. Evaluate different methods on a dataset and report results.

Project_Title: The Impact of Microaggressions on the Mental Health of Chinese International Students in Canada
Project_Description:
The rapid increase in international student enrolment in Canadian universities—rising by over 200% in the last decade (Canadian Bureau of International Education, 2023)—has introduced new complexities in fostering an inclusive and supportive educational environment.

Meanwhile, international students play a unique and complex role and cannot simply be defined in contrast to domestic students (Maleku et al., 2021). Despite their significant presence, international students often face unique challenges that are frequently overlooked, including the pervasive experience of microaggressions. These subtle, yet harmful, interactions can profoundly impact their mental health and overall well-being, emphasizing the critical need for targeted research and policy development to address these issues effectively.

Microaggressions are brief commonplace, daily, verbal, nonverbal, and environmental slights, insults, invalidations, and indignities, intentional or unintentional, directed toward a marginalized group, which are categorized as microassaults, microinsults, and microinvalidations (Sue et al., 2007). International students often face microaggressions, racial bias, and discrimination in various social settings, including post-secondary educational institutions (Sue et al., 2019; Zhou et al., 2017).

Despite a substantial body of research on overt racial discrimination and its impacts, there is a lack of in-depth understanding of how microaggressions uniquely affect the mental health of international students, particularly Chinese students, who have faced increased discrimination post-COVID-19. The existing literature often generalizes international students' experiences without adequately considering cultural and contextual nuances.

Current studies largely fail to differentiate between types of microaggressions and their specific psychological impacts. Most research lacks a comprehensive examination of the intersection between microaggressions and mental health issues, such as depression and anxiety, particularly in non-Western student populations. Additionally, few studies explore effective strategies for educational institutions to mitigate these impacts. Overall, the problem being addressed in this study is the lack of targeted research and effective policies to support the mental health of Chinese international students facing microaggressions in Canada.

The problem being addressed in this study is: How do microaggressions impact the mental health of Chinese international students in Canada, both on campus and within the broader community?
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
-Support the development of a systematic literature review.
-Support interviews with international students and mental health professionals.
-Assist with the development of thematic analysis.
-Participate in the writing of a journal article and book chapter.

Project_Title: Examining Leadership Networks Among Major League Baseball Executives
Project_Description: Business guru, Tom Peters, once stated that "leaders don't create followers, they create more leaders." In this vein, the current project will use data from the Society for American Baseball Research's (SABR) Major League Baseball (MLB) Team Employee Database to analyze the breadth and depth of leadership lineages in MLB and how these relate to team performance. Specifically, we will calculate Mott's (2009) five measures of network quality (first generation network, total network, first generation ratio, extended network ratio, and adjusted first generation ratio) and assess statistical relationships between these measures and various success outcomes (e.g., win percentage, World Series championships) to determine which leadership networks have been the most successful in perpetuating leader development over time.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: The student will have responsibility for obtaining and cleaning the raw data from the SABR MLB Team Employee Database. From there, they will calculate the five measures of network success for each MLB General Manager using Mott's (2009) approach. Following, they will complete various statistical analyses to determine which networks have been most successful in perpetuating leader development over time. The research findings will be presented at the UWill Discover and other conferences and published in the form of a research manuscript.

Project_Title: Examining the Community-Building Experiences of Black Queer and Trans Migrants in Windsor-Essex County
Project_Description: The project is the next step after a pilot research project conducted in the summer of 2025. In that project, the primary objective was to conduct an exploratory study on how Black queer and trans migrants and refugees foster a sense of community and belonging, thereby supporting one another and preventing social isolation and alienation during their transition to life in Canada after relocating to Windsor-Essex County. In this next step, the data qualitative collected will be analyzed and used to contribute to and queer the literature on immigrant incorporation, which often takes for granted the premise of heteronormativity, i.e., heterosexuality as the norm, to study newcomers’ incorporation in North America. In other words, the literature often assumes that the reason why immigrants and refugees – usually heterosexual research participants – build migrant communities is to support co-ethnic or co-national heterosexual immigrants/refugees in their host societies. As such, the literature makes the community-building experiences of queer and trans migrants/refugees invisible in North America. This project attends to this issue by documenting how queer and trans newcomers carve out spaces of support and solidarity for themselves during their incorporation in Windsor-Essex County.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:

1. assist with qualitative data analysis 
2. assist with writing of manuscripts for publication
3. contribute to knowledge mobilization efforts, including co-organizing a workshop with immigrant and queer and trans communities in Windsor-Essex
4. have the opportunity to co-present the research results with the lead researcher during the the Black Scholars Institute conference in spring 2026.

Project_Title: Relative age effects: Mitigating developmental inequities in youth ice hockey
Project_Description: Relative age inequities occur when young athletes are split into age groups (often by calendar year of birth) for training, practice, and competition. Within the group, those born earlier in the year (relative older) may reach physical milestones (e.g., speed, strength) ahead of those born later (relatively younger). This results in increased attention from coaches, access to higher levels of training, and a greater likelihood of selection for elite participation opportunities. This can leave relatively younger athletes feeling discouraged, tends to compound over time, and can result in talented relatively younger participants leaving their sport. A variety of solutions to RAEs have been proposed, but few have been empirically tested and implemented in practice. This is due to a lack of evidence-based recommendations in the published literature and inherent difficulties associated with changing existing sport structures. A small study conducted in Europe identified the promising use of age-ordered shirt numbering to encourage talent scouts to account for relative age when evaluating athletes. Briefly, this approach provides explicit information with respect to each athlete's relative age to talent scouts such that the numbering on athletes' jerseys corresponds to the relative age of each player (i.e., the oldest wears #1 and the youngest wears the highest number). Using this work as a foundation, the overall goal of the current study is to adopt the design for a Canadian-led study, which will build on previous findings in a novel sport context (North American ice hockey) and age group (12-13 years). Mixed method analyses, incorporating an active control group and objective measures of performance (n = 30), will evaluate the effectiveness of the experiment. The findings will inform promising practices, and potentially provide a strategy for mitigating RAEs that can be easily implemented at the developmental levels of sport.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: The Outstanding Scholars student will be directly involved with quantitative and qualitative data analysis and knowledge synthesis/mobilization. This student will gain practical experience working alongside the research team and will be encouraged to present research findings at local (e.g., UWindsor’s UWill Discover student research conference, Kinesiology Research Day) and national and/or international conferences (e.g., SCAPPS, NASSM), as well as publish their work in the form of a peer-reviewed journal article.

Project_Title: Using AI with CAD scripting tools to create geometry
Project_Description: Engineering students acquire computer aided design (CAD) skills during their u'grad. However, other people have ideas that they would like to explore & see built, but they can't, because they don't have the design skills. 3D printing has democratized manufacturing, but you need a CAD model first. In this project, the main concept is to go from a sketch (along with a vocabulary or additional information such as colour coding) to creating a robust / valid CAD model that can be used for downstream manufacturing. With this project, interns will be working with natural language processors (Gemini, Copilot, etc.) to create Python (or VBA) script files that will be loaded into Rhino-Grasshopper CAD software to create the CAD model. There is opportunity to explore using macros for CATIA and SolidWorks as well using the same approach.  It is anticipated that a structured approach must be taken, and that along with developing a vocabulary, the AI tools must be provided examples so it can learn.

Python & code debugging

Project_Title: Head impacts in youth hockey
Project_Description: Considerable research on head impacts and equipment design has previously been conducted on professional and college players in different sports, due to the potential for brain damage and the consequences that can result from this type of trauma (e.g., concussions). Comparatively, very little is known about head impacts in youth hockey, despite hockey being one of the most popular sports for children in North America. Therefore, the purpose of this research project is to document and evaluate head impacts that occur during youth hockey games in the Windsor/Essex region. The aim of this work is to describe how and where head impacts are occurring during game play, as well as to quantify the kinematics of the impacts so that injury risk associated with them can be estimated, using modeling techniques.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Successful candidates will be trained to collect video data during youth hockey games. They will also be given the opportunity to learn now to analyze the video records in the lab to quantify the number of impacts, and evaluate the circumstances of the impacts to determine the potential for injury. Successful candidates will work with a graduate student in a collaborative research environment, both in the field and in the lab. Presentation of results from this project at future internal (e.g., Kinesiology Research Day, UWill Discover) and external (e.g., Ontario Biomechanics Conference) conferences is anticipated.

Project_Title: Assessing helmet fit in youth hockey
Project_Description: Research indicates that helmet fit can play an important role in how well helmets protect athletes. Recent work at the University of Windsor has shown that helmet fit assessment can improve how some players wear their helmets during game play, thereby potentially improving player safety when head impacts occur. However, relatively little is known about how young players (both girls and boys) wear their helmets and if educating them about proper helmet fit can change helmet wearing behaviour. Therefore, the purpose of this research project is to assess helmet fit in youth hockey players in the Windsor/Essex region and provide education to players whose helmet wearing behaviour is not optimal. It is hoped that this work will help to reduce the risk of head impact-related injuries and increase player safety in the local community.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Successful candidates will be trained to assess helmet fit using a standardized checklist at two points during the hockey season. They will educate players about proper fit, should the players need it. Successful candidates will work with other students in a collaborative team environment to collect the data in the field. Presentation of results from this project at future internal (e.g., Kinesiology Research Day, UWill Discover) and external (e.g., Ontario Biomechanics Conference) conferences is anticipated.

Project_Title: Using Arts-based approaches to create and map the help-seeking journey of women who have physically separated from a violent intimate partner
Project_Description:
This is a WeSPARK funded project in collaboration with Dr. Kendall Soucie from the Department of Psychology and the Welcome Centre Shelter which is a community based resource for women who are leaving violent relationships. 

Purpose: This research underscores the need for survivor-centered, trauma-informed approaches that recognize the diverse ways women navigate help-seeking pathways. Therefore, the purpose of this study is to employ a qualitative arts-based exploration to understand and map women's experiences of help-seeking after recently separating from a violent intimate partner. 

Methodology: This qualitative research will employ an arts-based research (ABR) design with a life narrative approach to visually map women's experiences seeking help from formal service providers following their separation from a violent intimate partner. The project centers around participatory co-creation, using visual art as a reflective medium to deepen our understanding and articulate complex help-seeking journeys across sectors. The goal is to better understand women's challenges in rebuilding their lives after separation and amplify survivor voices to understand what would be helpful when accessing general services. By visually mapping survivor journeys using arts-based methods, this research aims to capture the nuanced realities of service access, identify systemic gaps, and inform improvements in policy and practice. Women who have recently separated from a violent intimate partner will collaborate with researchers to co-create maps of their help-seeking journey and experiences, illustrating how they have navigated various systems and services to improve their health and quality of life post-separation.

Project_Title: Improving International Student Engagement in Higher Education Classrooms
Project_Description: This is a multi-national mixed-methods longitudinal research study involving partner universities from Canada, the United Kingdom, Germany, France, and Malaysia. This research aims to enhance the scholarly literature by building a grounded theory on improving international student engagement in higher education classrooms. It will also contribute to improving international student engagement and participation in the classroom, with a particular focus on fostering in-class communication among students from culturally diverse backgrounds. More specifically, the project aims to investigate the complex interplay between university belonging, social interactions, and engagement in collaborative learning among international student university beginners at both the undergraduate and graduate levels. Our team of 13 educational researchers will delve into how these factors, including university support services, accommodation arrangements, and the university climate influence or moderate these processes. Further, based on examining effective case studies with as many as 50 students from each country, we will compile a repository of best practices and disseminate successful case studies of participation and intercultural collaboration in the classroom through various mediums (e.g., academic publications, website, conferences, institutional workshops) to support higher education instructors. By sharing best practices and facilitating knowledge exchange from the four countries, the project will contribute to creating a safe, inclusive, welcoming, and enriching learning experience for all students. It will also address the gap in the international literature on how the complex interplay between university belonging, social interactions, and engagement in collaborative learning among international student university beginners compares across differing cultural and national contexts and throughout the student lifecycle.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
1. To serve as the project manager for the research project.

3. To participate in the development of a pilot study.

5. To write a first draft of a research article on the research project.

Project_Title: International Student Engagement - Book Publishing

Much of the research in this area has been conducted in the Global North, with much of it completed in North America. This book seeks to expand our knowledge of international student engagement to include a wider set of national and transnational experiences. We seek to address the shifting dynamic of privilege and marginalization that international students face. Project_Title: Shifting Landscapes: Male Survivors’ Help-Seeking and Service Utilization Before, During, and After COVID-19

Project_Title: Developing HR and OB Business Case Studies

The final deliverables will include both the case narrative and a teaching note for each case, outlining learning objectives, discussion questions, and suggested teaching strategies. This opportunity will provide the students with valuable research and writing experience while contributing directly to the improvement of business education materials.

7. Organize citations and ensure academic integrity throughout all work.

Project_Title: Class and Gender in Indian Art Cinema
Project_Description: This research project flows from a book manuscript I am completing on Indian art cinema, a corpus of mid-to-late 20th century alternative Indian cinema patronized exclusively by India’s cultural elites that shaped their class identity in the decades after independence in 1947. The project involves identifying a prominent subset of the art film, the “woman’s film,” to examine dominant tropes and themes, particularly related to sexuality and respectability, that typically foreground and shape class identity.

Project_Title: Lab technician - using physiology to assess breeding status in an Arctic seabird

Project_Title: 2. Nurse Police Teams (NPT) an innovative care approach for equity-deserving community members: A focused ethnography study
Project_Description:
[Dr. Laurie Freeman is the Principal Investigator] Rachel Elliott is working as the project research coordinator on this team.
The research aim of this project is to explore, through qualitative interviews, the experiences, attitudes, and beliefs of the members of the newly formed NPTs providing direct care to Windsor citizens experiencing homelessness, substance use disorders, and mental health issues. A secondary aim is to also explore how strength-based approaches can improve NPTs when providing care for equity-deserving persons in Windsor Essex County.

Interprofessional collaborative teams (IPT) are a common practice model in healthcare and have been associated with improved health outcomes (Wei et al., 2021). Although IPTs are common in healthcare settings, partnerships between non-medical groups and healthcare providers are unusual. Police- Nurse partnerships are rare. Typically, the police only interact with nurses within healthcare for two major reasons; either they are associated with persons requiring medical assistance (Jacoby et al., 2018; Wardrop, et al., 2023) or the police have been called in to assist during violent clinically unmanageable situations (Allen, et al., 2014). Although the disciplines of nursing and policing ideologically may differ in many ways, ultimately, both are concerned with the safety and wellbeing of citizens.  NPT were created to bring healthcare and other resources to community members where they are, instead of requiring them to travel to local hospital Emergency Departments for needed care. Current media reports suggest that NPT are working, and working well in the city of Windsor (Windsor Star, 2024). However, no research has been conducted with NPT team members to explore their experiences, attitudes, and beliefs around these new roles and partnerships.  Nor have team members been formally asked what improvements they would suggest to potentially expand and improve the current NPT program.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
Literature review support and background information
Data collection (transcribing, cleaning)
Data analysis

Project_Title: Detecting Cancer Biomarkers with Surface-Enhanced Raman Spectroscopy
Project_Description: Raman spectroscopy is a technique used to identify molecules by measuring how light interacts with them. It works well with biological samples containing water, which makes it useful for biomedical applications, but its signal is inherently weak. This challenge can be addressed by locating the sample near gold or silver nanoparticles, which greatly amplify the signal in a method called surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS). This project focuses on using SERS to detect cancer biomarkers in model systems and biological fluids. Targets include modified nucleic acids, proteins, and small molecules.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Student tasks may include hands-on experience in synthesizing and modifying nanoparticles, preparing samples for SERS, detecting molecular targets, studying target interactions with nanoparticles, analyzing spectral data, reviewing relevant literature, and contributing to scientific writing, such as a research paper or a book chapter.

Project_Title: Intelligent Control of Clean Combustion Low-Carbon GDI Engine: Oxygen Sensor, Fuel Injection, and Engine Cold-start
Project_Description:
Advanced in-cylinder strategies can decrease the dependence on aftertreatment system, which can reduce the cost and future enhance system durability. The proposed research project focuses on three important aspects affecting advanced gasoline direct injection (GDI) engine operation, including oxygen sensor performance, fuel injection and spray characterization, and robust engine cold-start strategy investigation. University of Windsor and FCA Canada have formed a collaborative research consortium. There are three main objectives in modules within the proposed project. 
Module 1 Oxygen sensor characterization and model verification
Module 2 Investigation of fuel injection pressure impact on spray characteristics and rate of injection
Module 3 Investigation of engine cold-start characteristics 
The intern will be participating in and working together on the three overarching project objectives.  The intern will involve in Modules 2 and 3 objectives to design and implement components for testing oxygen sensors, increasing response speed with better selectivity including the following:
1) To develop proper matching between fuel injection strategy and ignition strategy
2) To study the impacts of fuel injection strategy on exhaust temperature and exhaust compositions on sensors.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: The student will learn engineering design and drawing for customized components, component implementation on exhaust testing system, data analysis and reporting, literature search, to assist senior researchers in experimental set up for sensor tests.

Project_Title: Sex differences in the leukocyte exercise stress response
Project_Description: Regular physical activity (PA) across the lifespan increases cardiorespiratory, skeletomuscular, metabolic, and psychological health, and consequently, is associated with an improved quality of life and lowered risk of all-cause mortality.   PA also appears to be anti-inflammatory and may reduce infection risk compared with fully sedentary individuals.   Nonetheless, the immunomodulatory effects of PA are less studied than other health benefits.  In addition, a high volume and intensity of exercise training, as would typically be observed in elite athletes, may actually be immunosuppressive.  In addition to PA, biological sex is immunomodulatory.   For example, females are more likely to suffer from some autoimmune diseases, but typically exhibit better resistance to and outcomes after infection compared with males.   However, many aspects of the sex-exercise-immune relationship are poorly understood.  This is includes how the immune system responds to the stress of exercise and what any changes mean functionally.   As such, we plan to investigate changes in stress protein expression and function in leukocytes in males and females following exercise.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: The student candidate will have the opportunity to produce a systematic review that will serve as the background for future experiments, they will learn basic lab techniques to measure and visualize protein expression in various tissues, and will have the opportunity to present data our faculty's research showcase.

Project_Title: Tesla coil for interactive physics display
Project_Description: The project is to create a Tesla coil, a large electrical resonant transformer circuit that is a high frequency, high-voltage, and low-current source (and thus safe for students) that can lead to ionization, material breakdown, and plasma formation. Designing and building a Tesla coil will introduce the undergraduate student to the experiential learning process while emphasizing several aspects of science and engineering such as electricity and magnetism, electrical engineering, and plasma physics. The student will need to understand and build solenoids and transformers for the resonator circuit.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: The student will be expected to design the coil, itemize the required items, and construct a working high-voltage source. Because this Tesla coil will be used as a physics display tool, the student will be expected to remotely control the device using a computer.

Project_Title: Women as sport consumers - a research review
Project_Description: Research on women as sport consumers (e.g., participants, fans) is beginning to emerge in the literature, but has largely lagged behind the knowledge base on men’s sport consumption. With the growth of both women’s sport and women’s interest in men’s sport (as spectators), more research is required to understand this important group for continued growth and acceptance of women in sport. This project will lay the groundwork for additional research on women as sport consumers. A systematized review will be performed to provide a broad understanding of the existing literature on women as sport consumers, which may include, but is not limited to, sport participation, sport fandom/spectatorship, and sport gambling behaviours.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: The Outstanding Scholars student will be primarily responsible for completing the review of literature in the related areas, under the guidance of the supervisor and part of a research team with 1-2 other experienced faculty. The student will gain an understanding of how to read and synthesize (academic) research, and how to develop a future research direction from existing work. The student will also be encouraged to present research findings at local (e.g., UWindsor’s UWill Discover conference, Kinesiology Research Day) and national/international conferences (e.g., NASSM, SMA).

Project_Title: Physiological sex differences to sprint training intensity and loads.
Project_Description: Interestingly, female tissues appear resilient to many stressors including exertional heat stress and women generally show more robust innate and acquired immune responses, but women are more susceptible to muscle changes with disuse.  Given that these stimuli play important roles in muscle adaptation following exercise and recovery (i.e., pressure for adaptation), we may hypothesize that males and females should train differently to see equal results.  Consequently, we aim to explore whether the physiological responses to different (i.e., intensity and recovery as independent variables) training programs alters the stimuli necessary for adaption.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: In this project, the student will aid in research ethics development and submission, generating knowledge background, learning physiological analytical techniques including the analysis of blood and its constituents, and knowledge dissemination.

Project_Title: Ontario RN Prescribing Programs: Learners' Perspectives
Project_Description: This mixed methods study will explore learners' perspectives of the four newly established RN prescribing programs in Ontario. Given this is a new expansion to the RN scope of practice, this will add beneficial information for program developers and policymakers.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:

Knowledge dissemination

Project_Title: SRT. Supporting Resilient Teams
Project_Description: This project entails contributions to 2 studies we have completed on team resilience - interviews with entrepreneurial teams and surveys in a healthcare organization. Our overarching goal is to understand what contributes to the resilience capacity and dynamics of teams. A related goal is to provide organizational insights to our industry partner.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: The work required consists of: (a) Conducting statistical analyses (using SPSS) for our ongoing project on supporting resilient teams. This would entail assessing sample demographics and key relationships, which we will prepare for a summary report; along with (b) Qualitative coding of open-ended feedback from participants about the strengths and weaknesses of their organization (about 180 participants). Along with this, providing insights on preparing a report for participants and senior leadership.

Project_Title: Symplectic Methods for Simulating Classical Spin Systems
Project_Description: Magnetic systems host a wide variety of rich and interesting collective behaviour. Many of these behaviours can be studied in the classical limit where the dynamics of these spins is described by the Landau-Lifshitz equations. Having better numerical methods to solve these equations is necessary to explore this behaviour in more detail, including at larger and larger system sizes and at longer and longer timescales. In this project the student will explore new numerical methods to solve these equations that respect exact conservation laws of the system, such as energy or total magnetization conservation. These methods promise more physically accurate simulations, especially at long times, and should (by design) be easier to parallelize to take advantage of powerful co-processors such as GPUs.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: The student will learn how to implement and derive symplectic methods for simulating classical spin systems. This will involve understanding how to apply operator splitting techniques to construct numerical solutions to a set of coupled ordinary differential equations that preserve the geometric and physical structure (energy conservation, constraint satisfaction, etc). Using a graph colouring scheme, these methods will be generalized to systems with three to four sublattices and then made adaptive and benchmarked against state of the art non-symplectic techniques. The student will have the opportunity to apply these techniques to spin systems of interest in the magnetism community as well as implement these methods for highly parallel processors (i.e. GPUs).

Project_Title: Targeting Cell Cycle in Glioma
Project_Description:
Glioblastoma is an aggressive brain cancer of which there are currently no effective treatment options for. While the majority of tumour cells may respond favourably to chemotherapy and/or radiation, there are populations of cells called ‘Glioma Stem Cells’ (or GSCs) that are highly resistant to all current therapies and cause tumour recurrence comprised entirely of treatment-resistant cells.
With CIHR funding the Porter lab has developed a platform capable of dissecting the biology of individual patient GSCs before, during and after treatment. They have discovered several proteins that control how these cells resist therapy and support a more aggressive, untreatable tumour. This project will dissect how these proteins function to support GSCs during the process of standard of care therapy, permitting the evolution of this dangerous population of cells. The ultimate goal of this work is to discover methods of stopping GSCs from driving recurrence of glioblastoma and providing new hope for patients with this aggressive form of cancer.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
assisting with basic laboratory tasks
shadowing and assisting with experiments
support data collection and analysis

Project_Title: Assessing breeding status in an Arctic seabird using physiological markers
Project_Description: Arctic seabirds are facing multiple direct and indirect impacts from climate change. However, without assessments of impacts at different life history stages (e.g., breeding, migration, wintering), it is challenging to determine the sources for ongoing population declines. We are developing a physiological panel to assess the breeding status of birds within unknown breeding history to determine the proportion of birds are breeding in a given year, and in response to changing climatic conditions.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Sample handling and sorting. Sample cataloging and categorizing. Development and optimization of physiological assays for the detection of key markers of egg development. Running of plasma samples from free-living birds to assess breeding status.

Project_Title: Research Opportunity in the Neuropsychology of Aging, Memory, and Emotion
Project_Description: The Cognitive Health and Aging Lab is looking for one or more Outstanding Scholars to help with research projects related to clinical neuropsychology, aging, memory, and emotion. In the Cognitive Health and Aging Lab, we study how cognition changes as people reach their senior years (ages 60+), as well as how the early signs of neurodegenerative diseases, like Alzheimer's, affect memory and daily function. We also are involved in research to evaluate technology-based interventions to help older people maintain their cognitive health. Finally, we conduct research with undergraduate students looking at basic mechanisms of memory, attention, and emotion. The Outstanding Scholar will assist with projects related to these topics that match their own research interests and the current needs of lab.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: The Outstanding Scholar will work with the PI and graduate students on tasks that include conducting literature reviews, recruitment and running of research participants in behavioural studies, entering and managing research data, assisting with statistical analysis, knowledge translation activities in the community, assisting with ethics applications, and help with research presentation and manuscript submission.

Project_Title: Identification of mRNA translational control mechanisms in synaptic plasticity
Project_Description: The formation of long-term memories is the essence of human nature. In the brain, long-term memories are formed by de novo protein synthesis through mRNA translation. The newly synthesized proteins modify neural networks by strengthening or weakening synaptic connections through activity-dependent, long-lasting alterations at synapses across numerous species. Phosphorylation of eIF2α plays a crucial role in regulating the translation of specific mRNAs. Four eIF2α kinases (HRI, PKR, GCN2, and PERK) phosphorylate eIF2α at serine-51, engender a decrease in ternary complex, facilitate the translation of selective mRNAs required for synaptic modification and memory formation. Research indicates that the translation mediated by p-eIF2α is essential and sufficient for associated learning behaviour. The exact mechanisms of how mRNA translation is mediated by p-eIF2α controls learning in specific cell signaling in different brain regions is unknown. Therefore, we are using advanced genetic mouse models, cell-type-specific biochemistry, behavioural, and electrophysiological approaches to address these fundamental questions. The knowledge gained from our research will help expand our understanding of the basic mechanisms by which manipulation of the eIF2α promotes cognition. This research will contribute to our understanding of the brain and lead to the development of gene therapy to aid in various neurological disorders.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: We seek outstanding research scholars with a background in molecular neuroscience, neuropharmacology, or related fields to join our project. Your skills and expertise will be essential in driving this state-of-the-art research forward. You will have the opportunity to gain practical experience with advanced techniques, including viral vector delivery, stereotaxic brain surgery, immunohistochemistry, and behavioral neuroscience assays. You will be involved in designing and executing experiments aimed at modulating cell signaling in specific brain cell populations, including performing FUNCAT, preparing brain tissue samples for immunohistochemistry, and conducting behavioral and electrophysiological studies. Join us on this exciting journey towards making significant discoveries and fostering your professional growth and networking opportunities in a collaborative research environment.

Project_Title: Investigating the effect of mRNA translation on synaptic development and neuronal circuit formation
Project_Description: mRNA translation is crucial for synaptic development and for the building of neuronal circuits. Dysregulation of mRNA translation results in various neurodevelopmental disorders and neurodegenerative diseases. mRNA translation is involved in de novo protein synthesis. The newly synthesized proteins modify neural networks by strengthening or weakening synaptic connections through activity-dependent, long-lasting alterations at synapses across numerous species. Phosphorylation of eIF2α plays a crucial role in regulating the translation of specific mRNAs. Research indicates that the translation mediated by p-eIF2α is essential and sufficient for memory and other behaviours. The exact mechanisms of how mRNA translation is mediated by p-eIF2α in specific cell signaling in different brain regions are unknown. Therefore, we use advanced genetic mouse models, cell-type-specific biochemistry, and behaviour approaches to address these fundamental questions. The knowledge gained from our research will help expand our understanding of neural networks that govern the brain. This research will contribute to our understanding of the brain and lead to the development of gene therapy to aid in various neurological disorders and diseases, such as Autism and Alzheimer's.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: We seek outstanding research scholars with a background in molecular neuroscience, neuropharmacology, or related fields to join our project. Your expertise will play a crucial role in advancing this innovative work. As a participant, you will contribute to groundbreaking research, gain hands-on experience with advanced methodologies, and receive mentorship from experienced researchers. You will have the opportunity to gain practical experience with advanced techniques, including viral vector delivery, stereotaxic brain surgery, immunohistochemistry, microscopy, and behavioral neuroscience assays. You will be involved in designing and executing experiments aimed at modulating cell signaling in specific brain cell populations, including performing FUNCAT, preparing brain tissue samples for immunohistochemistry, and conducting behavioral and electrophysiological studies. Additionally, you will contribute to data analysis, literature reviews, and presentations at scientific meetings. This is an exciting opportunity to foster your knowledge about the brain's inner workings. Join us on this exciting journey towards making significant discoveries and fostering your professional growth and networking opportunities in a collaborative research environment.

Project_Title: Encouraging an open discussion of death by utilizing a Death Cafè in an undergraduate nursing course
Project_Description: Qualitative analysis and writing an article for publication

Project_Title: Making Nutritional Supplements and Pharmaceuticals Work Better via Materials Chemistry!
Project_Description: The therapeutic benefit of many nutritional supplements and pharmaceuticals is limited by how well the compounds dissolve and are absorbed by the body. Our research focuses on improving how well these compounds are absorbed by altering the solubility or extending the release rate of these compounds. We do this by creating new solid-state crystalline materials or crystal forms of the nutraceutical or pharmaceutical we are interested in. This process is called crystal engineering and uses non-covalent interactions between molecules to drive the formation of new materials.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Student will learn how to use high-throughput screening methodologies to discover new solid forms of nutraceuticals or pharmaceuticals. They will learn different synthetic methods to make these materials as well as characterize them using standard solid-state chemistry techniques, such as powder X-ray Diffraction (XRD) as well as IR and UV spectroscopy. Furthermore, students will determine the release rate of these materials in simulated body fluids to optimize the compounds release rate for targeted applications.

Project_Title: Politicians' Use of Multilingualism for Electoral Mobilization
Project_Description: This project looks at politicians' (legislators and political candidates) use of multilingualism for electoral mobilization (parliamentarian websites, social media accounts, as well as Householders). It seeks to determine which factors are pushing politicians to opt for different languages to reach out to the electorate and to what extent it serves their “permanent campaigning” strategy.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: The Outstanding Scholar would be responsible for reviewing literature and collecting data (knowledge of French and/or Spanish is an asset). There is opportunities to co-represent and co-publish research results.

Project_Title: Neurophysiological markers of attention during real-world tasks
Project_Description: Humans interact with machines and systems on a daily basis. This project is part of the Human Systems Lab's (www.hslab.org)'s ongoing research portfolio investigating the use of neurophysiological markers for detecting dynamic changes in cognitive functions in the real-world.

There are 2 parts to the case: 1) the problem writeup that students read and solve and 2) the instructor note (which provides sample solutions and activities to assist in classroom instruction).  The topic may be strategy, entrepreneurship, organizational behaviour, or marketing related. This project  is suitable for a 3rd or 4th year student. Project_Title: Strategic planning in nonprofit sport: The influence of language, actions, and emotions
Project_Description:
To understand how strategy works in sport organizations, it's important to look at how language, actions, and emotions shape strategy development and implementation. Since people play a crucial role in the operation and direction of sport and physical activity programs, it's essential to consider whose voices are included or left out during the strategic planning process. For example, language used and emotions expressed by leaders can influence interactions and decisions, helping them gain support, secure resources, and maintain focus on strategic goals.

This research will add to our knowledge about how language, actions, emotions, and strategy interact, showing how values and priorities are proposed, debated, embraced and resisted in nonprofit sports organizations. The findings will be shared with the nonprofit and community sport sectors, along with recommendations to enhance the quality of strategic planning processes.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: After completing the Research Ethics Board Training, the Outstanding Scholars student will conduct a literature review and be directly involved through quantitative and qualitative data collection and analysis as well as knowledge translation/mobilization. The student will be encouraged to present research findings at local (e.g.,  Kinesiology Research Day) and national and/or international conferences, as well as lead the creation of knowledge mobilization products (e.g., SIRC blog, The Conversation article).

Project_Title: Investigating cardiovascular function and body composition to better understand the health of female collegiate athletes.
Project_Description: This project will involve collecting and analyzing indices of cardiovascular function such as; blood pressure, resting heart rate and heart rate variability, as well as indices of body composition, such as; body fat (%) and fat free mass, to better understand the interplay between each on health and performance in female collegiate athletes. Lancer varsity athletes will be assessed in the Centre for Human Performance and Health, located in the HK. Each assessment will take place during the varsity program's regularly testing periods throughout the calendar year.

Project_Title: Game theoretic analysis of platform supply chain management
Project_Description: This ongoing research project aims to investigate several challenging operational decision issues in platform supply chain management (PSCM). The first component of this proposal is to develop game-theoretic models to characterize interactions of PSC members under economic and noneconomic criteria such as profitability, fairness concerns, reciprocity, and social preferences in PSCM. The second component of this proposal is to devise social and environmental responsibility allocation schemes in PSCM. The third component is to put forward effective regulation frameworks for PSC operations. The fourth component of this proposal is to establish biform game models for analyzing cooperative and noncooperative behaviour in PSCM.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
Student opportunities and tasks include but are not limited to:
1. Assist in identifying proper literature and conducting literature review.
2. Proofread research manuscripts.
3. Carry out game theoretic analysis.
4. Conduct numerical analysis by using certain software packages.
