Project_Title: Multimethod assessment of emotion dysregulation - Position is filled for the FAll 2025 Semester.
Project_Description:
Position is filled for the FAll 2025 Semester.

This project centers around improving the assessment of emotion dysregulation, or the inability to change emotions in a desired way. Emotion dysregulation is a key factor in the development of many mental disorders, yet our current measurement methods are inadequate as they often rely on one self-report measure. Given that emotional experiences involve subjective, behavioural, and physiological responses, it is imperative to measure all three components using a multimethod assessment approach. This study will attempt to gather initial data to support a new standardized assessment of emotion dysregulation.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: As a research assistant, you will help us launch the study by assisting with developing and testing the procedures, recruitment of participants for the online assessments, and oversee some of the day-to-day aspects of running the study (e.g., advertisements, participant payments, tracking). Further opportunities will involve presenting updates at lab meetings, assisting with the preparation of the second part of the study (which will involve behavioural and physiological assessments), and developing other research skills such as writing and oral presentation skills with supervision.
Faculty_Phone_Ext: Please contact me via email at alexd1@uwindsor.ca

Project_Title: Innovative therapeutic approaches for correcting dysregulated translation in autism and epilepsy.
Project_Description: Neurodevelopmental disorders such as autism and epilepsy often involve genetic mutations that hyperactivate the mammalian target of rapamycin (mTOR)-mediated signaling pathway. This research project aims to pinpoint the specific cell types vulnerable to disease pathology due to abnormal activation of mTOR-dependent translation. We will use advanced genetic models and pharmacological agents to modulate mTOR/eIF4E/eIF2α signaling in specific brain cell populations. Our study employs state-of-the-art techniques, including fluorescent noncanonical amino acid tagging (FUNCAT), immunohistochemistry, behavioral assays, and electrophysiological recordings to dissect the cellular and molecular mechanisms underlying these disorders. Our ultimate goal is to identify the cell types with dysregulated translation and develop and test targeted therapeutic strategies to correct these dysregulated translational regulation processes, potentially transforming the treatment landscape for autism and epilepsy.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: We seek outstanding research scholars with a background in molecular neuroscience, neuropharmacology, or related fields to join our project. Your expertise will play a crucial role in advancing this innovative work. As a participant, you will contribute to groundbreaking research, gain hands-on experience with advanced methodologies, and receive mentorship from experienced researchers. You will be involved in designing and executing experiments aimed at modulating mTOR/eIF4E signaling in specific brain cell populations, including performing FUNCAT, preparing brain tissue samples for immunohistochemistry, and conducting behavioral and electrophysiological studies. Additionally, you will contribute to data analysis, literature reviews, and presentations at scientific meetings. This is an exciting opportunity to foster your professional growth and networking opportunities in a collaborative research environment. Join us on this exciting journey towards making significant discoveries and fostering your professional growth and networking opportunities in a collaborative research environment.

Project_Title: Targeted gene therapy for ISR kinase inhibition to combat memory loss in Alzheimer's Disease
Project_Description: Alzheimer's disease (AD) is characterized by the accumulation of cellular stress and progressive neuronal loss, leading to cognitive decline and memory loss. Targeting the integrated stress response (ISR) pathways, specifically eIF2α kinases (PERK, PKR, and GCN2) and phosphorylated eIF2α (p-eIF2α), presents a promising therapeutic strategy for treating AD and rescuing memory loss. Despite the potential, cell-type-specific studies targeting the ISR in AD are limited. This project aims to address this gap by developing a gene therapy that delivers short hairpin RNA (shRNA) sequences to knock down eIF2α-regulating kinases in specific cell types in the 5xFAD mouse model of AD. This study aims to elucidate the cell-type-specific effects of ISR inhibition on AD pathology and memory, paving the way for targeted gene therapy approaches to treat Alzheimer's disease.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: We seek outstanding research scholars with expertise in cellular and molecular neuroscience, neuropharmacology, or related fields to join our innovative project. Your skills will be essential in driving this state-of-the-art research forward. You will have the opportunity to gain practical experience with advanced techniques, including viral vector delivery, stereotaxic brain surgery, immunohistochemistry, and behavioral neuroscience assays. This project provides an interdisciplinary research environment that integrates cellular and molecular biology, gene therapy, and neurobehavioral analysis to protect against memory loss in Alzheimer's disease. In addition, you will receive mentoring from expert researchers, which will enhance your professional growth and provide valuable networking opportunities within a collaborative and dynamic research setting at the Department of Biomedical Sciences.

Project_Title: The hype of Artificial Intelligence (AI)

Project_Title: Sustainability assessment in geotechnical engineering
Project_Description: This project addresses a critical gap in current sustainability assessments for civil engineering systems by focusing on the often-overlooked social impacts. The challenge lies in quantifying 'the immeasurable' aspects of social sustainability. This project aims to identify relevant sustainability indicators, with a specific focus on the social aspects, to comprehensively evaluate the sustainability of common geo-structures (e.g., foundations, retaining structures, and slopes). The project further seeks to develop a multi-criteria decision analysis framework that utilizes these indicators for a comprehensive and holistic sustainability assessment in geotechnical engineering.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:

-identify and develop social indicators relevant to geo-structures of interest
-develop a multi-criteria decision analysis framework

-learn climate mapping tools and GIS to conduct vulnerability assessments of selected regions of interest.

Project_Title: The Appropriateness of the 40 km Weather Station Rule of Thumb
Project_Description: In Ontario, the nearest weather station’s value of a Fire Weather Index (FWI) system variable may be treated as being representative of the area within a 40 km radius, but is this always appropriate? Using historical weather station data, differences in observations located within a small radius can be used as measures of error related to this "Nearest Neighbor" approximation. These errors will be used to study how they depend on location (different regions of Ontario), time (e.g., month), FWI system variable (e.g., FWI, ISI, BUI, etc.), and variable magnitude (e.g., risk classification bins). The expected values and variabilities of these errors are of interest to investigate the reliability of this simple approximation, as well as to create benchmarks to contrast the performance of more elaborate spatial interpolation methods against in future studies.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: The student will learn about Canada's Fire Weather Index system and gain practical experience analyzing spatial data using the statistical software R. In addition to constructing a relevant data set of errors based on daily weather station observations, they will perform exploratory data analysis to investigate the dependencies between these errors and several predictors of interest. This will involve the use of both visualizations as well as some basic statistical models.

Project_Title: Battery Thermal Management Systems for Electric Vehicles
Project_Description: Latest regulatory trends implemented in order to limit emissions combined with research advances in alternative fuels have paved the road toward vehicle electrification. Major original equipment manufacturers (OEMs) have already marketed electric vehicles in large scale but apart from business strategies and policies, the real engineering problems must be addressed. Li-ion batteries are a promising technology for energy storage; however, their low energy density and complex electro-chemical nature, compared to fossil fuels, presents additional challenges. Their complex nature and strong temperature dependence during operation must be studied with additional accuracy capable to predict their behaviour.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Students will conduct research to identify sources of heat in batteries for electric vehicles. They will work closely with graduate students to develop methods to cool the batteries. They will investigate plating and other behaviours that affect the optimal performance of batteries. Students will use COMSOL Multiphysics for modelling.

Project_Title: Self-Immolative Polymers, Secondary Research
Project_Description:
Self-immolative polymers are materials that depolymerise when exposed to an external stimulus. This
triggered degradation makes them ideal for high-value applications such as drug delivery, where they
could be used for selective targeting of disease states through controlled release of active
pharmaceutical ingredients. This rapidly expanding field has attracted considerable multidisciplinary
attention, with contributors from across the chemical and materials disciplines. However, this broad
interest has led to the scholarship within the field lacking uniform standards when reporting
characterisation data, physical properties and synthetic (and degradative) procedures. The Trant group
seeks to add to the scholarship within this area by producing a comprehensive book to set the gold
standard within the literature. This will involve extensive review and comparison of the literature, with
sections on synthesis, characterisation and phsyico-mechanical properties. Students engaged with this
project will be involved in extensive literature research directed towards this goal and will be trained
in techniques for searching and obtaining literature data and will present their findings in regular
meetings. They will furthemore make written contributions to appropriate chapters within the book,
receiving authorship credit for their work. The study of these techniques will additionally translate in
future years towards their lab-based studies within the group.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
Research, writing and communication. Senior students will also be given the opportunity for
supervision after a year once well established in the group. Small team collaboration is essential.

Project_Title: Smart Cementitious Materials
Project_Description: This project aims to develop functionalized cementitious materials for various applications by embedding sensors and Internet of Things (IoT) technology into cement-based materials to monitor structural health, environmental conditions, and material performance in real-time. Students will explore the integration of advanced materials like graphene and conductive fibers to enhance the properties of traditional cementitious materials. The project combines materials science, civil engineering, and digital technology, with a goal to not only improve the longevity and safety of construction materials but also to pave the way for sustainable and intelligent infrastructure development.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:

-Hands-on experience in synthesizing cementitious materials integrated with smart technologies, such as embedding sensors or IoT devices within the material matrix.
-Performing a variety of tests to assess the mechanical properties, durability, and responsiveness of the developed materials to various environmental conditions.
-Implementing and managing data collection from embedded sensors, followed by analysis to interpret 
-Evaluating the environmental impact of the developed materials to ensure they align with sustainable construction practices.
- Working in multidisciplinary teams, often collaborating with industry partners or academic researchers, to foster a real-world learning environment.
- Developing skills in documenting research findings, preparing reports, and presenting outcomes to peers, faculty, and industry stakeholders.
- Encouraging creative problem-solving to overcome challenges encountered during the development and testing phases.
- Gaining proficiency in specialized software and tools used in material testing and data analysis.
- Opportunities for networking with professionals in the field, which can lead to internships, research positions, or future employment prospects.

Project_Title: Design of omniphobic surfaces
Project_Description: Omniphobic (liquid-repellent) surfaces found in nature have great potential for enabling novel and emerging products and technologies to facilitate our daily lives. One example is the wingless arthropods, which evolved a highly textured, hierarchically arranged surface pattern that allows mechanical robustness and wetting resistance at elevated hydrostatic pressures. Based on the obtained liquid repellence of various liquids, the project will provide general design criteria for robust omniphobic surfaces.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
1. Collect and analyze the recent journal publications on the design of omniphobic surfaces. 
2. Help developing a numerical model of a superhydrophobic surface. 
3. Create the 3D computer geometry of a surface pattern of a proposed superhydrophobic surface.

Project_Title: Regulation of long-term memory via translational control in astrocytes
Project_Description: Long-term memory can be enhanced by suppressing the evolutionary-conserved intracellular signaling pathway, Integrated Stress Response (ISR). The ISR allows cells to cope with distinct cellular stresses by modulating the production of new proteins. Suppression of the ISR via genetic or pharmacological manipulations enhances long-lasting memory in healthy mice and corrects memory deficits in animal models of Alzheimer’s disease. Despite significant progress in understanding the underlying mechanisms by which the ISR controls cognitive functions, several key questions remain unanswered. For instance, the role of the ISR in astrocytes, non-neuronal cells that are implicated in cognitive processes, is not yet known. Also, the mechanisms that link neuronal activity to suppression of the ISR need further understanding. Therefore, we are using advanced genetic mouse models, cell-type-specific biochemistry, behavioral, and electrophysiological approaches to address these fundamental questions. The knowledge gained from our research will help expand our understanding of the basic mechanisms by which manipulation of the ISR promotes cognition. Most importantly, it has the potential to facilitate and refine the development of gene therapy based on ISR modulation to treat memory loss in diseases such as Alzheimer’s and dementia.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: We are investigating the role of the ISR in astrocytes and its impact on cognitive processes in health and disease. We invite passionate and enthusiastic students to join us in this exciting venture. We offer students the opportunity to explore this research area using a combination of cutting-edge approaches. Students will gain hands-on laboratory experience working alongside skilled researchers in a state-of-the-art lab setting. They will conduct biochemistry, behavioral science, and electrophysiology experiments, engage with field experts, attend conferences, and be duly acknowledged in the publications. Come and be a part of this exhilarating journey towards discovering breakthroughs in this fascinating field!

Furthermore, this study can be used in the future to design better fall detection gear that will be able to protect vulnerable bodies and reduce the number of fatal injuries caused by falls each year. (figure out which bio signal will detect quickly, not just accuracy, speed also)

Project_Title: Characterization of PLK4 protein-protein interactions.
Project_Description: The student will be part of a team expressing proteins involved in cell cycle regulation in mammalian, bacterial and insect cells. Specifically, the goal is to characterize the interaction we see between PLK4 and GADD45a and PLK4 and JAK2. These are all important cell cycle regulators that when expressed abnormally are correlated with the incidence of cancer.

Project_Title: UWill Discover Ambassador
Project_Description: UWill Discover Sustainable Futures is a year-long peer-reviewed project where students share their scholarship, research, and creative activities in academic and public settings. In the fall and winter semesters, we host pre-conference workshops and a writing retreat. Our 2024 conference will be held on March 19 - 22, 2024. The project includes a series of events such as workshops (for developing research proposals, presentations and posters), thematic presentations (plenary sessions), the main conference activities (held in March) and the publication of student work on scholar.uwindsor.ca, preparation for the UWill Discover Journal, and more.

Project_Title: Investigation of Ionic Wind for Cooling Electrical Components
Project_Description: Ionic wind is air flow caused by its local ionization while subjected to very high potential electric field between sharp and blunt electrodes. The ions are attracted to the electrode of lower relative potential and induce neutral molecules to flow through transfer of their momentum. A simple ionic wind apparatus has been constructed in our laboratory and preliminary tests have been conducted. This project involves using the facility to obtain additional experimental data and to consider modifications to improve its performance in cooling electrical components.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Learn experimental methods of measuring velocities such as hot-wire anemometry and surface temperatures using thermal cameras. Apply MatLab skills to image processing of thermal images.

Project_Title: Exploring Social Media Insights through Emotion and Sentiment Analysis
Project_Description: In the digital age, social media has become a powerful platform for individuals and businesses to express opinions, share experiences, and engage with others. Understanding the sentiments and emotions expressed on social media platforms is crucial for various stakeholders, including businesses, governments, and researchers. The main objective of this project is to develop a comprehensive social listening platform capable of gathering, processing, and extracting valuable insights from social media data. By employing emotion and sentiment analysis, we seek to provide actionable information to support decision-making processes, enhance customer experiences, and gain a deeper understanding of public sentiment on various topics with a particular focus on the Healthcare and Agriculture domains.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
Hands-on Experience: Students involved in this project can gain practical, hands-on experience in data collection, preprocessing, and analysis. They will have the chance to work with real-world social media data, improving their skills in data science, natural language processing (NLP), and machine learning.
Interdisciplinary Learning: The project combines elements of healthcare, agriculture, AI, and social media analysis. Students from diverse backgrounds, including computer science, data science, healthcare, and agriculture, can collaborate and learn from each other, fostering interdisciplinary skills.
Research Opportunities: This project provides an opportunity for exploring cutting-edge AI techniques in emotion and sentiment analysis within specific domains like healthcare and agriculture.
Problem-Solving Skills: Students will be tasked with solving real-world problems related to sentiment analysis and decision support in healthcare and agriculture. This helps them develop critical problem-solving skills that are transferable to a wide range of careers.
Contributing to Society: The results of this project can have a positive impact on healthcare and agriculture by improving decision-making processes, enhancing customer experiences, and gaining insights that can lead to better practices and policies.

Project_Title: Community Engagement Assistant
Project_Description: Undergraduate students who have good connections with the African, Caribbean and Black communities will help in social media mobilizing and community engagement through translating objectives and findings in a research project that will explore how African, Caribbean and black women experience racism.  The research is undertaken by an interdisciplinary group of scholars in the Humanities and Social Sciences (example - Sociology, Women's and Gender Studies), Education and the Faculty of Law.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Improved understanding of African, Caribbean and Black communities in Windsor Essex especially with regards to racialized barriers; working with a wide section of students, researchers and community groups; developing skills in communication, publicity, engaging with a wide and varied audience; understanding of how research can be translated and mobilized for the larger community; developing improved comprehension of issues related to equity and diversity and how they are experienced in the everyday world; enhancing one's ability to express an idea in different communication modes.

Project_Title: Female Infertility, Decision Making and Behavior
Project_Description: Increasing number of women delay childbearing age and hence the female infertility rate is on the rise (CDC,2022). Dealing with infertility, treating it or preventing it, involves high monetary, psychological and physiological costs and significantly impacts the decision making and behavior of women and their partners. Nevertheless, the studies in consumer psychology domain that look into infertility are scarce. This proposal intends to fill the current gap in literature by investigating the impact of receiving information about female infertility on motivational orientation, decision-making process and behavior. Particularly, this project intends to investigate how receiving information about female infertility may change the way individuals process information.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Literature Review

Project_Title: Argumentation and Extremism
Project_Description: This is an ongoing project that investigates the ways argumentation has been used by groups defined as "extremist" to move from the margins of society and become part of the status quo. We explore both successful (i.e. suffragists) and unsuccessful (i.e. Occupy Wall Street) groups. Early chapters on definitions and the nature of argumentation theory are complete. Further chapters exploring case studies are in process. Future writing reflecting on the lessons learned and the ways argumentation can be used constructively to enhance social movements will be conducted over the coming terms. This is a team project currently involving faculty and graduate students, but looking to involve at least one undergraduate researcher.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: This is an opportunity to be mentored by experienced faculty and graduate students, joining a project that is the third year of development. Student will be part of the development of case studies and the writing of  papers drawn from the research. The student will be part of the development of materials for a web site that supports the projects and makes our research available to high school students. The graduate students involved have so far presented at three international conferences. The incoming undergraduate will have the opportunity to present at future conferences.

Project_Title: Role of the Retinoblastoma family in development
Project_Description: Retinoblastoma (Rb) was the first tumour suppressor gene discovered. Rb coordinates cell division and differentiation along with family members Rbl1 and Rbl2. Though various cell types exit the cell cycle in embryos missing the Rb-family, their absence results in embryonic lethality. To reveal the essential role of the Rb-family during embryonic development, we have collected single-cell RNA-seq profiles in a time-series of embryonic development. Students will analyze this data and perform validation experiments to identify cellular and molecular differences across embryonic lineages caused by the removal of the Rb-family.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
-immunohistochemistry to validate embryonic changes in gene expression
-flow cytometry to validate cellular changes in mutant embryos
-CRISPR/Cas9-mediated gene knockouts to dissect the Rb-related network
-directed differentiations to evaluate the effect of knockouts on cell cycle exit
-data visualization using ggplot (R)
-data analysis using Seurat (R)
-R programming (customized analysis of single cell RNA-seq dataset)
-statistical analysis

Project_Title: Theatre Criticism and Contemporary Canadian Feminist Theatre
Project_Description: This project gives the OS student hands-on research experience on multiple projects engaging with the intersections of theatre criticism, gender and feminism, and theatre and performance in Canada. It will include analyzing theatre reviews of feminist theatre productions from the 1980s to present; researching and writing about topics related to contemporary Canadian feminist theatre; transcribing and analyzing artist and audience interviews; and supporting the completion of an edited volume on playwright Hannah Moscovitch. The students will be mentored in research skills and invited to team meetings to learn from scholars and artists across the country. They will also be encouraged to present their work at the UWill Discover conference and/or other events of interest.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
-gather theatre reviews of feminist theatre productions from the 1980s to present
-analyze theatre reviews and social media posts using qualitative data analysis (QDA) software (training will be provided)
-gather and summarize primary and secondary sources related to the work of playwright Hannah Moscovitch, including archival materials and photographs
-transcribe and analyze artist and audience interviews
-contribute to research team meetings
-contribute to publications and presentations

Project_Title: Social Media/communications Internship with the Canadian Percussion Network
Project_Description:
The Canadian Percussion Network (CPN) connects percussionists from across the country to enhance percussion pedagogy, performance, and research. Born out of challenges with teaching percussion students at the onset of the COVID-19 pandemic, the network began as an informal gathering place through online events during the 2020-21 academic year.  

With support from the SSHRC Connections program, we offered an in-person festival at McMaster in April of 2022. Titled The Space Between, this event brought together dozens of percussionists from five provinces as well as several from the US to explore connections between percussion research and performance.

Thanks to a Partnership Development Grant (PDG) from the Social Sciences and Humanities Research Council of Canada (SSHRC), we are pleased to continue offering a combination of virtual and in-person events free of charge to connect the Canadian percussion community through 2026.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
Knowledge transfer, networking, and artistic connection are the primary objectives of the CPN. We are seeking an Outstanding Scholar with communications, social media marketing, and/or graphic design skills to assist with the CPN’s social media outreach initiatives. 

Successful candidates will work closely with faculty members from the University of Windsor, McMaster University, McGill University, University of Manitoba, and Acadia University to develop and execute a social media/communications campaign for the CPN. The goals of the campaign are to 1) promote the virtual and in-person CPN events; 2) promote calls for participation in various CPN activities (e.g., research projects, collaborations, etc.); 3) share the resources/knowledge generated through CPN activities, events, and/or research projects; and 4) share any resources/information deemed to be of interest to the CPN stakeholders/community.

Successful candidates will also have the opportunity to travel to attend the in-person events in Quebec, Manitoba, Nova Scotia, and/or Ontario (approximately $1500 per event will be provided to support travel). 

A music/percussion background is preferred but not required for this position.

Project_Title: Sexual Violence Prevention in Teacher Education
Project_Description: This project inquires into the development of a pedagogy of social change among teacher candidates who take a service learning course on sexual violence prevention education (developed and taught by the PI). The action component of the project involves teacher candidates developing and implementing consent programs for youth in secondary schools. In this project, Participatory Action Research involves collaborating with emerging teachers to study their experiences in a the service learning course. Participants/co-researchers will be involved in validating research questions, collecting and analyzing data, and mobilizing knowledge in academic and non-academic settings.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: The Outstanding Scholar will be engaged in a variety of tasks including assisting the team with a literature search and annotated bibliography, recruitment, data collection and analysis, writing, and knowledge mobilization. They will ideally be someone who is interested in education and preventing sexual violence in schools.

Project_Title: Preparation of a State of Science Report of Community Annoyance and Human Health Impacts from Airport and Aircraft Operations
Project_Description: The main project task will be to work with the advisor and post doc to research and assist in the preparation of a State of the Science Report focused on a critical review of current and relevant studies related to community annoyance and human health impacts due to airport and aircraft operations. This activity is to address the existence of conflicting methodologies and data given in the current literature regarding noise community impacts from noise associated with in-flight airport operations.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: The student will gain experience in research and reviewing literature pertaining to airport and aircraft noise and will also be involved in the writing and editing of the report. The student may also participate in the writing of paper(s) for future journal publication. An excellent opportunity for someone who will continue to a MASc or PhD level.

Project_Title: Developing and Evaluating an EDI Training Curriculum designed for Health Care Professionals
Project_Description:
Part 1 of this  project will entail designing a research evaluation of an EDI Training that will be delivered to medical staff at the NICU at McMaster Children's Hospital in Hamilton, ON. With my guidance and supervision, we will be working alongside a team of Medical professionals to review the existing curriculum and develop a survey comprised of validated measures that will be used to improve evaluation of their EDI Training. This student will work alongside a medical student at McMaster who has extensive experience with the first phase of this research.

Part 2 o this project will be focused on curriculum design. With my guidance and supervision, the student will be asked to research EDI-relevant material to improve the existing curriculum in preparation for a new training program to begin in Fall 2023 with Medical professionals.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
Part 1
 - determine appropriate constructs to evaluate
 - determine appropriate validated research measures
 - develop a survey instrument to be distributed to health care professionals (online)

Part 2
 - assist in research relevant EDI concepts and information
 - assist in curriculum development
 - develop PowerPoints to support delivery of material for EDI-training

Project_Title: Resilience and self-exploration through drag
Project_Description: The purpose of this project is to better understand whether drag performers see their activities as a medium to build resilience and explore their gender identity. This will be completed through interviews with drag kings, drag queens, and bio queens; participants of all gender identities (e.g., cis, trans, non-binary) will be included.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Review relevant research literature, transcribe interviews, participate in data analysis, engage in knowledge mobilization activities (e.g., prepare manuscripts for publications, prepare presentations for conferences)

Project_Title: Investigation of Transport Phenomena for Performance Improvement of Energy Systems
Project_Description:
This research focuses on sustainable energy systems and the main contributions are pertaining to investigations of transport phenomena to improve their overall performance. The main contributions are to apply entropy and exergy-based concepts to multiphase systems and gain insight into transport phenomena in:
1) bubbly mixed flows to design new predictive models,
2) phase changes and heat recovery to develop design tools for improved energy efficiency.
The unique features explored in this proposal are the parallels between momentum, energy, and mass transport with respect to entropy and the Second Law of Thermodynamics.
The main application in this proposal is the thermochemical Cu-Cl cycle for hydrogen production, an advantageous technology that could produce a promising fuel of the future, hydrogen, at a large scale, and alleviate climate change concerns. The focus of this proposal is to gain a better understanding of transport phenomena in the unit operations and make future integration of the cycle possible by identifying and minimizing losses via the Second Law of Thermodynamics. Limited experimental studies of mass transfer, heat transfer and momentum based on the Second Law of Thermodynamics that apply to the Cu-Cl cycle for hydrogen production exist in the literature. Hence, the goal of this research program is to provide detailed information on the irreversibilities within the cycle and improve its performance.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
The research provides students with important skills in thermal and fluid engineering for applications relating to sustainable energy technologies, as follows:
•	students will be applying their knowledge base to develop new technological innovations. 
•	Students will be trained in experimental processes to perform preliminary tests with the dissolution cell and crystal recovery system. This will include training for proper use and calibration equipment. 
•	Students become proficient in experimental setup design, data collection, data interpretation, and formulation of expressions that identify irreversibilities in fluid flows. 
•	Students will be cultured with forward-thinking fundamental knowledge and experimental expertise associated with sustainable energy systems. 
•	Students will be valuable in the energy economy sector in Ontario, in Canada, and internationally as they will possess knowledge vital to protecting our environment, ensuring our safety, and securing a sustainable energy future for generations to come.

Project_Title: UWill Discover Journal
Project_Description: The UWill Discover Sustainable Futures project is a year-long project where students learn about research dissemination, present at a conference, attend workshops, publish their works on scholar.uwindsor.ca, and publish a journal. Students involved in the UWill Discover projects can also compete to participate in the Model United Nations in Washington DC.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
•	Collect, assess, and rank submissions for the first annual UWill Discover Journal
•	Copy-editing and some graphic design for the UWill Discover Journal
•	Review and summarize undergraduate research websites to collect and summarize resources for the University of Windsor
•	Find and review other student research journals to inform how the journal will be completed.
•	Managing unstructured metadata to upload to the institutional repository (scholar.uwindsor.ca)
Become an official editor of our first annual UWill Discover Journal. 
The UWill Discover Project is partly funded by the Social Sciences and Humanities Research Council and the Office of the Vice President Research and Innovation.

Editors will also follow an experiential learning model informed by the Human Development and Capability Approach which is the underpinning framework for the United Nations Human Development Index and Human Development Reports. This project will work through the Outstanding Scholars Coordinator who is also an adjunct learning specialist with the Faculty of Science. The project is in partnership with the Leddy Library. Students in the project will also work with Pascal Calarco.

Project_Title: Scholarship to Improve Health Equity
Project_Description: The Outstanding Scholars will assist with literature searches, and the summary of findings, related to topics that examine equity, diversity, and inclusion and their impacts on the health of individuals and communities.  There is potential for publication of this gathered knowledge in peer-reviewed, scholarly journals, and potential presentation of this information at upcoming conferences.  Knowledge of APA 7th edition would be beneficial, but not mandatory.  Thank you!
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: The Outstanding Scholar will locate, review, and assist with summarizing the literature relevant to Health Equity for diverse populations. Through scholarship, the overall goal is to increase our awareness of the importance of diversity and inclusion to improve health outcomes.

Project_Title: Structure and function of deubiquitinase USP8
Project_Description: Deubiquitinase USP8 is a ubiquitin regulatory enzyme, mutations of which are known to cause Cushing's disease, a debilitating endocrinological adenoma. Understanding the structure, function and activity regulation of USP8 is essential to identify therapy for treating Cushing's disease. This project aims to use biochemistry and enzymology techniques to quantify the activity of the enzyme and explore the relationship of intramolecular interaction and its activity.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
The student will read the literature on the structure and function of the enzyme of interest.
The student will use bacteria to express different constructs of the enzyme recombinantly, purify the constructs to a high purity and homogeneity, and use fluorescence-based assay to measure the activity of the different constructs.
The student will prepare reports summarizing the results and present them in the group meetings in the lab.

Project_Title: Latent Aspect Detection
Project_Description: Within the context of review analytics, aspects are the features of products and services at which customers target their opinions and sentiments. Aspect detection helps product owners and service providers to identify shortcomings and prioritize customers' needs, and hence, maintain revenues and mitigate customer churn. Existing methods focus on detecting the surface form of an aspect by training supervised learning methods that fall short when aspects are latent in reviews. In this paper, we propose to extract latent occurrences of aspects.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
1. Integrate existing topic modelling methods to our pipeline
2. Experiments the methods on datasets and report the results

Project_Title: University of Windsor Student Exchange Placement
Project_Description: This project is for Outstanding Scholars students on exchange. Students journal experiences and observations to create reflections using the topics of interpersonal communication and the Capability Approach which is the underpinning framework for the United Nations Human Development Reports and Human Development Index (see hd-ca.org).
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
Review intercultural communication textbook chapters, guides, and academic articles.
Review introductory texts regarding the Human Development and Capability Approach (see hd-ca.org).
Review texts regarding ethnography.

Project_Title: Canadian politics, research and formatting for the 10th edition of a textbook entitled Canadian Democracy
Project_Description: This project involves updating and creating figures and tables for the forthcoming 10th edition of Canadian Democracy, a textbook on Canadian government and politics.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Strong working knowledge of Excel is necessary for this position. The ideal candidate will have some experience in updating and creating tables and figures based on Statistics Canada data, survey data, and other sources of data that I will provide.

Project_Title: Query Refinement
Project_Description: Query refinements such as query expansion and query reduction can greatly improve search effectiveness. In traditional query refinement settings, one or more refinements are chosen to be applied to all queries. While, on average, this approach may improve the search effectiveness of a retrieval system, it is possible that some refinements actually degrade the effectiveness of certain queries. Likewise, some refinements may be highly beneficial to a select few queries, but not the majority. In this project, we investigate inter-language translation to address the problem of how to automatically select the most effective query refinement method on a per-query basis.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Adding query refinement methods based on translations to-from different natural languages

Project_Title: Work-life Balance and navigating virtual and hybrid essential service work
Project_Description: This is a literature review that addresses relevant work-life balance research and relevant research about essential service workers, particularly since the onset of the COVID-19 pandemic
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Meetings with your faculty advisor and a Leddy Librarian to develop and summarize a literature review.

Project_Description: The student will conduct research using state-of-the-art sound analysis software to measure bird behaviour, bird migration, and bird mating strategies. For the 50 hour work term during the Fall semester, the student will use the sound analysis computers in the Mennill Laboratory to scan and annotate recordings collected in the field. The research will focus on recordings of night flight calls of migratory birds through Essex County, and recordings of Savannah Sparrows breeding in Eastern Canada.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: The student will be overseen by Dr. Mennill and his graduate students and research technicians. The student will keep a lab book, briefly summarizing the hours worked and the research progress completed.

Project_Title: Variational calculations for helium in qd-precision
Project_Description: The project involves the development and implementation of computer programs to perform variational calculations using extended (qd) precision arithmetic for the Rydberg states of helium.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Computer programming in FORTRAN, using the David Baily package for qd-pecision (70 decimal digit) arithmetic, and perform production runs to obtain new results for publication.

Project_Title: Local Black History Symposium
Project_Description: In conjunction with the Detroit River Project, the Department of History at the University of Windsor will be hosting a symposium in the winter of 2023. The symposium will celebrate the history of Black experiences in the Windsor-Essex-Detroit region by welcoming distinguished scholars from the area and showcasing student-led public history projects, which are currently being developed in HIST 3610.

Project_Title: SCIE 3800 Service Learning, SCIE 3900 Undergraduate Research Experience, or SCIE 3900 Internship
Project_Description: Students in all programs in the Faculty of Science can apply to have SCIE 3800 Service Learning, SCIE 3900 Undergraduate Research Experience, or SCIE 3990 Internship coursework be their OS placement for one semester.

Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
1) Processing biological samples for stable isotopes (freeze drying, grinding and weighting)
2) Loading and running the isotope ratio mass spectrometer.
3) Assessing data qualty.

Project_Title: Synthetic Organic Chemical Methodology
Project_Description: Synthetic organic chemical methodology - making new chemical

Project_Title: Marketing and Promotion of Odette Research
Project_Description: The Odette School of Business includes many strong impactful faculty researchers.  However, this is not well-communicated on campus, to student body, nor to the Windsor Essex Business community.

It will involve the collection of primary survey and interview data from our graduates, and will examine their perceptions of EDI on campus and in the workplace, and factors affecting their resilience.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Student (s) will work with Dr Francine Schlosser and Phil Balyot to collect and analyze primary data.

Project_Title: Relaunching Technovation Post Covid: Participatory Action Research Approach
Project_Description: Technovation is the largest global entrepreneurship challenge for young girls interested in STEM. Female high school students in teams of 3-5 work together to identify a social challenge, ideate a solution through a mobile application, and present a business plan for their solution to a panel of judges. Each team works closely with their mentor through the guided curriculum. Connecting Windsor-Essex (CWE) collaborates with local school boards to encourage girls to join the 12-week challenge. Over the pandemic, the success of the Technovation challenge has declined, and CWE is looking to relaunch the program with a new strategy to increase the number of team registrations and participants to encourge more young females to enter STEM-related fields and industries.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
This will be a participatory action research project, wherein the research team will be focused on participation and action of the students and teachers affected by the Technovation team, addressing questions and issues significant to the Technovation community and the ecosystem.

- Compare quantitative and qualitative data pre-COVID, during COVID, and post-COVID to represent the number of registrations and active participation of students and teachers.

- Actively participate in strategically determining the outreach strategy for Technovation partners.

- Identify the obstacles and challenges that act as deterrents for young females to enter the STEM industry.

- Propose methods to overcome above-mentioned obstacles and implement such methods through the Technovation program.

- Emphasize collective inquiry and experimentation of the Technovation program and its social history and impact.

Project_Title: SEERa: A Framework for Community Prediction
Project_Description: This is an open-source extensible end-to-end python-based framework to predict the future user communities in a text streaming social network (e.g., Twitter) based on the users’ topics of interest. User community prediction aims at identifying communities in the future based on the users' temporal topics of interest. We model inter-user topical affinities at each time interval via streams of temporal graphs. Our framework benefits from temporal graph embedding methods to learn temporal vector representations for users as users' topics of interests and hence their inter-user topical affinities are changing in time. We predict user communities in future time intervals based on the final locations of users' vectors in the latent space. Our framework employs layered software design that adds modularity, maintainability, ease of extensibility, and stability against customization and ad hoc changes to its components including topic modeling, user modeling, temporal user embedding, user community prediction and evaluation. More importantly, our framework further offers one-stop shop access to future communities to improve recommendation systems and advertising campaigns. Our proposed framework has already been benchmarked on a Twitter dataset and showed improvements compared to the state of the art in underlying applications such as news article recommendation and user prediction.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Adding baseline methods in topic modeling and graph embedding layers of the framework.

Project_Title: Media reporting controversial issues
Project_Description: Often we hear about claims of media bias. How do we assess whether the media provide a balanced portrayal of issues, people, or events? Using content analysis, we can assess media coverage in a systematic and objective manner. The projects will vary from semester and reflect current issues in the news.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Collect newspaper and/or electronic media from Leddy Library. With the guidance of the supervisor, students will develop a coding protocol, engage in intercoder reliability, and assess media coverage of controversial issues. Students will also input data in spreadsheets, and may be asked to generate preliminary data.

Project_Title: A Global Shift in Family Nursing Practice
Project_Description: Written narratives(stories) will be requested from volunteer practice committee and IFNA( International Family Nursing Association) members about their pandemic family nursing practice experiences and how they were impacted. Family nurses from countries throughout the world will be asked to share how the Covid 19 pandemic impacted families and their family nursing practice. IFNA  members are located in almost every country in the world and the network meets monthly in various committees including international  teams of researchers and practitioners who focus on  improving the health of families globally.

The students will be provided with email contacts of those wishing  to share their stories. The stories will be compiled for an EBook supported by the International Family Nursing Association.

Project_Title: Ecological responses to underwater sound
Project_Description: The underwater environment is a very noise place and humans are making it noisier with increased industrialization. This increased noise level can have significant impacts on fish and marine mammals so it is critical to better understand responses of organisms to these noise sources.  We use camera and audio recordings to analyse these effects and need students to help us in this analysis.

Project_Description:

Method

Search 4 different databases (a) IEEE Xplore for a technology perspective; (b) PubMed, for a medical Perspective; (c) Engineering Village, for an engineering perspective; and (d) Web of Science for a cross-disciplinary perspective
