Project_Title: A Study of Queueing System Optimization
Project_Description: A queueing system describes the process in which customers contend with each other for a system’s resources. One way to model such a system is through the use of Markov chain theory and the application of Matrix Analytic Methods (MAM). Through this project, we will explore the application of Markov Decision Processes (MDPs) within these models to optimize dynamic server decision making.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: The student will learn how to model and analyse queueing systems using MAM and explore the use of MDPs to optimize server behaviour. Tasks will include being trained in relevant theory, reviewing relevant literature, conducting numerical analysis in R, and writing a report to summarise findings.

Project_Title: Association between 24-hr blood pressure and sleep quality in healthy collegiate students athletes.
Project_Description: This is project will examine the association between systemic blood pressure when recorded over 24-hr and its association to sleep quality in healthy student athletes. Blood pressure will be examined using an automated blood pressure cuff positioned on the left arm, that is programed to recorded over a 24-hr period. Sleep quality will be assessed using the Pittsburgh Sleep Questionnaire Index (PSQI).
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: To begin constructing an ethics application to submit to the University of Windsor LREB.

Project_Title: Employment Trajectory of Philippine-Educated Nurses in Ontario
Project_Description:
This mixed-methods study will explore Philippine-educated nurses' (PENs) transitioning experience into, and labour market outcomes within employment in Ontario.  This will involve administering a province-wide survey in 2022-2023 using several validated questionnaires measuring microaggressions, which have been designed to capture the intersectional experience of microaggressions by Asian women in North America. The proposed work will be the first research that utilizes these measures of microaggressions within health care professionals, which will yield novel findings in the field of Psychology. Preliminary data from the surveys will inform the development of our interview guide for in-depth semi-structured interviews with PENs who will be randomly drawn from those who responded to our survey and through purposive and snowball sampling and are working as registered nurses, working as registered practical nurses, working in another area of healthcare, and working in a field other than healthcare. We will also interview Filipino-Canadians who completed their undergraduate nursing education at a Canadian postsecondary institution to explore group differences in employment trajectories and experiences.  The study's findings will provide a clear understanding of the labour market outcomes of PENs and their experiences as they journey to integrate into Ontario’s workforce. The findings will also shed light on the factors impacting their employment trajectories, and the impact of gender, class, and race in relation to their employment trajectory and labour market outcomes in Canadian society.

We welcome Outstanding Scholars from the Faculty of Nursing and the Department of Psychology who belong to federally designated groups of women and racialized people/visible minorities.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
Outstanding Scholars (OS) will be providing support to the PI (Dr. Edward Cruz) and Co-A (Dr. Kristoffer Romero) in conducting systematic literature searches and review, setting up the REDCap platform for data management, developing recruitment flyers, scheduling interviews,  collecting data (qualitative and quantitative), and supporting the PI, Co-A and GRAs in analyzing data.  Closer to the end of the project, we will be guiding our Outstanding Scholars in creating an infographic to summarize the key findings of the study.  Outstanding Scholars will also be encouraged to present their work at the annual UWindsor UWill Discover Research Conference. 

Outstanding Scholars will be trained on the use of REDCap through the WE-Spark Health Institute (Kyle Lago, personal communication, January 6, 2022). The Co-A (Dr. Romero) also has experience using REDCap for collection of quantitative health data. Outstanding Scholars will also be encouraged to attend research colloquia and other research-related workshops regularly scheduled within the UWindsor. As needed, training on data analysis software use will be provided through Research Design and Analysis Workshops at UToronto.

Project_Title: American Politics: creating graphics for the 3rd edition of a book
Project_Description: The 3rd edition of Understanding American Politics includes many tables and figures from the previous edition that require updating, and many new tables and figures that need to be created. No political science background is necessary, although students in political science, international relations or history may find this most interesting. Data will be provided by the professor.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Using Excel or other graphics creation software to update existing tables and figures and to input data in order to create new ones.

Project_Title: Development of Advanced SAS Analysis Software
Project_Description: The treatment and analysis of small angle scattering (SAS) data is always a technical hurdle and a barrier for many groups. Through this project, we aim to develop a web based analysis package for researchers to use. The goal is to make SAS accessible to non-experts to increase the applications of the technique.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Student will implement existing SAS models into a web based application. The student will use Python to create both the user interface as well as the background computational machniery.

Project_Title: The physical demands of drum set performance
Project_Description: The Drummer Mechanics & Ergonomics Research (DRUMMER) Lab’s overall research goal is to do for drummers what sport science is doing for athletes: help them to achieve their peak performance while reducing their risk of injuries. Students will be expected to help with several projects investigating the physical demands of drum set performance.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
Students will gain experience with a combination of quantitative (e.g., heart rate monitoring, electromyography, three-dimensional motion capture, accelerometry, surveys, etc.) and qualitative (e.g., semi structured interviews) research methods.

Students will assist the faculty supervisor and graduate students with a variety of research related tasks, including (but not limited to): review of literature, data collection, data entry, data cleaning and analysis, and knowledge translation (e.g., reports, posters, presentations, manuscripts, articles in popular media, social media posts/graphics, etc.).

Project_Title: Assessing Environmental Temperature Impacts on Human Health
Project_Description:
As we all know, Windsor is the warmest city in Canada, and there are projections that close to 2 months of the year will be above 30oC in the coming decades.  An understanding of the impact temperature change has on our populace will be important for health and city planning.
 
The specific questions we are trying to answer are:
 
•	Are there sex differences in the incidence of heat stroke/heat exhaustion?
•	How is the incidence of heat stroke/heat exhaustion influenced by environmental temperature (i.e. average temp vs peak temp vs days of elevated heat)?
 
As such, we’d be looking to see if we could get access to any or all of the following data:
•	Number of patients who attended the emergency department and were diagnosed with heat stroke/heat exhaustion
•	Patient demographics (i.e. age, sex, race)  
•	If possible, core temperature at intake
•	If possible, outcomes (i.e. discharge vs death)
•	Environmental conditions in the periods that aligned with patient data from standard sources.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
Aid in REB completion and submission.
Access, collect, and organize online and in-person medical data.
Read and make notes from medical charts.
Liaison with hospital staff. 
Run statistical analyses on data.
Aid in knowledge translation.

Project_Title: Creating a social, psychological, pedagogical, and professional module
Project_Description: The project intends to create a module focused on social, psychological, pedagogical, and professional learning as an online toolkit to offer emotion regulation, stress management, resilience, and work-life balance strategies for students to feel greater autonomy in their lives in mitigating pandemic-related anxiety.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Students hired will help to build resources to create the modules, and write the manuscript.

Project_Title: Building industry-inspired medical biotechnology labs
Project_Description: The project intends to develop industry-inspired laboratory biotechniques & related pedagogical resources. The skill-sets honed by these resources are highly desired by prospective employers in academia & the medical BioTech industry, & will get the students better prepared for undertaking scientific investigations.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: The student will help in developing biotechniques & related teaching and pedagogical resources, in troubleshooting, and in manuscript writing. Some of the tasks may be completed online.

Project_Title: Innate anti-cancer immune responses
Project_Description: The main focus of the lab is to understand the mechanism of cancer immune recognition and immune evasion. The functions of immune cells are regulated by cell surface receptors. Using genetically modified mouse models lacking one or more of these receptors, we study their function and their impact on anti-cancer immune responses. These studies will reveal the mechanisms of immune recognition by innate immune cells, and how cancer cells can evade immune detection.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Students are required to complete online biosafety courses, help in developing and writing standard operating procedures and protocols. These activities are aimed at improving trainee’s writing skills and understanding lab procedures and protocols. Students will then be paired with graduate students and may learn techniques in tissue culture, DNA and RNA preparation, PCR amplification, agarose gel electrophoresis, immune cell preparation and analysis. Students must attend and present data at the lab meetings, if their course schedule allows.

Project_Title: Stress Reduction Peer Support & Presence in the Context of Counseling Online Verses In-person
Project_Description:
This position covers two projects:
1) Drs. Connie Kvarfordt (University of Windsor) and Kimberly Calderwood (Trent University) have completed a Stress Reduction Peer Support project where 12 third-year social work students provided peer support to Trent students over a 12-week period. We are now looking for support in conducting a literature review about models of peer support in higher education.
2) The same professors completed a different study about teaching counselling skills online. We are looking for support in conducting a literature review about counseling and the difference between being present online verses being present while in person.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: This position requires the applicant to work closely on the research projects mentioned above. This will include: • Developing a systematic way to search in scholarly databases for articles related to the research topics above. • Reading conceptual and empirical scientific literature and identifying aspects relevant for two manuscripts in progress. • Working on the development of a citation database in correct APA form. • Participating in brainstorming and debriefing sessions in team meetings with the two faculty. • Conducting general research-related work as needed.

Project_Title: Statistical learning algorithms and applications.
Project_Description:
The main objective of this projects is to learn and apply some basic statistical learning methods to build predictive and classification models or perform exploratory analysis.  Methods include the concepts of Linear regression, Classifications, Resampling 
Linear Model Selection and Regularization, Non-linear Models, Tree-Based Methods, Support Vector Machines and Unsupervised Learning.  COVID19 data set will analyzed using some of the motioned methods.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: The students will make a literature review surrounding the topic of Statistical learning methods and read some recent references about applications of statistical learning methods for Covid-19 datasets. The student will implement or use some existing R packages to analyze new datasets.

Project_Title: The Self-Reference Effect and Associated Cognitive Abilities in 4- to 6- Year-Old Children
Project_Description: There are two aims of the proposed study. The first aim is to evaluate the presence and magnitude of the self-reference effect (SRE) among 4- to 6-year-olds across encoding tasks varying from physically active, mentally active, and passive. The second goal of the proposed study is to examine the cognitive abilities associated with the SRE among 4- to 6-year-olds. The cognitive abilities to be assessed will include a measure of receptive language skills, theory of mind, short-term auditory memory, expressive language skills, visual short-term memory, processing speed, cognitive flexibility, attention, and autobiographical memory.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
1.	Contact and schedule participant meetings with parents
2.	Learn and practice using standardized psychological test batteries 
3.	Complete data collection through the administration of psychological tests in 4- to 6-year-old children. 
4.	Score data and perform data entry
5.	Attend bi-weekly lab meetings

Project_Title: AI and IoT Enabled Automated Driving Behavior Detection
Project_Description: Driving behavior recognition can support many promising applications, such as car insurance, driving safety, and anti-car theft. This project aims to develop an automated driving behavior detection system by using Internet of things (IoT) devices and Artificial intelligence (AI). The IoT devices will be deployed in the car to collect the driving data and send the data to the cloud, where AI algorithms will be employed to analyze data to recognize the driving behaviors.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
Develop the IoT systems to collect data and send data to servers.
Develop AI algorithm to detect different driving behavior with high accuracy.

Project_Title: International students in distress: exploring the impact of COVID-19
Project_Description: The study is meant to explore in greater depth the post-pandemic psychological distress international students experience. The rationale is to bring awareness to the potential emotional distress, mental health problems, academic pressure, occupational uncertainty, and alienation experienced by the international students. Additionally, to accommodate these students better to provide socio-emotional, psychological, academic support, and inclusive educational opportunities primarily by university instructors and support services.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Student researchers will get valuable strategic, research training on scholarly teaching & learning, literature searches, exploratory mix-method study design, use of scales/surveys, data analysis, manuscript writing, conference/workshop presentations, networking, identifying well-being risk factors, and measures.

Project_Title: Intestinal Healing and Circadian Rhythms
Project_Description: This project involves testing whether the intestine of mice heals according to a 24-hour day and night cycle.  Different mouse mutant and transgenic reporter strains will be tested to discover how the circadian clock regulates healing.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Crossing and handling of research mice. Molecular/cellular analysis of mouse tissue samples. Data analysis and statistics. Preparation of lab reports and presentations to convey results of research.

Project_Title: Agrigultural Biodiversity & Multivariate Statistics
Project_Description: The Windsor Essex region is the one of the most biodiverse regions in Canada and yet is almost entirely impacted by human development and agricultural intensification. To better inform conservation and restoration, a robust baseline dataset is required. Agricultural drainage systems - waterways, roadside drains and wetlands - are critical biodiversity hotspots and yet there is little empirical data for them. This OS will contribute to compiling survey data and learning a suite of multivariate statistics to explore relationships between biodiversity and human impacts, with the potential to launch into independent research projects locally.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
- Work with datasets including environmental and biodiversity data
- Learn multivariate statistics using R
- Work with a team
- Some fieldwork may be required

Project_Title: Centering Indigenous Ways of Knowing Nature through Storytelling
Project_Description:
Indigenous perspectives on Nature and Place are not often recognised or valued in Science. To address this, the Healthy Headwaters lab has committed considerable resources and support to centre Indigenous ways of knowing in research related to ecosystem restoration. We do this in recognition of the reciprocal relationship between water and Indigenous peoples, and as part of our commitment to embed reconcilitation with Indigenous Peoples in all we do. One way to do this is through relationship-building and storytelling as a form of knowledge
sharing. Students in science are also multi-dimensional in their experiences, talents and interests that encompass art, humanities and Indigenous ways of knowing. We seek to support Outstanding Scholars called to bridging ways of knowing nature.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
The student will assist researchers in the Healthy Headwaters Lab by developing creative
works that extend from primary research being conducted. This will create connection,
experiences and share knowledge related to work of the Ode’imin (Indigenous Knowledge
Circle).

Project_Title: Systematic review of freshwater mussel data
Project_Description: Freshwater mussels are keystone species in stream and river ecosystems. Understanding their role in the food web is essential for informing their conservation and restoration. This project focuses on extracting data from various knowledge sources to assess the effectiveness of freshwater restoration and management.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Work with a research team to extract information from various sources - papers, reports, spreadsheets - and generate summary statistics. Meet weekly to bi-weekly with an interdisciplinary team to analyze findings. There may be some opportunities for fieldwork to sample freshwater mussel and invertebrate communities in local streams and rivers.

Project_Title: Biodiversity Assessment & Restoration of Human-Impacted Waterways
Project_Description:
Human-impacted waterways are relied upon to provide multiple services that benefit
human health and well-being from flood mitigation, nutrient and pollution removal, to
provision of drinking water, culture and recreation. Additionally, they are the remnant
habitats for many plant and animal communities here in the Great Lakes. The
assemblages that remain are highly resilient and are indicators of ecosystem health.
The Healthy Headwaters Lab is engaged in assessing the health of human-impacted
waterways which includes benthic macroinvertebrate communities (e.g., insects, worms,
snails), and the application of this knowledge to restoration efforts across the region.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
The Oustanding Scholar will be trained to identify benthic invertebrates collected from a
network of field sites from the region. They will process samples collected from the field,
learn protocols for identifying benthic invertebrates, enter data, statistically analyze
datasets, and engage in scientific writing and communication.

Project_Title: Microbial Influences on Aquatic Ecosystems
Project_Description: Government decision support or risk models  includes sediment/water quality characteristics and microbial community (NextGen) information to further translate microbial transcriptome measurements into risk categories of active or pending nutrient flux culminating in an internal load 'vulnerability score' for monitored locations. To facilitate treatment options, we are working with our industry partners specializing in Environmental Solution Technologies to determine the proximate mechanisms of their mitigation technologies by characterizing the microbial function/technology interactions.   This is being addressed by improving both characterization and functional interpretation of sediment microbial communities in selected watersheds. to date we have accumulated  comprehensive data sets which require further assessment.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Student will work on metagenomic data sets related to molecular characterization of microbial communities collected from several NSERC SPG sponsored  field locations.

Project_Title: Carbon Characterization of Soil and Water in Human-Impacted Landscapes
Project_Description:
Carbon is an essential nutrient in all ecosystems and a key driver of human-driven
climate change. Our lab conducts research to characterize carbon using various
analytical tools in the lab, from soil and water samples across human-impact gradients
(urbanized, agriculturally-intensified) as part of a larger effort to explore and advance
approaches for ecosystem restoration. Working in partnership with a diverse research
team that includes Settler and Indigenous communities and perspectives, leading edge
science will be promoted and achieved through diverse and inclusive training on
analytical equipment housed at the Great Lakes Institute for Environmental Research
(GLIER) and across a network of local field sites across Essex County, Sydenham River
and Bkejwanong Territory.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
The Oustanding Scholar will be trained to operate analytical equipment used to
measure inorganic and organic nutrients found in soil and water samples. They will
process samples collected from the field, do limited field work, enter data, statistically
analyze datasets, and engage in scientific writing and communication

Project_Title: Coral Reef Fish Detection using eDNA
Project_Description: Coral reef fish communities are among the most diverse and complex on earth.  However, documenting that biodiversity, much less monitoring it, is daunting.  Environmental DNA (“eDNA”) is DNA extracted from an environmental sample (often water). eDNA can be used with PCR and massive parallel (Next Gen) sequencing (NGS) to identify the fish species present near where the water samples were taken.  This project will use control eDNA from coral reef aquariums (where the species are known) and from a protected coral reef on the south edge of the Great Barrier Reef to determine the fish species assemblages.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: The student will develop universal coral reef fish species primers, extract eDNA from filtrates of water samples and amplify the marker region for the fish species present in the water samples.  We will use NGS to determine the presence of all possible coral reef fish species.

Project_Title: SARS-CoV-2 Wastewater Campus Surveillance
Project_Description: lties of Science and Engineering are part of a first-of-its-kind province-wide SARS-CoV-2 wastewater surveillance project.  The project will enhance critical province-wide coordination and viral surveillance and support provincial and national efforts to understand how the virus that causes COVID-19, SARS-CoV-2, is changing over time through emerging variants, including variants of concern. The virus responsible for COVID-19 is shed in feces. Collecting a sample from the wastewater stream effectively provides a "community-wide swab" of the contributing population can provide critical information about COVID-19 community spread sooner than individual test results or reports of illness. As part of the project, the technique is currently being used to monitor a student residence on campus. During the Fall 2021 and Winter 2022 terms, additional locations on campus will be identified and added to the surveillance program.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: The outstanding scholars working on the project will aid the project team in placing/ collecting/ processing samples or related data collection. The samples will be collected from wastewater discharge pipes directly or through sewer manholes.

Project_Title: Bacterial Community Chatacterization
Project_Description: Great Lakes recreational water use at designated beaches and other public areas is common, especially during the summer months. This NSERC-funded project seeks to use modern molecular genetic methods to determine the bacterial composition within the water and sediments at recreation beaches around Essex County. This novel approach will allow faster and more accurate assessment of recreational water safety.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: The student will be trained in aquatic microbrial sampling and sample preparation. DNA extraction by automated liquid handling will produce DNA for molecular genetic bacterial characterization. The student wil assist with PCR amplification of the 16S rDNA gene from the environmental water sample. Next Generation sequencing libraries will be constructed for each sample, purified and sequenced on the U Windsor ION Torrent Next Generation sequencer. The student will assist in data organization and analysis.

Project_Title: Genetic Analysis of Microbiome Variation
Project_Description: The objective of the project is to develop and apply microsatellite DNA markers to two native cyprinid fish species known to exhibit highly variable microbiome diversity. The microsatellite markers will allow us to quantify genetic diversity among the fish to assess the role of genetic relatedness on microbiome variation.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
•	Develop appropriate primer microsatellite markers
•	Optimize the primer markers for us in a massively parallel (NextGen) sequencer-based assay
•	Conduct literature searches
•	Prepare the PCR and NextGen sequencing library

Project_Title: DNA Methylation: Genome Surveys
Project_Description:
DNA methylation was traditionally identified as a form of DNA damage caused by environmental stress. Recent work has shown that methylation may be an adaptive form of epigenetic gene expression control. This NSERC-funded project will characterise DNA methylation in salmon to determine the role of methylation in the evolution of location adaptation.

The student will be trained in DNA extraction by automated liquid handling. The students will assist with methylation assay development and PCR amplification of the selected candidate genes. The students will be trained in Net Generation sequencing library construction and the library will be sequenced on the UWindsor ION Torrent Next Generation sequencer.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: The students will assist in data organisation and analysis.

Project_Title: Genetic Algal Viability
Project_Description: The potential for harmful algea species to invade the Great Lakes via ballast water discharge is high, despite ballast water treatment option because even very small number of viable cells can colonise new habitats. This industry and NSERC-funded project will use modern molecular genetic methods to determine the viability of algal cells after UV treatment. This project will determine the level and intensity of UV exposure needed to damage the genome of the algae sufficiently such that they cannot replicate.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: The student will be trained in aquatic algal culturing and sampling (filtering). DNA extraction by automated liquid handling will produce DNA for molecular genetic characterization, while the students will be trained in lab-based RNA extraction. The students will assist with PCR amplification of the 18S rDNA gene from the algae as well as the Next Generation sequencing libraries construction for all samples. The library will be sequences on the UWindsor ION Torrent Next Generation sequencer. The students will assist in data organization and analysis.

Project_Title: Population Genetics of Salmon Inbreeding
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Extract DNA from existing salmon tissue samples, run PCR, and NextGen sequence to determine levels of DNA methylation in stressed fish.

Project_Title: Sleep Problems and ADHD Symptoms in School-Aged Children
Project_Description: The primary objective of the proposed project is to determine correlations between activity, attention, and sleep in school-aged children.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
Contact and schedule participant meetings with parents 
Learn and practice using standardized psychological test batteries  
Complete data collection through the administration of psychological tests in school-aged children 
Score data and perform data entry 
Attend bi-weekly lab meetings

Project_Title: Thermal Modelling and Cooling Solutions for Induction Motors
Project_Description: The proposed research aims to improve the torque output, efficiency, and lifespan of induction motors for electric vehicle (EV) application. EVs have the potential to significantly reduce the amount of greenhouse gases emitted to the environment by replacing internal combustion engine vehicles. However, EV technology must continue to advance and become more affordable so that EV popularity can continue to grow. One major hurdle that currently inhibits the further advancement of EV performance and efficiency is the lack of an effective cooling solution that specifically targets the most thermally sensitive component within the motor, the stator winding insulation.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: In this work, Computational fluid dynamics (CFD) simulations will be used to optimize the coolant flow regime through the minichannels and reduce peak temperatures when the motor is operated at full load. A Nusselt number correlation will be developed to estimate the convection heat transfer coefficient associated with the proposed cooling method. Once optimized, the cooling method assembly process and performance will be validated experimentally.

Project_Title: State of Business Communications in North America and Gap Analysis: Business Communications & Current Industry Needs
Project_Description:
This two-semester research project will aim to collect recent and relevant data on business
communications, including mediums, uses and trends in North America (N.A.), as well as, compare
these findings with what post-secondary Business Communications courses are currently offering.
Such a comparison will allow for a final “gap analysis” between the needs and desires of business
employers to what post-secondary institutions are offering in preparing graduates for successful
procurement of employment, and overall career success. Finally, an additional analysis will be
conducted comparing our current Odette business communications curriculum offerings with those of
N.A. post-secondary programs and our employer needs and desires research results.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
Perform secondary research in gathering information across disciplines from
accounting and finance, to marketing and communications, specifically focusing on
current business communication needs, mediums and uses, and trends.
o Compile and organize business communication curriculum specifics for comparision
across post-secondary institutions in N.A., as well as, Canadian verses American
programs.
o Examine and report Odette curriculum and course offerings for Business
Communications courses to that of other institutions in N.A., as well as, to the current
and trending employer needs and desires discovered.

Project_Title: Quantifying Intangible Investment and Social Enterprise Impact
Project_Description:
Project Description: The research question is: Can a SROI data capture and modelling methodology be created for the local Enactus organizations that is both easy to use (employs simplicity) and accurate (delivers credibility)?
Goal 1: To calculate a range of SROI of Enactus projects by extrapolating from existing SROI reports of similar organizations/projects. The following outlines the proposed process:
1.) Youthrive SROI measures and calibration – calculate SROI of past eight years projected into the future extrapolated from BCG report on JA. Because this organization and its programs is so similar to Youthrive, it is felt that only one SROI report is needed. Process is:
a. Gather data from Youthrive data base and Enactus Canada records (challenge reports) from past eight years to establish investment rate (key figures are: 1. amount of funds invested, 2. number of participants, 3. number of clients) over past eight years.
b. Create survey to independently establish degree of fit between Youthrive and JA programs. Complete approvals from REB.
c. Distribute survey to BDC employees – require at least 50 sample size. Employees should be of certain capacity that understands entrepreneurial development. Offer $10 Starbucks gift card in exchange for survey completion. Requires permission from BDC. Survey valence design must include not only fit but degree of fit.
d. Compile surveys – establish degree of fit. Calculate investment adjusted for inflation over past eight years. Based on degree of fit, establish SROI ratio. Decide upon range (eg: +/- 10%) and calculate final range of SROI over past eight years.
Goal 2: To calculate a more fixed present and future SROI of Enactus projects through detailed past participant interviews and corroborating with existing economic and social data. 
2.) Develop SROI measuring tool – calculate previous and future SROI using past participant interviews.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
Research Tasks and Activities:
1.	Coordinate with BDC for survey distribution.
2.	Coordinate collection of part 1 surveys and perform high-level assessment/compilation.
3.	Write background on Enactus Youthrive for report.
4.	Coordinate advertising for past Youthrive participant surveys.
5.	Coordinate collection of Part 2 surveys and perform high-level assessment/compilation.

Project_Title: Assessing social enterprise projects
Project_Description: Student and faculty will be looking into the interworkings of a social enterprise and how these operate from a number of perspectives. The first will be a research paper identifying ways and means of quantifying the intangible and Goodwill investment of social enterprise project sponsorship. The second is a research paper identifying ways and means of systematically quantifying and qualifying the social good and impact of social enterprise projects. The third potential is assessing the design and development of an on-campus club that creates a “Skunkwork” approach to resourcing, developing, operationalizing, measuring, and presenting an innovative social enterprise solution.

Project_Title: Artificial Intelligence Impact
Project_Description: Advances in technology may displace certain types of work, but historically advances in technology have been a creator of new jobs (Smith & Anderson, 2014). The fear of machines taking over jobs thereby making humans redundant is not new. From the time of the Industrial Revolution machines have been replacing blue-collar jobs (Chelliah, 2017).  "Technology will free us from day-to-day drudgery, and allow us to define our relationship with "work" in a more positive and socially beneficial way" (Smith & Anderson, 2014). Work may become less repetitive and may begin to require high skill sets. This study will examine the impact of AI on jobs in Canada.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Primarily through literature review. Student is expected to organize and summarize the literature to date.

Project_Title: Impact of Ethnic Entrepreneurship in Canada
Project_Description:
Large numbers of various ethnic groups are engaged in entrepreneurship. They tend to focus and congregate around certain type of businesses. Research questions to consider: Why do some ethnic groups tend to concentrate in certain type of businesses? What kind of information sharing is occurring among the ethnic groups? Have they been successful? How have they impacted the industry they are operating in? What types of modifications were made to the businesses?
Above questions are explore through literature review and ultimately development of surveys that could measure and provide answers to the above enquiry.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: 1)	Literature Review; 2) Research ethics; 3) Theory development; 4) Research design; 5) Data collection; 6) Data analysis ; 7) Report writing; 8) Research presentation/pubs

Project_Title: Exploring the Involvement of Institutions in Matching Immigrant Newcomers and Closing Family Businesses in Windsor
Project_Description:
Dr. Schlosser is working with colleagues from Brock and UNIFESP (Brazil) on the following project: 
Exploring the Involvement of Institutions in Matching Immigrant Newcomers and Closing Family Businesses in Windsor 

Small-medium businesses are an important feature of several immigrant receiving countries, including Canada. In Canada, there is a growing trend of family business implosion. For instance, a recent CFIB (2017) report suggests that one in two family businesses will fold within the next five years and 77% will fold within the next decade. Alarmingly, the main reason cited for small business closures is retirement. Studies have also found that many family businesses in Canada do not have a succession plan (e.g., Schlosser, 2015). It may take 10 to 20 years to develop a successor in a family firm (Longenecker & Schoen, 1978; Handler 1994). Without a family successor, owners are often unable to find a commercial buyer, and consequently unable to harvest the value they have developed in their businesses. 

Immigrants may provide a solution to this problem. Newcomers to Canada bring new knowledge, skills, and risk propensity to support a stronger more global expansion of scalable businesses, including those that were previously risk-averse, slow growing, and lifestyle businesses. In particular, foreign-born entrepreneurs are more likely to start and develop transnational businesses than domestically born (Zolin & Schlosser, 2013). Given an increasing supply of marketable small businesses and an increasing incoming supply of immigrant newcomers, we wonder how a matching program might be developed to help business owners leverage available partnerships with immigrants.  We also wonder how a staged succession program might provide an opportunity to engage entrepreneurial international students – first as employees chosen for their future potential as successor, and then transitioning to ownership upon permanent residency. 

We will conduct cross-sectional anonymous quantitative surveys of business owners, immigrants, and international students, we will test hypotheses related to key resilience-related variables identified in the literature review and develop entrepreneurial profiles of small businesses and immigrants. Survey respondents will also be asked if they would like to participate in a follow-up interview.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Students will work with a multi institution research team to monitor online data collection,  compile  survey data, assist with interviews/focus groups and transcribe interview/focus group data through Fall 2020 and Winter 2021.

Project_Title: Foundations of Industry Emergence: A Study of Canada’s Cannabis Industry
Project_Description:
The aim of this effort is to develop a contemporaneous understanding of how the cannabis sector emerged in Canada, spanning first experiments, the development of a nationally regulated medicinal program, and through today’s shift to legal recreational consumption. This project explores and documents the process of transformation from prohibition to commercialization in the founding of Canada’s nationals cannabis ecosystem, taking a wholistic perspective examining factors spanning legal, regulatory, cultural, social, organizational, and financial domains. 

The project will not require primary source data collection on the part of the OS Candidate, relying instead on secondary archival and media documentation available from public sources.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
The initial (one semester) engagement will task Giuseppe Corio with the development of an industry timeline, documenting all aspects of the ecosystem from first inception to the present day. This will require a substantial investment of time and effort of not less than one semester.

Subsequent engagement is possible, involving the development of industry and firm case studies and the dissemination of these studies.

Project_Title: Project Remediation: Better Business Performance through Optimal Technology Use
Project_Description: This applied research project involves case study research to explore how organizations are using management information systems, business analytics, and manufacturing technology. The objective of the research is to understand and make recommendations concerning how technology use can be improved to maximize the value that businesses obtain from their technology investments.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
Literature review:  
Determine data collection needs and develop research protocol
Field work: Conduct interviews with key stakeholders at study organizations
Assemble and analyze interview data
Assist in writing practitioner (industry) and academic reports that summarize research insights

Project_Title: Analysis of team structure and other factors as determinants of project innovation
Project_Description: This research will attempt to determine the extent to which team structure and composition – influenced by recruitment and hiring decisions – impact the project innovation process, and thus the overall impact and success of organizations. Research methodology will comprise collecting and analyzing data regarding Enactus teams – both Canadian and international. Enactus is a non-profit global organization with student-managed chapters run in educational institutions across the world. These local chapters run independent projects which address economic, environmental, and social issues through the application of entrepreneurial business models. The University of Windsor’s chapter has had some success domestically, but has struggled with innovation in the project development process. By analyzing the variables which affect other Enactus teams, we hope to discover determinants of both success and failure, which can be applied both to our Enactus team and other innovation-reliant organizations.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Research will comprise an evaluation of existing research, as well as the collection of primary data through interviews and surveys.

Project_Title: Advances in Finance Using Blockchain Technology
Project_Description:
Research Blockchain innovations in finance applications.
Understand the value proposition of decentralized finance.
Learn the benefits and constraints of Blockchain in specific use cases.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Utilize research tools to gather information to be used to create papers addressing the core ideas in the project description.

Project_Title: Data-Driven Policy Making
Project_Description: This project involves the collection and analysis of publicly available data sources as a basis for informing public policy decisions. As part of this initiative students will need to review the academic literature and popular press to inform their efforts and to prepare a comprehensive report that outlines what is currently known and discusses how government decisions can be improved through better use of existing data sources.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
Perform literature review to inform data collection 
Collect data to evaluate public policy
Prepare report that presents the results of the literature review and data analysis

Project_Title: Technology Obsolescence and Organizational Performance
Project_Description: The project is an archival study that draws on organizational performance data from public sources such as corporate annual reports and other online resources including Compustat and Bloomberg. This data will be combined with an existing dataset to obtain longitudinal insights into the organizational performance implications of technology obsolescence.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
Perform literature review to determine data collection needs
Collect organizational performance data
Assemble a dataset of organizational performance data

Project_Title: Composite Index Creation using AHP: Efficiency Optimization of the Health Care Industry
Project_Description: This project is focused on the creation of a composite index to measure the health care industry’s efficiency simplification of both internal and external decisions made for specific centres. It will aid in defining the optimal amount of hospitals that will fulfill the health care needs of designated geographic areas. The Analytical Hierarchy Process is the method to be incorporated in the creation of the composite index.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: The student will conduct literature review, theory development, research design, data analysis, report writing, research presentation, and possibly publication.

Project_Title: Cross-Border Supply Chain Management
Project_Description: This project is motivated by Windsor’s unique location that hosts the busiest Canada-US border. The security and compliance requirement at the border, lengthy inspection, and unpredictable delays bring new challenges to the businesses of the both sides of the border. The objective of the project is to understand how businesses plan, source, make and deliver products in this context. The project will bring an ample opportunity to an outstanding scholar to learn and apply various tools and techniques of the management science area as the student progresses through the program from 2nd , 3rd , and 4th year. The project will develop some new insights that the businesses can benefit from.

Project_Title: Case Research on Marketing Strategy
Project_Description:
Students will do research (relying primarily on news media) on a company and the marketing issues that this company faces. Current case ideas (i.e. companies and topics of interest) include (but are not limited to) the following: 
•	Gilette and the backlash the company received for airing a commercial with the tag line ‘the best a man can be’
•	Apple and it’s recent decision to launch a credit card
•	Facebook and their recent request for government regulation of content on social media
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Students will summarize their research and may also assist in writing cases on these companies and their strategic marketing decisions.

Project_Title: State of Business Communications in North America / Gap Analysis:  Business Communications & Current Industry Needs
Project_Description: This two-semester research project will aim to collect recent and relevant data on business communications, including mediums, uses and trends in North America (N.A.), as well as, compare these findings with what post-secondary Business Communications courses are currently offering.  Such a comparison will allow for a final “gap analysis” between the needs and desires of business employers to what post-secondary institutions are offering in preparing graduates for successful procurement of employment, and overall career success.  Finally, an additional analysis will be conducted comparing our current Odette business communications curriculum offerings with those of N.A. post-secondary programs and our employer needs and desires research results.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
o	Perform secondary research in gathering information across disciplines from accounting and finance, to marketing and communications, specifically focusing on current business communication needs, mediums and uses, and trends.
o	Compile and organize business communication curriculum specifics for comparision across post-secondary institutions in N.A., as well as, Canadian verses American programs.
o	Examine and report Odette curriculum and course offerings for Business Communications courses to that of other institutions in N.A., as well as, to the current and trending employer needs and desires discovered.

Project_Title: Developing a Press and Media Centred Marketing Course Curriculum
Project_Description: This project involves developing a framework and potential long-term road map for a marketing course focused on giving students real-world experience. The course would be concerned with taking the research findings of professors at Odette, having students write press releases based around the research, and ultimately sending these releases to relevant public news sources for potential publication/circulation. Ideally, students who take the course would gain real-world experience/publications to add to their portfolios and Odette (and potentially one day all UWindsor profs) would gain more research exposure. The objective of this research is to identify and analyze similar courses at institutions across North America in an effort to properly design the learning goals and structure of this course at Odette.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
- perform environmental analysis of current courses at similar institutions
- identify and create key learning objectives for the potential course
- create a potential framework and long-term road map (10 years+) for how to run and grow the course
- develop a course syllabus 
- develop marketing materials for the course

Project_Title: The Interaction Between Corporate Governance and Auditing
Project_Description: I am lauching a long-term research program to imvestigate the interaction betweem corporate governance and auditing.  The current project is to examine whether audit committee governance affects auditor reporting.  Specfically, I will explore how audit committee characteristics like expertise, gender, board member tenure, other board seats, etc. are related to the issuance of modified audit opinions, which include (1) qualified opinions, (2) adverse opinions, (3) disclaimed opinions, and (4) unqualified opinions with explanatory language.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: The student’s work is to manually collect auditing and corporate governance data from listed companies’ reports via the internet.

Project_Title: Revenue Recognition- Understanding the New Standard
Project_Description:
IFRS 15 is the new revenue recognition standard required to be used by publicly accountable enterprises under international financial reporting standards.
IAS 18 is the previous recognition standards required to be used by publicly accountable enterprises under international financial reporting standards.
The goal of this research project is to determine and report on the similarities and differences in the revenue recognition criteria used in each of IFRS 15 and IAS 18.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
The researcher will be required to review and understand the revenue recognition criteria used in IFRS 15 and IAS 18.
The researcher will document and report on the similarities and differences in the revenue recognition criteria in IFRS 15 and IAS 18.

Project_Title: MECHANICAL CHARACTERIZATION OF POROUS MATERIALS
Project_Description: This project offers the student the opportunity to take part in the fundamental research aiming at enhancing the understanding of relationships between the meso-scale architecture of porous materials and their effective properties.

Project_Title: Investigation of Hydrogen Production Methods in Windsor-Essex Area
Project_Description: Hydrogen is the most common element in the universe and a potential fuel of the future, but it does not occur by itself hence, it must be split from water and other compounds. Most hydrogen production uses methods that are not environmentally sustainable because they use non-renewable resources, or they result in substantial emissions of GHGs or pollutants – or both. However, the hydrogen atom is bound to a huge array of other materials and finding efficient and sustainable ways to break these bonds is the subject of a good deal of research globally. A wide range of options for hydrogen production exists or are being investigated using electrical, thermal, nuclear, solar, biotechnology and other methods using input material that range from petroleum or other hydrocarbons to ocean resources to waste streams from industrial or food production and even raw sewage effluent.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: In this work, a summary of hydrogen production methods are investigated. All the methods are analyzed in terms of their advantages, disadvantages and environmental sustainability. The hydrogen production methods presented are classified into Reforming Methods, Electrolysis - Making Hydrogen from Water, and Experimental Hydrogen Production.

Project_Title: Heat Recovery Test Setup for Performance Improvement of Sustainable Energy System
Project_Description: Heat recovery from molten CuCl droplets will be examined. The team will use exergy analysis to predict more efficient configurations of equipment and the system layout for the process of heat recovery. With this the team is to build a fixture capable of doing all of the former and recording test results.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Work closely with MASc students to setup an experimental testing fixture. Focus will be on the sourcing of the parts required as well as the design and setup of the fixture.

Project_Title: Unlocking the Potential of Graphene: Towards a Predictive Model
Project_Description: Graphene is a nanomaterial that has exceptional electrical, thermal, mechanical, and optical properties. It is a carbon-based material that is similar to graphite, but only one atom thick (0.345nm, or 1000 times thinner than a human hair). It has the potential to radically improve performance of renewable energy systems, allowing for mitigation of negative climate change and ambient air pollution effects. Graphene has applications in many other areas of nanotechnology as well; however, its widespread adoption is limited due to a lack of high-yield scalable synthesis methods. One potential method is microwave-plasma assisted reactors (MPARs), although the method is not well-understood. The Scholar will adapted a detailed numerical code for modeling diffusion flames (CoFlame) to microwave-plasma assistant reactors to provide insights into this synthesis technique.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: 1) Initial feasibility study of using CoFlame for MPARs, 2) Modification of CoFlame for MPARs, 3) Produce initial simulation results

Project_Title: Detailed Numerical Modeling of Carbon Nanoparticle Formation
Project_Description: Carbon nanoparticles, also known as black carbon, or soot, are a collection of small, nanometer-size, particles that are mostly carbon that are generated during combustion processes. These particles are cancerous and the second largest contributor to global climate change. The fundamental mechanisms of the complex soot formation process are still not well-understood, thus detailed numerical models are used to test mechanism hypothesis. One such models CoFlame, has been used extensively in research literature to model soot formation. The CoFlame code is a computational fluid dynamics (CFD) code that includes chemical kinetics, particle/aerosol dynamics, radiation, and thermodynamic models. The Scholar involved with this project will use this code to perform an investigation of the effect of different model parameters on soot formation. The Scholar will have the support of Dr. Nick Eaves and a MASc student to assist in performing this project.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: 1) Perform simulations of flames with CoFlame code, 2) Analyze results of simulations to understand the effects of model parameters, 3) Write report on utilizing the CoFlame code

Project_Title: Modeling Black Carbon Transformation to Reduce Its Impact on Climate Change
Project_Description: Black carbon nanoparticles are the second largest contributor to climate change and major contributor to particulate matter pollution. Such particles are emitted from combustion processes that are used in over 90% of the world’s power generation and transportation systems. It is imperative for the health of our planet and species that these negative effects from black carbon nanoparticle emissions be mitigated. In order to accomplish this, there must be models that can describe the fate of black carbon when it enters the atmosphere. Once emitted black carbon undergoes many changes to its composition and shape which dramatically influence its climate change effects. The Scholar will test the feasibility of using an advanced black carbon formation model developed for internal combustion engines to model the effect of atmospheric processes on black carbon.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: 1) Initial feasibility study of using EPEM for black carbon modeling, 2) Modification of EPEM for black carbon, 3) Produce initial simulation results

Project_Title: Detailed Numerical Modeling of Soot Formation in High Pressure Flames
Project_Description: Soot is a collection of small, nanometer-size, particles that are mostly carbon that are generated during combustion processes. These soot particles are cancerous and the second largest contributor to global climate change. The fundamental mechanisms of the complex soot formation process are still not well-understood, thus detailed numerical models are used to test mechanism hypothesis. One such models CoFlame, has been used extensively in research literature to model soot formation. The CoFlame code is a computational fluid dynamics (CFD) code that includes chemical kinetics, particle/aerosol dynamics, radiation, and thermodynamic models. The Scholar involved with this project will use this code to perform an investigation of the effect of high pressure on soot formation. This is a very important area of research as most combustion devices operate at high pressure. The Scholar will have the support of Dr. Nick Eaves and a MASc student to assist in performing this project.

Project_Title: Review of Management Tools That Can Be Utilized For Hospital Management and Simulation
Project_Description: Review of management tools that can be utilized for hospital management and simulation.

Project_Title: Vehicle Navigation using Standalone Inertial Measurement Units
Project_Description:
Inertial gyroscope technology is an established field. MEMS-based vibratory inertial sensors have recently shown promising performance characteristics, which is enabling the development of next-generation high precision standalone navigation systems. MEMS-based IMUs (inertial measurement units), which are often equipped with 3-axis accelerometers, gyroscopes, and magnetometers theoretically provide sufficient information to track position relative to an initial starting location by continuously double-integrating the 3-dimensional accelerometer data and incrementally updating position, a method known as dead reckoning. This project will implement mechatronic principles to demonstrate standalone vehicle navigation using IMUs.
The OS student will: review the literature; design an efficient microelectronic circuit; implement a prototype; experiment; and contribute to the analysis of its performance in gyroscope characterization

Project_Title: Soft robot gripper design and automation
Project_Description: Picking up fabric is a manual task, and is a bottleneck in the production process. Before composite fiber solutions can become a reality, the pick and place activities need to be automated, which is the focus of this project. New soft gripper design and manufacturing solutions will be manufactured using additive manufacturing to create specialty molds, and the working solutions need to be automated. This is a multi-term project.

Project_Title: Developing an Energy Efficient Readout Circuit for MEMS Gyroscope
Project_Description: Inertial gyrosocope tehcnology is established field. Any improvement in the device’s performance or testing is of great interest for research and industry. Development of a gyroscope readout circuit with low power consumption, integrability, and high sensitivity can address the growing demand for miniaturized inertial units with low power consumption and high reliability. This project will develop a compact circuit for micro-electro-mechanical systems (MEMS)-based gyroscope implementation, based on off-the-shelf components.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: conduct literature review; design an efficient microelectronic circuit; implement a prototype; and experiment with the prototype, contributing to the analysis of performance in gyroscope characterization.

Project_Title: Electronics Development for Capacitive Sensing in Droplet Based Microfluids
Project_Description: Lap-on-a-chip (LOC) devices are miniature micro/nano-fluidic devices that integrate different laboratory biochemical functionality at the microscale. LOC devices handle small amounts of fluids and offer lower sample and reagent usage, reduced cost, and faster biochemical analysis. However, real-time controlling and sensing of biochemical analysis in LOC devices remains a challenge. This project will develop a real-time capacitive sensing platform for droplet-based LOC devices. In electrowetting on deilectyric (EWOD) based LOC devices, a droplet is sandwiched between two electrodes, creating capacitors. The ropolet is manipulated via application of electric potential between the electrodes, which makes changes in capacitance.  The project will first use a capacitence meter to demonstrate proof of concept and identify sensing parameters. It will then develop a simple electrical circuit for sensing the changes in the capacitance when the droplet moves and reactions occur. The project will involve a literature review on capacitive sensing, designing an electrical circuit platform, and testing performance of the electronics.

Project_Title: Investigating Mechanical and Physical Properties of Multi-material Specimens
Project_Description: The goal of this project is to provide fundamental knowledge to support using AM processes, in combination with other fabrication strategies, to develop prototype and short production run tooling.  The binder jet printing AM process family will be employed for fabricating an external shell for a mold and various interior materials will be employed to fill the mold shell to provide the required heat flow and strength characteristics. Raw materials (particulate matter size and type, resin, hardener, etc.) are the base materials, which need to be employed, as well as the appropriate 3D printed bounding layers and its form. Mixing of the particle types with the resin and hardening material to establish an optimal density, thickness, and material balance distribution needs to be performed as the first step. Determining the mold shell design characteristics, and testing them is the next step. This includes determining the heat transfer, compression, thermal expansion and endurance characteristics are established for the fill material composite (the focus of this project), various sub-process alternatives need to be explored.  The processing alternatives with respect to the mold surface layer shape, thickness and filling/compacting strategy, will all impact the tool life, part quality, and ease of use. The product bill of materials needs to be identified in a rigorous manner, as well as the processing steps, order of operations, process parameters, layering configurations, and so forth.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Design of experiments for the experimental strategy, Design, fabrication, and testing of test specimens, data collection, and data analysis.

Project_Title: Basal Metabolic Rate Calculator
Project_Description: Student will follow the basic steps of the research process to develop a VBA-driven calculator that receives personal data as input and outputs useful information related to calorie/energy balance.

The system will be designed such that the user interface and information retrieval/display is understandable to a user with little subject knowledge. Project_Title: Investigation of materials, processees and techniques for 3D Printed Molds
Project_Description: To date, molds are typically fabricated via machining. Additive manufacturing processes are employed in rapid tooling scenarios, where the AM process is employed to create a pattern. This project relates to exploring the suitability of selected additive manufacturing processes for mold fabrication. One idea relates to fabricating a shell which includes the molding pattern, and then incorporate a low cost epoxy-resin-filler mix for the balance of the mold. The additive manufacturing element will simplify the manufacturing complexity related to the mold shape, and the filler material needs to provide the strength, endurance, heat transfer, and related characteristics. We need to investigate various fill materials / material combinations in a systematic manner experimentally prior to developing realistic design models. This work is part of an industrial research project.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
Investigate appropriate filler materials
Investigate epoxy resins
Fabricate test specimens: Mix materials, create test samples, and measure the ‘cured’ specimen characteristics
Destructive testing of specimens: compressive, bending, fatigue, etc. (with graduate students)
	Assist with the data collection
Assist with multiple material test specimen development (with research team): both design and physical experiment specimens

Project_Title: Investigating 'Exo-Skeleton' Design Solutions Using Reverse Engineering and 3D Printing
Project_Description: Orthotic braces are used for a wide range of applications. Their fundamental purpose is to for motion control or support. The long term goal of this project is to develop an effective system for creating braces for every joint in the human body, with a high degree of customizability and control, while maintaining a low cost and user-friendly procedure. There are numerous constraints, both related to design and manufacturing. Effectively employing flexible, advanced data collection, design, and manufacturing techniques would address this situation. Previous research has shown that robust reverse engineering body components is a challenge, as well as developing models that readily adapt to differing body shapes. A FARO Platinum portable CMM / scanner will be employed to collect ‘hand’ data (a variety of scenarios will be explored, i.e. glove, glove with markers, etc.), and CAD tools will be used to create models. CAD tools, such as Rhinoceros® with the Grasshopper® graphical programming interface (R-G) will be explored for this project. SolidWorks and CATIA V5 CAD/CAE software tools have used to develop parametric solid models for the fingers and brace solutions, but they are limited in scope. The R-G tools are used for complex, organic shapes, and may be more applicable than the other software tools employed. This is an extension of prior work
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: CAD surface modelling using R-G tools, data collection strategies,  fixturing design and fabrication, and data analysis. Ultimately, the student would like to print 3D model hat originated from the parametric CAD design of selected finger bones.

Project_Title: RESOURCE-BASED AND GLASS CLIFF PERSPECTIVES: ETHNIC CEO TENURE
Project_Description:
Cliff hypothesis extends to ethnic minority CEOs. The Glass Cliff hypothesis holds that women tend to be appointed as CEOs in precarious situations. If the Glass Cliff is true, it explains low rates of appointment of female CEOs (because they are considered for only a subset of CEO openings - those where the firm is in a precarious state) and are likely to exit earlier (because firms in a precarious state are more likely to fail). I will examine whether the same concept applies to ethnic minority CEOs.

First, I will test the contention that ethnic minorities become CEOs in precarious situations. I will compare the average riskiness of situations in which ethnic minorities are appointed as CEOs with the riskiness of situations in which white males are appointed as CEOs.  Risk will be measured using the return on assets (ROA) and the standard deviation of the market returns on the firm's stock for the 12 months preceding the new appointment.  The second way that I will examine the extension of the Glass Cliff hypothesis is to test an implication of the hypothesis: If ethnic minorities do become CEOs in precarious situations more frequently than white males, we would expect, all else equal, that ethnic minorities' tenure as CEO would be shorter than that of white males, i.e., that ethnic minorities would leave their CEO roles a shorter time after entering compared to white males.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
- Build a database that includes all of the ethnic minority CEO successions available on the Execucomp database between 1992 and 2019. 
- Code data collected from firm proxy statements, the Marquis Who’s Who database and Bloomberg.

Project_Title: Re-Examining the Glass Cliff using Survival Analysis: The Case of Ethnic CEO Tenure
Project_Description:
Objectives: To determine whether ethnic minority corporate Chief Executive Officers (CEOs) experience shorter tenure in the CEO position compared to Caucasian CEOs.
Methodology: To test if CEO tenure depends on race we use survival analysis with the ethnicity of the CEO as a covariate. Survival analysis is a regression technique specifically designed to model time-to-an-event (in this case, time to CEO turnover, also known as CEO tenure). 
Our sample will consist of hand-collected data on CEO appointments between 1992 and 2016. The sample will be formed by searching Standard and Poor’s ExecuComp database for appointments of ethnic minority CEOs.
A corresponding matched sample of white male CEOs will also be created. The matching will be done by industry and size. First we will find all the firms in the same 4-digit Standard Industrial Classification (SIC) code as the sample firm, then from this list we will select the observation with the closest total assets in year 0 (year of the CEO appointment) to the original sample firm. Firms’ proxy statements, the Marquis Who’s Who database and Bloomberg will be used to obtain data that is not available on ExecuComp database. Financial statement information will be obtained from Compustat.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: The student will learn how to use financial databases (Execucomp, Bloomberg) and how to read and interpret corporate regulatory filings such as proxy statements, annual reports and 10Ks.  They will gain knowledge of corporate governance practices that may help them navigate their own careers.

The outstanding scholar will also assist with the development of presentation and workshop materials (e.g., Powerpoint, posters, etc.). These will be adaptable for online delivery.

Project_Title: Students' Knowledge Application in Nursing Education
Project_Description: This project involves several components related to students' application of learned knowledge to: (1) simulated MAiD (Medical Assistance in Dying) situations, (2) animated scenarios, (3) interprofessional nursing education, and (4) graduate nursing education.

Project_Title: Engaging Stakeholders to Enhance Interprofessional Palliative Care in Southwestern Ontario
Project_Description: Interprofessional palliative care is the “gold standard” for residents living in long-term care facilities. Unfortunately, there are significant regulatory, organizational, and professional obstacles to its integration.  Appropriate palliative care is especially hindered when residents experience impairments in cognitive and verbal communication.  In particular, residents with dementia are less likely to report pain and to receive treatment for pain.  A systematic review conducted by our research team reveals the significant barriers to include: a lack of consistent and accurate assessment of pain by long-term care team members; insufficient team communication; and a lack of interprofessional knowledge and education. There is need for interprofessional palliative care education in areas such as: pain and symptom management, delirium, dyspnea/airway clearance, psychosocial and spiritual support, and interprofessional communication.  Discussions with stakeholders have emphasized the need for a task force to develop (a) guiding principles for long-term care; and (b) a standardized educational protocol.  Local stakeholders include The Hospice, the Alzheimer Society, the Erie-St. Clair Local Health Integration Network, the Southwestern Ontario Academic Health Network, Tecumseh and Southwood Lakes Extendicare and Franklin Gardens Nursing Home.   Financial and in-kind support from the deVeber Institute of Bioethics and Social Research will support planning and a pilot intervention of the protocol.

Project_Title: The Impact of Diabetes, Hypertension, Heart Failure, and COPD on Stroke Rehabilitation Outcomes
Project_Description:
OBJECTIVES: 
1. Examine the independent associations between diabetes, hypertension, heart failure and COPD in stroke patients and functional gains, length of stay, and discharge destination.
2. Determine whether increased risk of stroke (based on an increase in CHADS2 score) in patients with multiple comorbidities is associated with functional gains, length of stay, and discharge destination.

IMPLICATIONS:
We hope that our study will provide a better understanding of whether patients who have a particular comorbidity in addition to stroke are more likely to experience poorer functional outcomes and to benefit from rehabilitation than those who do not have that particular comorbidity.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
1. Conduct a literature search
2. Locate, and download appropriate articles
3. Summarize contents of the articles in a review table.

Project_Title: Natural Health Products in Neurodegenerative Disorders
Project_Description: Human cell culture, firbro blasts from Healthy and AD patients (commercially available) will be grown and, human neuroblastoma cells will be differentiated to neuronal cells.  NHPs like CO10, Methylemblate will be used to study their protective ability on these cells.

Project_Title: Broadcasting Happiness
Project_Description: This project is research-based and is a great opportunity for a student to follow a team as strategies are implemented to encourage a positive, happy workplace. COVID19 has placed enormous pressure on all healthcare employees and it has become difficult to keep spirits high. The student will be involved in the research and development of surveys, activities and metrics that will enhance morale of the team. This will need to be done in a manner that is organic and non-intrusive. The objective is to demonstrate that by creating an optimistic workspace it will pay big dividends by enhancing productivity, retaining great employees, decrease workplace stress, encourage fact-checking (opposed to assuming), encourage productive communication among team members, staff engagement and that all these cultural advancements will translate into a positive patient visit all around.  The student will receive support from the Executive Director and from all the team members who are active participants of our in-house team building committees.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: The student will create a performance review process that provides structure for management, expectations for team members, necessary feedback, an opportunity to request professional development, and methods for employee coaching or assistance for those who those who may become dissatisfied.  Existing strategies from other industries will need to be researched to find a model that will work within the workplace and the student will need to develop baseline surveys, strategies, measure how the strategies contribute to staff morale, retention of staff and enhance workplace culture, create performance review materials, processes and determine frequency of reviews.

Project_Title: Fostering Students as Partners: An Examination of Student-Faculty Partnerships in Science
Project_Description:
Student partnership can be described as a collaborative, reciprocal process through which all participants have th
e opportunity to contribute equally, although not necessarily in the same ways, to curricular or pedagogical conce
ptualization, decision making, implementation, investigation, or analysis. Working with students as partners can in
clude a range of practices (e.g., curriculum development, community service, and disciplinary research) and offer
s a plethora of benefits to students and faculty (e.g., increased learning, employability, and confidence). The purp
ose of this project is to examine students as partners within the Faculty of Science with the goal of promoting the
benefits of this approach across campus. This project will include examining the ways in which faculty are engagi
ng with students as partners, the key characteristics that underpin these partnerships, student and faculty percept
ions of the benefits and challenges of working as partners, and an exploration of current and future opportunities f
or formal recognition for students who engage as partners. The outcomes of this project will be used to develop re
sources for students and faculty interested in engaging with students as partners, and will be disseminated through campus-wide workshops and teaching and learning conferences. Note: Dr.Dora Cavallo Medved will be a
co supervisor for this project.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: The Outstanding Scholar will work closely with the research team, consisting of Dr. Laura Chittle (Postdoctoral Fellow in Science), Dr. Dora Cavallo-Medved (faculty member in Biomedical Sciences), and Dr. Chris Houser (Dean of Science) in completing this project, which has the potential for multiple terms of work. The student will: locate and review literature, support data collection through creating an online survey and interviewing science faculty members and students, assist in the analysis of qualitative and qualitative data, and contribute to the dissemination of research findings through conference presentations, manuscripts, and design of interactive workshops.

Project_Title: Program Development in the Faculty of Science: Examining Procedures, Challenges, and Opportunities
Project_Description:
Program development refers to the creation and revision of courses and degree programs. This process plays a
critical role in enhancing and inspiring student learning. The purpose of this project is to examine approaches to p
rogram development within the Faculty of Science. In particular, we are interested in understanding departmental
procedures and policies for program  development and their perceived efficiency and effectiveness as well as the
challenges experienced throughout the program development process. These goals will be accomplished by cond
