ucting a literature review, interviewing faculty members in all eight departments within Science, and analyzing and
disseminating these results to the campus community. Findings from   this project will provide meaningful contributions to the creation of new courses, programs, and certificates in the Faculty of Science. Note: Dr. Chris Houser will be a co-supervisor for this project.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
The Outstanding Scholar will work closely with the research team, consisting of Dr. Laura Chittle (Postdoctoral Fellow in
Science), Dr. Chris Houser (Dean of Science), and Dr. Erika Kustra (Director of the Centre of Teaching and Learning) in
completing this project, which has the potential for multiple terms of work. The student will: locate and review literature,
support data collection through interviewing science faculty members, assist in the analysis of qualitative data, and contribute to the dissemination of research findings through conference presentations, manuscripts, and departmental reports.

Project_Title: GnRH and Soma Size Comparison in Electric Yellow Ciclids
Project_Description: The student will determine whether there is a correlation between GnRH and soma size, between gender, GSI, and any of the correlates from previous measurements. The student will also feed and care for the fish.

Project_Title: Exploring the perspectives of older adults with respect to aging, physical activity, healthcare, and activities of daily living
Project_Description:
Gathering and analyzing the perspectives of older adults about their experiences with various aspects of life is important for furthering research, designing innovative products, and modifying policies related to public health.
Working on this project will afford you the opportunity to work with a team interviewing older adults, analyzing data, and various approaches of knowledge dissemination (e.g., papers, presentations)
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
Work within a research team
Analyze data (training provided)
Potential to participate in conducting interviews
Potential to assist with various aspects of knowledge dissemination (e.g., papers, presentations)

Project_Title: Conjugated Polymer Nanoparticles for Nanomedicine
Project_Description: The research project focuses on the design and synthesis of new conjugated polymers nanoparticles. These types of nanoparticles are particularly interesting for bioimaging and labeling of different types of cells. The student will design, synthesize, and characterize nanoparticles to target specific types of malignant cells and tumors through molecular recognition. A complete characterization of the nanoparticles will be performed, both in silico and vivo with our collaborators.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: The student will learn basic and advanced organic chemistry. Polymerization techniques and reactions will be used to prepare the various nanoparticles and materials. Full characterization of the materials will be performed which will allow the student to learn various instrumental techniques. In the final step of the project, the materials will be used to track different types of biological markers.

Project_Title: Conjugated Polymers for Next Generation Electronics
Project_Description: The research project focuses on the design and synthesis of stretchable and self-heatable semiconducting polymers. By using organic synthesis, different types of conjugated polymers, known to be excellent semiconductors, will be prepared and tailored to possess both good electronic and mechanical properties. This will be enabled by the utilization of various strategies, including supramolecular chemistry and polymer blending. This project will focus on the polymer design and synthesis, but will also include materials characterization through multiple analytical techniques and structural optimization by theoretical calculations and simulation.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: The students will learn basic and advanced organic polymer chemistry. Full characterization of the materials will be performed by multiple techniques. The student will be actively involved in the group via group meetings and collaborations with other team members.

Project_Title: Violence victimization for college students
Project_Description: This study examined the characteristics of lifelong polyvictimization among college students in North America by using the WHO’s typology of interpersonal violence. The study also identified the differences in victimization based on demographic characteristics, such as gender, sexual orientation, and race/ethnicity.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: •	Literature review and literature analysis

Project_Title: Intellectual Property Cores for FPGAs
Project_Description: Intellectual property cores are crucial in many real world FPGA applications such as digital signal processing, cryptography, networking, etc. The students will focus on a specific application area and prepare a report on the IP cores available and identify the potential for future work in that application area. If time permits, they can also enhance existing IP cores and create new ones. The students who want to work on this project must have a grade of at least 75%  in the 06-88-330 course.

Project_Title: Auditory Mechanism
Project_Description: Hearing is dependent on both the brain and the ears. Our research focuses on processes and mechanisms in the  brain that are important for hearing. We also study how changes in physiological, chemical, and anatomical features in the brain structures responsible for hearing can lead to hearing abnormalities such as deafness and tinnitus.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: The students will participate in weekly lab meetings, reference article reading, and helping graduate students in experiments and data analysis.

Project_Title: Genetic Screening of Circadian Clock Targets in the Drosophila Intestine
Project_Description: Student will set up crosses of Drosophila melanogaster in order to obtain the correct genotype. This will involve training in Drosophila genetics and stock maintenance. A library of RNAi lines will be crossed into this background in order to deplete genes of interest in intestinal stem cells.  The offspring will be the focus of the study. Flies will be fed intestine-damaging chemicals and the intestines will be dissected and examined by flourescence microscopy.  Genetic screening will be image-based and will involve quantifying intestinal stem cells based on transgenic markers in this tissue.  The stress response will thus be studied in order to identify genes that disrupt regeneration in this context.

Project_Title: Microbiology Activities for Outreach and Improved Learning
Project_Description: There is a need for engaging and effective activities to help introduce people to key microbiology concepts, observing microbes in the world around them, and understanding the differences between different types of microbes. This project focuses on activities for use with Foldscopes (affordable paper microscopes) and for Introductory Microbiology classes and/or laboratory exercises.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
-	Review literature on Foldscope use, microbiology misconceptions.
-	Develop materials to help students:
o	Assemble Foldscopes
o	Use Foldscopes to view/photograph microbes in their surroundings
-	Assist with development of class activities and resources that address key misconceptions about eukaryotic microbes, using evidence-based methods that actively engage students and help them address their own misconceptions. These resources will be used in the Introductory Microbiology courses in the Fall.

Project_Title: Asymmetric Prep and Partial Molecular Volumes
Project_Description: Investigating plasma membrane coupling across leaflets and changing partial molecular volumes on lipid composition.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Create solutions, measure mass of lipids, measure density using densitometer, and prepare vesicles.

Project_Title: Drosophila Nociception
Project_Description: Detecting and avoiding noxious stimuli is critical for survival. Nociceptive sensory neurons detect these stimuli and activate neural circuits that elicit stereotyped escape responses. Drosophila larvae have class IV (cIV) multidendritic neurons which are morphologically and functionally analogous to vertebrate nociceptive neurons. Drosophila larvae use these sensory neurons to detect noxious stimuli and then escape by eliciting a sequential body bending and corkscrew-like rolling response. The aim of this project is to identify genes that are required for this nociceptive-like escape response.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: This project will involve Drosophila genetics and thermal nociception assays.

Project_Title: Investigation of Asymmetric Membranes
Project_Description: Investigating plasma membrane coupling across leaflets. This has implications in protein functioning and signalling.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Conduct NMR measurements, prepare vesicles, and characterize particles.

Project_Title: Olfactory Sub-Systems at the Base of Vertebrae Evolution
Project_Description: Neuroanatomy of olfactory sensory neuron and of second order neutrons will be probed to understand how the vertebrate brain organizes sensory input to generate a behavioral response.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Retrograde nerve tracing and animal care

Project_Title: Examining Chemosensory Response of the Accessory Olfactory Organ in Sea Lampreys
Project_Description: The accessory olfactory organ in sea lampreys will be studied to observe whether or not responses to certain pheromones will be observed. The method used to load the dye is called electroporation and involves two electrodes that will be inserted into the organism to make a complete electrical circuit. This method is novel in Sea Lampreys
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
-	Handle animals and ensure proper care
-	Conduct experiments using electropuration machine
-	Use cryostat (used for making miniscule slices of biological structures)
-	Use microscope
-	Conduct calcium imaging experiments
-	Make solutions needed for experiments
-	Help graduate students and others with tasks in lab

Project_Title: Computational Investigations on Physiologically Important Enzymes
Project_Description:
Society is facing a global health crisis; the rise of drug-resistant pathogens. That is, society faces the real prospect of returning to a time when common diseases, many that are presently easily treatable, could again become deadly. Hence, there is a need to identify, characterize and/or design new therapeutic agents and their targets. Aminoacyl-tRNA synthetases are a class of enzymes found in all living organisms that have been shown to have a broad range of biochemically essential roles.1 For example, they are thought to be involved in viral assembly, porphyrin biosynthesis, inflammation, and apoptosis (cell death). They are perhaps best known, however, for their central role in protein biosynthesis. In this role they catalyse two reactions within a single active site. First, they catalyse the activation of their respective amino acid by reacting them with ATP to give an aminoacyl-adenylate derivative. The latter is then reacted with their respective tRNA to give the desired aminoacylated-tRNA species'.1

It is only quite recently that we have begun to more fully understand the atomistic details of the catalytic mechanisms. For instance, it has been shown that many aaRS's have not only an aminoacylation activation/transfer active site, but also an editing site. The former catalyses the amino acid activation and subsequent aminoacylation of the tRNA. In contrast, the latter site catalyses the removal of incorrect aminoacyl groups from aminoacylated-tRNA.

Thus, aaRSs present potentially rich targets for therapeutic agents. However, to date, only one potential drug agent that targets aaRS's, in any organism, has been identified. In this project, building upon our recent studies on the editing mechanisms of several related aaRS (e.g., MetRS, LeuRS and ValRS), we will aim to identify potential inhibitors, therapeutic drug agents. This will involve data mining of standard "drug databases", as well as Docking, Molecular Dynamics (MD) and related methods.

Molecular recognition and specificity are key features of biomolecular processes, e.g., enzyme catalysis. These concepts also form the foundation of the design of many synthetic systems such as industrial catalysts and therapeutic drug agents. Amongst biocatalytic systems the enzymes aminoacyl-tRNA synthetases (aaRSs) stand out for their apparent exceptional fidelity. This is all the more remarkable in part because they have been found to be involved in a broad range of biochemically essential roles.1 Furthermore, due to their role at the interface between nucleic acid and protein biochemistry, they are thought to be very ancient.2 Hence, they potentially represent fundamental approaches to biocatalysis and molecular recognition.
AaRSs are perhaps best known for their central role in protein synthesis. In particular, they are catalyse activation of amino acids by attaching them to their cognate tRNA, such that they can then be incorporated into proteins, the biomolecular "work horses" of life. That is, they catalyse aminocylation of their cognate tRNA.1 Remarkably, despite their different substrates and non-conserved active sites, all aaRS's catalyse the same reactions within a single active site: (i) reaction of their cognate amino acid with ATP, followed by reaction with their cognate tRNA.3,4 Furthermore, on average they catalyse these reactions with an outstandingly low error rate of approximately 1 in 10000! However, how they are able to catalyse such reactions and with such high-specificity is unclear. In many cases aaRS's have been found to contain a second active site known as the editing site. Within this site any mis-aminoacylated tRNA's are reacted such that the incorrect amino acid is removed. Some, however, do not possess or even need an editing site.
Cysteinyl-tRNA synthetase (CysRS) catalytically activates cysteine and its attachment to its cognate tRNA. It is one of just three (3) aaRS's that have to-date been found to contain a metal ion, specifically a zinc (II) ion, within its active site.5 While the exact role of the Zn(II) ion is unknown, it has been suggested to be only involved in substrate binding and recognition. Importantly, cysteine is of similar size to alanine and has similar size and functionality to serine. Remarkably, despite these similarities, CysRS does not contain or appear to require an editing site. That is, it can discern between cysteine, serine and alanine with complete fidelity! Central to an understanding of an enzymes biochemical role and the development of highly-specific therapeutic drugs is elucidation of its catalytic mechanism.
In this project a state-of-the-art multi-scale computational chemistry approach will be used to investigate the: (i) catalytic mechanism of cysteine activation by CysRS; (ii) binding of cysteine, serine or alanine within the active site of CysRS, and (iii) characterization and identification of possible inhibitors (e.g., therapeutic drug agents). More specifically, we will apply Docking, molecular dynamics (MD), and ONIOM-type hybrid quantum mechanics/molecular mechanics (QM/MM) methods in combination with very large enzyme chemical models.
1. Brown, M. V.; Reader, J. S.; Tzima, E. Vascul. Pharmacol. 2010, 52, 21.
2. Uter, N. T.; Perona, J. J. Biochemistry 2006, 45, 6858.
3. Minajigi, A.; Francklyn, C. S. PNAS USA 2008, 105, 17748.
4. Guth, E.; Connolly, S. H. et al. Biochemistry 2005, 44, 3785.
5. Sokabe, M.; Ose, T. et al. PNAS USA 2009, 106, 11028.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
The student should have a broad knowledge of fundamental chemical principles, e.g., knowledge of organic, inorganic and physical chemistry. While at least some Quantum chemistry and biochemistry background and knowledge is advantageous, it is not essential. Previous students in my group have come from pharmacy, chemistry, biochemistry and biology backgrounds. Our research group has experience and is adept at training undergraduates in such skills and also teaching them related knowledge during the course of the project. Computer coding skills are not essential as all software used (Gaussian 09, Molecular Operating Environment, NAMD) is commercially available.

Students will prepare and submit calculations, help analyze the data, and also prepare presentation materials. Students will also work with other members of the group to pursue and achieve the research goals.

Project_Title: Z Wave Technology for Smart Homes
Project_Description:
ZWave is a protocol for communication among devices used for home automation. The objective of this project is to:
a- Investigate the application of ZWave technology to design a smart home
b- Develop a method to combine ZWave and Near Field Enabled (NFC) technologies for home automation
c- Develop an application for remote automation using smartphones

Project_Title: Sequencing RNA Splice Junctions for Prostate Cancer
Project_Description: The student will run agarose cells, perform Western blot analysis, RNA extraction, cDNA reactions, QRT-PCR tests, data analysis, and make solutions such as buffers.

Project_Title: Sensor and Tracking System in Autonomous Vehicle
Project_Description: Advanced Driver Assistance System (ADAS) has improved the overall safety of the driving vehicle. Within ADAS, sensors are the crucial component of the autonomous vehicle by analyzing surrounding information. In this project, researching student will engage in investigating different types of sensors, their contributions in ADAS, and how to improve the tracking performance.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
stigating different types of sensors within modern ADAS
Proposing methods to potentially improve the tracking performance 
Documenting any findings in a professional report

Project_Title: Sleep, Socioemotional Functioning, & Cognition in Sport-Related Concussion
Project_Description: The purpose of this project is to investigate the interactions between sleep, socioemotional functioning and cognition in the context of sport-related concussion (SRC). It is well known that sleep dysfunction is a common consequence of SRC, but the specifics of this association have not been well-described. Furthermore, sleep is known to influence many of the clinical variables involved in SRC, including symptom-reporting, cognition, and socioemotional functioning. This project will longitudinally track, for several weeks, collegiate level athletes with SRC using wrist-worn actigraphy (Fitbit) as an objective measure of sleep variables. These data will be complemented by self-report data on sleep, socioemotional functioning and subjective cognitive complaints, as well as performance-based measures of cognition. The goal will be: (1) to temporally map sleep after concussion and (2) examine the effects of sleep on clinical variables.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Student responsibilities will include: gaining competence at interviewing skills and neuropsychological test administration, as well as  higher-level skills, such as becoming familiar with the logistics of running a large, complex study. Furthermore, there is potentially opportunity to develop skills in statistical analysis and research communication via research reports and/or poster presentations at conferences.

Project_Title: Simulation of an Atmospheric Boundary Layer in a Wind Tunnel
Project_Description: A wind tunnel is an important research tool in the field of wind engineering. It is used extensively to study various wind-related structural and nvironmental issues accurring within the atmosphereic boundary layer, of which the characteristics of the approaching wind could be considerably affected by the terrain conditions. Therefore, when studying these issues in the wind tunnel, it is of utmost importance to fithfully reproduce the terrrain conditions at the site. This is usually achieved by setting up passive devices in the upstream of the wind tunnel test section, and the spire-roughness-element technology is the most widely-used approach. In the current study, this technology will be applied to simulate the atmospheric boundary layer in a new wind tunnel facility at the University of Windsor. The student will be assisting a master’s degree student to conduct the simulation in the wind tunnel, and will help in the experimental set-up, the data collection, and the data analysis.

Project_Title: Social Media for Good or Evil in Music and Arts Education
Project_Description:
Social media and social network sites (SNSs) are ubiquitous to the point that it is hard to imagine modern life – including all aspects of music and arts making, learning, teaching, and consumption – without them. Although music and arts education researchers have begun examining social media and SNSs in learning and teaching, exploration of their use in education remains largely unexamined from a critical perspective. 

The successful student will work with Dr. Sirek (Faculty of Education) on a research project investigating social media and social network sites, and music and arts education. The project with Dr. Sirek will be connected to Dr. Janice Waldron (School of Creative Arts) and another Outstanding Scholar to create an interdisciplinary team and facilitate research mentorship and learning. The culmination will be an academic presentation and subsequent publication of research disseminated at MayDay Group Colloquium 33, a forthcoming event co-hosted by Dr.Janice Waldron and Dr. Danielle Sirek at the University of Windsor in June 2022.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Prepare literature review; contribute to data analysis; prepare an academic presentation; prepare a manuscript for publication; conference planning and facilitating of knowledge dissemination; formatting and academic editing of publications associated with the MayDay Group and its annual colloquium (e.g., TOPICS;  ACT; MDG Newsletter);  outreach in the community as related to the research project and/or colloquium 33 at University of Windsor.

Project_Title: Social Network Analysis
Project_Description:
Social Network Analysis is growing in popularity given the ubiquity of its use and increasing volume of usage data collected, particularly publically accessible data. Furthermore, commercial social network platforms continue to provide APIs for accessing the data. With the advancements in artificial intelligence, particularly in the field of machine learning, we seek to employ advanced heuristics to develop analysis tools capable of handing dynamic real-time networks.
This work presents several potential applications of value to society, of which we can address an example with disaster detection. Can the real-time analysis of social media (tweets, posts, etc.) help us detect happening disasters in real-time? The work can range from global scale mapping and notification to local, eg. campus-level monitoring. The impact is expected to have value in assisting emergency response personnel and ultimately saving lives.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
Use of social network API’s to search, parse and send posts
- Use of graphing libraries to visualize data
- Create an intelligent monitoring system that monitors social media to detect disasters, this would require new development and use of heuristics in machine learning.
- Develop a dashboard monitor or client tool for information dissemination.
- Analyze locations to determine area impacted by a disaster; may require the use of NLP libraries to extract locations from the text.

Project_Title: Standardization of variable names used in lab activities
Project_Description: Standardizing research terminology within the Adult Neuropsychology Research Group. This is a short-term project aimed at extracting the variable names used in existing SPSS databases and developing a common, standardized nomenclature and SPSS code book for use across the lab. The resultant list of terms will then be incorporated into instructional literature used in training research assistants in test scoring and SPSS data entry.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Acquire SPSS skills; extract variable lists; carefully label variables in a way that is readily recognizable to subsequent users under Dr. Erdodi’s supervision, establish a list of acceptable terms; present to other members of the lab and provide any needed support setting up databases for new studies, review and revise all instructional literature used for training test scoring and SPSS data entry

Project_Title: Space Audit - Programming
Project_Description: The completion of a space audit for the Centre for Teaching and Learning and the development of a functional space program that supports the CTL`s needs and enhances its learning-centred focus.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
•	Literature review and evaluation
•	Collecting information
•	Data analysis and interpretation
•	Interviews and presentations

Project_Title: Task force on student evaluations of teaching (SET)
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: In general, the student will help with library research and data gathering that may include web searches and contacting personnel at other universities to learn more about best practices for gathering teaching feedback. Also, the student may possibly aid in a research study to learn how students complete the current SET questionnaire.

Project_Title: Test and Evaluation of Electronic Nose Sensor Prototype
Project_Description:
Assisting graduate students and post doctoral fellows with programming, testing, and evaluating electronic nose sensor prototypes. Students will gain understanding of electronic nose systems concepts and micro machined (MEMS) sensors for medical and environmental monitoring application. Students will assist in conducting sensor tests and evaluations using a gas management system and conduct sensor data analysis. 
Student will assist with other analysis related projects. Student write reports, provide presentations, and will check format and review reports written by other lab members.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Literature review, developing program recipe to run gas management system, accompanying researchers during the prototype test and evaluations, analyzing test data, writing reports, presenting test results, additional related work

Project_Title: The Effect of Motivation Status on Performance Validity in Concussion Baseline Testing
Project_Description: A study examining how factors such as malingering affect results in neuropsychological assessments
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
-Receive training in neuropsychological tests
-Learn and Practice the tests
-Conduct neuropsychological assessments with participants who sign up via the Participant Pool.

Project_Title: The Food History of the Windsor/Detroit Borderlands
Project_Description: Food practices are crucial to immigrant identities, from the purchase of products in specialized markets to the preparation of food in the home according to traditions brought from the homeland. Food History traces both the economic and political framework in which foodways are practiced, as well as the social and cultural ways in which food preparation and consumption tells us who we are and where we came from.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Students working for this project will identify a specific Windsor/Detroit Foodway, create an annotated bibliography of that food tradition and then research and interview members of that community.

Project_Title: The Evaluation of the ATP Short Form.
Project_Description: To evaluate the reliability and validity of the scale that measures positive/negative attitude towards poverty and poor people.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: To complete a literature review on the scale and find out what kind of information (reliability and validity) is avaliable.

Project_Title: The Inspired Acting Lab
Project_Description: The Inspired Acting Lab is a practical lab in which participants are working on developing new acting exercises in Fantastic Realism.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: The student will observe the work, take notes, transcribe comments from participants, edit notes, record the exercises using AV equipment, and edit the recorded material.

Project_Title: The Medieval English Barbers: Public versus Private Spheres of Activity
Project_Description:
The project seeks to compare public and private documents containing information about the activities of the early English barber, in hopes of finding to what extent public opinion (barbers as criminals, being involved with prostituition, and illicit medicine) correlates with fact.
The OS student would be asked to participate in the research in a fashion and field that they find interesting and engaging. Examples of tasks (depending on skill level) include: creations of annotated bibliographies, locating archival materials, compiling, sorting, and archiving data, read and write reviews and précis of secondary materials, and finally working to assist in publishing the research. 
For further information about the project, as well as what the research work may involve, contact Dr. Mark Johnston.

Project_Title: The Politics of Resource Extraction and Its Impact on Local and Indigenous Communities
Project_Description: Indigenous communities and those in remote locations are the most affected by resources development. This proposal is for assistance in the development of a research project launched jointly with community members and academic partners to study the relationship between corporate entities and governments in the context of resource development and how that relationship affects local communities. This first phase of this project is to gather background information to compile a literature review for a grant application for the Social Sciences and Humanities Research Council (SSHRC) Insight Connection Grant. The second phase of this project is aimed to expand the scope of this research to conduct a study of the politics of resource extraction and the impact of resource extraction on local and Indigenous communities in several regions across Canada, Russia and the US.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: The assigned researcher (outstanding scholar) would gain valuable experience by assisting with an extensive literature review for the proposed research project, and would develop expertise in grant writing by helping prepare the Insight Connection grant. This is the preliminary work of a larger research project.

Project_Title: The Role and Influence of Indigenous Women in Canadian Politics
Project_Description: In recent years, there has been a noted increase in participation of Indigenous women in the political sphere in Canada.  A number of Indigenous women currently serve as senators, members of the federal parliament, and represent Indigenous voices at the provincial and national level. There are many others who are involved in First Nations band-level leadership. The aim of this research project is to explore the influence of the increased participation of female Indigenous voices in Canadian politics. The first phase of this program is to gather information through a literature review (including government documents and grey literature) and media survey. The second phase of the project is to expand the scope of the project out of the literature and into the lives of the Indigenous women serving in these roles, as well as those who serve along side them to fully capture the story being told. Designed in collaboration with other Indigenous women in academia, our aim is to provide an overview of the influence of Indigenous women in Canadian politics, and give voice to their stories.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: The assigned researcher (outstanding scholar) would gain valuable experience by assisting with an extensive literature review for the proposed research project, and would develop expertise in grant writing by helping prepare the Insight development SSHRC research grant. This is the preliminary work of a larger research project. The current research is possible through remote engagement.

Project_Title: The Use of Standardized Simulated Clients for Social Work Training
Project_Description: In partnership with Dramatic Arts, the School of Social Work is seeking to support a project in which dramatic arts students act as standardized, simulated clients, to help social work students train for practice. This research project includes compiling and creating relevant research to ensure program justification and ultimately success.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
Supporting focus group data collection and analysis
Research on the use of standardized simulated clients for student training (to expand previous work)
Supporting current Graduate Student Assistants

Project_Title: The Translation of Official Documents in Officially Bilingual Countries
Project_Description: This project will look at the linguistic and cultural challenges associated with the translation of official documents issued by the governments of bilingual countries.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
-Data collection
-Typing and checking the format of documents

Project_Title: Thermally Sensitive End-Caps for Self-Immolative Materials
Project_Description: Self-immolative polymers are degradable polymers whose decomposition needs to be triggered-they are perfectly stable plastics until a specific stimulus is applied. They are different from traditional biodegradable polymers in that degradation takes place in hours rather than months. Recent efforts have shown that heat is an excellent trigger. However, ideally, a library of compounds would be available that would allow for different temperatures to be used to activate the cleavage. This project is based on the synthesis and characterization of new molecules that can degrade at different temperatures and involves synthetic chemistry and physical organic chemistry.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Synthetic organic chemistry. Kinetic measurements by NMR, determination of rate constants. Research, writing and communication. Senior students will also be given the opportunity for supervision after a year once well established in the lab. Small team collaboration is essential.

Project_Title: Ultrasonic Transducers Evaluation and Characterization
Project_Description:
Assisting graduate students and post doctoral fellows with test and evaluation of ultrasonic imaging transducers, assisting in building test set up. Students will gain understanding of ultrasonic imaging and advanced MEMS-based  ultrasonic transducers. 
Student will assist with other analysis related projects. Student write reports, provide presentations, and will check format and review reports written by other lab members.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Literature review into ultrasonic imaging for medical application,developing program and test setup for evaluating commercial and prototype ultrasonic transducers,accompanying researchers during the prototype test and evaluations, analyzing test data, writing reports, presenting test results, additional related work

Project_Title: Understanding Economic News
Project_Description: The main purpose of this project is to design an Economics course based on current economic news. The novelty of this proposal is to design a course that (i) breaks down Economics into small, trivial, intuitive steps and (ii) uses the media as the main source for the course material. Newspapers articles, for instance, are often used in Macroeconomic courses to illustrate a concept or a particular relationship between variable. The proposed course intends to invert this relationship. Students would read about the economy from different media sources (newspapers, magazines, blogs, ...), the instructor would flag key concepts and explain them in details. The course content would be updated constantly (weekly, daily).
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Research Tasks and Activities: Select different media sources (newspapers, magazines, blogs, ...) of economic news; prepare a set of slides covering key economic variables (output, unemployment, inflation, interest rates, exchange rates), adapted to be used in the proposed course; prepare a database of these economic variables to generate figures and currently update it; Prepare the online platform for the online course; * Sample of news/articles sources: The Economist, Wall Street Journal, Financial Times, New York Times, The Globe and Mail, Financial Post, Bloomberg.com, Reuters.com, Bank of Canada, Federal Reserve Board (US), project-syndicate.org; several blogs/podcasts by renowned economists. (Students have access to several of these sources through the Leddy Library).

Project_Title: Understanding the Landscape of Brain Tumor Research Funding
Project_Description: This project will focus on obtaining a better understand of brain tumour research funding in Canada.  Successful student will gain non profit experience and a greater understanding of where funding comes from, in what denominations and trends in funding.  This includes past, present and future understanding of the landscape.  Additionally, exploration will be done of the impacts of funding, if any, due to the COVID-19 pandemic.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Student will be tasked to explore who is funding brain tumour research, where support is obtained, history and future of funding and understanding trends in flow of financial commitment.

Project_Title: Using The Simpsons to Foster Bidialecticalism in an FSL Classroom
Project_Description: The purpose of this study is to examine how the Parisian and Quebec versions of The Simpsons can be used in an FSL classroom to teach both Quebecois and Parisian French.

Project_Title: Varieties of Women’s Movements in Central Asia
Project_Description: The post-Soviet transition has not been easy for women in Central Asia. However, it would be misleading to view women as passive and voiceless victims of the post-Soviet transformation. In fact, women in Central Asia have proven to be resilient to the social and economic pressures and innovative in the strategies used to challenge and counter the men-dominated political and social structures of post-Soviet states. Over time different forms of women’s mobilization emerged in Central Asia, shaped by the distinct experiences of women across Central Asian republics. Some republics such as Kyrgyzstan and Kazakhstan witnessed the emergence of regular socioeconomic protests organized by women activists and the rise of a genuine feminist movement. In contrast, women in Tajikistan and Uzbekistan demonstrated lower levels of socioeconomic and political activism. Several factors, including the differences in the levels of urbanization, lifestyle and economic development, but especially women’s enrolment in tertiary education, political openness and exposure to Western ideas, contributed to different levels and forms of women’s activism in Central Asian republics. This project offers a comparative analysis of women’s movements in four Central Asian republics of Kazakhstan, Kyrgyzstan, Tajikistan and Uzbekistan. Using quantitative and qualitative data, including semi-structured interviews with women activists, and building on women empowerment literature, the paper explores how the changing socioeconomic, cultural and political environment and different institutional mechanisms contributed to different levels of women’s activism across Central Asian republics.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Typical responsibilities of a research assistant in this project will include the following: Collect and systematize data, conduct literature reviews on selected countries, transcribe interviews, assist in the preparation of an article and present the research findings at a conference. Knowledge of the Russian language is desirable.

Project_Title: Victimization and perpetration between heterosexual dating partners: Roles of gender, community cohesiveness, adverse childhood experience, and mental health factors
Project_Description: Despite abundant studies that report the majority of Intimate Partner Violence (IPV) is perpetrated by men against women, other empirical studies suggest that women in heterosexual relationships are just as abusive as men. Gender symmetry argues the comparable numbers of men and women perpetrate and are victimized in their relationships with intimate partners. However, many studies often simply count frequencies/rates of violent acts without considering such crucial factors as mental health, childhood experience, and community support. This study aims to investigate whether gender moderates victimization/perpetration of IPV in relation to such variables as Adverse Childhood Experiences (ACE), Childhood Community Cohesiveness (CCC), Present Community Cohesiveness (PCC), and mental health.

Project_Title: Violence victimization for college students: the asymmetry of LGBTQ students
Project_Description: It is import to understand the whole picture (lifelong) victimization for LGBTQ. They are disproportionately more victimized compared to the general population (male & female). In addition, the victimization is strongly related to the negative outcomes in later life.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: •	Literature review and literature analysis

Project_Title: Visualization and Analysis of COVID-19 Data
Project_Description: This project will collect, curate and present some of the Canadian public data on the covid-19 pandemic. It will examine, visually or analytically, the relationship between hospitalizations, deaths and confirmed number of COVID-19 cases in relationship with other health determinant factors across the Canadian provinces.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
1. Search and obtain the COVID-19 publicly available data sets 
2.Curate the data by using Tableau and other software such as R and analyze to answer the objectives above.

Project_Title: Various Opportunities Involving Research on Autobiographical Memory, Emotion, and Psychological Closure
Project_Description:
This position is best suited to Outstanding Scholars (OSs) who wish to gain experience in planning, conducting, analyzing, writing up, and promoting research centered on how people evaluate and feel about personal life experiences (e.g., events that represent personal change, events evaluated as positive, negative, unresolved, or traumatic), also known as autobiographical memories. My latest research focuses on a construct called psychological closure, or the sense that an event is resolved and no longer bothersome. Specifically, I have examined how using different perspectives when recalling and describing past events (retrieval strategies) influence emotions and appraisals of closure for those events. This research is important given the often long-lasting negative effects unresolved and distressing memories can have on individuals' psychological and emotional well-being and sense of self over time. 

My program of research includes projects at various stages of development, so successful candidates will have the option of choosing opportunities suited to their interests, knowledge, abilities, and goals (these opportunities are listed below). Broadly, OSs are provided with opportunities to learn about research design; quantitative and qualitative methods; lab development, coordination, and promotion; issues related to ethics in human research; and the entire research process from planning to submitting manuscripts for publication. Additionally, OSs may be included on manuscripts if desired and the expectations for authorship are met. 

These activities can all be completed according to the OS's schedule, in keeping with the weekly hour requirement set by the OS Program. Meetings will occur on a weekly basis to review progress and provide one-on-one support, training, and/or mentorship for those interested in pursuing education and training involving research generally, and psychology in particular.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
Conduct literature reviews, read event narratives and identify/code various features of the narratives, conduct statistical tests using SPSS or R, contribute ideas about clinical (psychopathology, psychotherapy) applications of the research, help with research and lab promotion (e.g., write summaries for conferences or online media platforms, design portions of the lab website), assist with organizing/building a lab (e.g., setting up materials and equipment, organizing files, creating manuals), assist with applications for research ethics and funding, coordinate the activities of other research assistants (if available), and possibly help with manuscript preparations and submissions.

Note: Successful candidates may indicate or rank order task preferences from the above list, however, there are certain tasks that may be required. Work duties and tasks will be collaboratively discussed at the start of the position.

Project_Title: Self-Similar Higher-Rank Graphs
Project_Description: Higher-rank graphs are a natural generalization of rank-1 graphs (ie., directed graphs), and have attracted a great deal of attention in these recent years. They provide not only many important and interesting examples of operator algebras, but also have a lot of connections with other research areas. Very recently, we studied self-similar actions of (discrete) groups on higher-rank graphs. These self-similar actions include higher-rank graphs and classical odometers as special examples.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: (1) Do thorough background studies on higher-rank graphs and self-similar actions; (2) find some nice and interesting concrete examples of self-similar actions on higher-rank graphs' (3) analyse some important properties of the examples they find.

Project_Title: Study of Drosophila Cell Division
Project_Description: We are interested in understanding the regulation of cell division in normal and in cancer cells. We use the Drosophila model system to study the effect of gene mutations, gene overexpression, and gene deletions on cell division. This project will focus on the study of genes that are involved in the ubiquitin-mediated degradation of important cell cycle proteins.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: In the first year, the student will learn techniques used in Drosophiila geneatica and molecular biology, and learn basics for maintaining a collection of transgenic and mutant Drosophila strains. The student will also assist senior undergraduates and graduate students in their projects.

Project_Title: Synthetic Research Assistantship
Project_Description: I will be conducting meta-analysis research to expand further on the class paper. This will consist of online research through various databases and meeting electronically with a librarian from the University of Windsor. The is a group research assignment with 8 peers each working on their own individual papers. The purpose is to explore synthetic research at the graduate school level and progressing from beginner to intermediate skill levels of research concepts.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Some of the tasks associated with the positions in making online presentations to the group, connecting virtually with librarians and peers, and studying assigned readings. I will also be required to commit to the process 10 hours per week in the from of reading, writing, and research methods. Another task is to explore online peer-reviewed databases to pull research from.

Project_Title: Synthesis and Characterization of Bispiding Compounds
Project_Description: This research project focuses on the synthesis and characterization of N,N’-diacyl bispidines, a new category of organic compounds which have recently been found to exhibit planar chirality and the capability of substantial chiral-achiral switching.  Our approach involves the synthesis and characterization of bispidine derivatives bearing a range of functional groups. The syn/anti ratio of these compounds will be investigated in solvents of various polarity to explore the steric and electronic factors which may influence the rotation of the chiral planes as well as molecular switching. The results from this investigation will contribute to the fundamental understanding of planar chirality with potential applications in the development of molecular switches and sensors.

Project_Title: Synthesis and Characterization of New Glycosphingolipids
Project_Description: Carbohydrates play essential roles in the immune system’s response. Glycosphingolipids, found in the cellular membrane of all organisms, can be specifically recognized by certain immune system regulators. These moleucles elicit a very powerful immune response and have potential as potent immunostimulatory elements. Many of these moleucles have just been isolated in the last few years and have never been made synthetically. This is essential, as we need more of the material to properly characterize their immunological response. This project is focused on the synthesis of never before made glycolipids, and the testing of these materials for their ability to activate Natural Killer T-Cells. The immunological work will be carried out in collaboration with Dr. Haeryfar at Western.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Synthetic organic chemistry. Research, writing and communication. Senior students will also be given the opportunity for supervision after a year once well established in the lab. Small team collaboration is essential.

Project_Title: Research Project in Musicology
Project_Description: The student will work closely with the professor on locating and assessing primary source materials as well as participating in the editorial process that will lead to the submission of a work for publication.  The student will gain a critical understanding of how to locate a variety of diverse primary source materials, study the function and limitations of these resources and learn bibliographic techniques at a professional level.  (S)he will acquire experience as a critical reader and editor and develop a fluency in managing bibliographic materials, skills that are essential for writers and for graduate school in the humanistic disciplines.

Project_Title: Role of Spy1 in Mammary Gland Development
Project_Description: This student will learn some of the basic techniques required for a cell biology project. The student will run agarose gels, autoclave, maintain the lab, measure protein concentrations, and perform DNA extraction.

Project_Title: Role of SPY1 in High Grade Gliomas
Project_Description: This student will study the effects of Spy1 on initiation progression and relapse of high grade gliomas. The student will study Spy1’s role in stem cell population of the brain.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
-mouse injections
-brain injections
-cell culture
-lab maintenance

Project_Title: Role of Spyl in Hematopoietic Cell Renewal
Project_Description: This student will learn some of the basic techniques required for a cell biology project. The student will prepare materials required for the project and learn the background material behind this project and other projects in the lab.

Project_Title: RELIGION IN THE POST-APOCALYPTIC WORLD OF THE 100
Project_Description: Prepare a paper for presentation focusing on the role of religion in the post-apocalyptic world of The 100, a series currently in its 5th season on the CW network.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
•	Locate and summarize relevant articles
•	Prepare paper outline and abstract proposal
•	Consider appropriate conferences to present paper
•	Review and edit paper drafts in conjuction with supervisor

Project_Title: Research Position in Choral Literature
Project_Description:
The student Choral Scholar will be expected to research and produce the program notes for the UWindsor choral concerts. This research, under the guidance of the Director of Choirs, will comprise general traits of the works, their composers, how these works represent the period of composition or caused change in the period, initial reception of the works, and consequent performances. The research will inform the writing of program notes which will be published in the UWindsor concert programs.

Through hands-on experience the student will also learn the importance of maintaining and managing a choral library, preparing scores for distribution and collection, and ensemble administration.

Project_Title: Research in Composition and Arranging
Project_Description: The student will research composition techniques for large and small jazz ensembles and create original compositions using the methods learned from independent study of prominent composers in the field. In addition, arranging techniques will be applied where applicable in the jazz idiom, especially in large-ensemble compositions.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Study of prominent jazz composers and arrangers, with specific focus on the techniques used; Composition and arrangement of original material following the guidelines of the jazz idiom

Project_Title: Refined Curve Counting on Abelian Surfaces
Project_Description: An abelian surface is roughly a 4-dimensional version of a torus. The computation of the(reduced) primitive class Gromov-Witten invariants of abelian surfaces was one of the first curve counting problems to be solved in more than just finitely many cases (J. Bryan, C. Leung, "Generating functions for the number of curves on abelian surfaces," Duke Math. J. 1999). A natural refinement is to count how many of the stable maps of each genus factorize through a given isogeny of abelian surfaces. This refinement boils down to a combinatorial problem.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: The task is to investigate this combinatorial problem. Most likely, a completely explicit answer is not possible because the Bryan-Leung formula is also a generating function, so part of the problem is finding the right language to express the answer.

Project_Title: Random Matrices
Project_Description: Examine matrices whose entries are randomly generated from some distribution (2g. continuous uniform, normal, discrete Poisson) and compute determinant and eigenvalues using R language. then study the distribution via histograms and determine properties.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Generate random matrices using R, study their properties, and attempt to explain them. The results will be presented in a report using LaTeX.

Project_Title: PSPice and Matlab for Electric & Electronic Circuits
Project_Description: The project serves to improve the learning experience of students studying Electric Circuits and Electronics courses. The project will tackle the behaviour of circuits under transient and steady state  conditions. Several circuits will be considered and will be simulated using PSpice. Matlab code will be developed to accept circuit parameters and provide the status of the transient circuits under considerations.

Project_Title: Publishing Internship with Black Moss Press
Project_Description: The student will develop skills through experiential learning working as a publishing intern for Black Moss Press under the supervision of Marty Gervais. Tasks may include editing and proofreading manuscripts, liasing with authors, market research, social media marketing, event planning, and historical research for future Black Moss Press books. The goal of this project is to provide the student with valuable career development in the publishing industry.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
- Manuscript Editing
- Market Research
- Social Media Marketing
- Historical Research

Project_Title: Promoting an Understanding of Developmental Disabilities through Experiential and Critical Disability Theory Approaches
Project_Description: Medical and social theories have explored challenges faced by individuals with developmental disabilities. Attempts to treat, cure, or manage developmental disabilities reflect a medical model of disability that often resulted in the segregation of individuals into groups that proved counter to the objective of inclusion. The social model of disability serves to point out that societal attitudes and not the disability resulted in limiting the lives of people with disabilities but falls short of including and meeting the particular needs of persons with disabilities. Another approach, critical disability theory exposes how market ideologies or neoliberal influences, supported by social policy, have encouraged us to apply interventions similarly to people with disabilities based on short-term efficacy. Additionally, this project will explore the importance of experiential qualifications when researching persons with disabilities. The objective of this project is to explore both critical disability theory and methods to include experiential opinions of adults with developmental disabilities.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
To research the social model of disability and its limitations
To research critical disability theory 
To explore literature on experiential research by persons with disabilities (community based research)

Project_Title: Pharmacokinetics of new delivery system of antiretrovirals for COVID-19
Project_Description: Past research has shown that a technology called DehydraTECH™, developed by the Canadian company, Lexaria Bioscience (Kelowna, BC), has successfully improved the absorption of fat-soluble drugs throughout the body. This translates into improved efficacy of the particular drug. The degree to which DehydraTECH™ may improve the absorption of the antiviral drugs darunavir and efavirenz - candidate drugs identified to potentially help treat COVID-19 - are still unknown. This study aims to determine whether DehydraTECH™ affects the rate of absorption and overall bloodstream appearance of the orally-ingested antiviral compounds, darunavir and efavirenz.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: The Outstanding Scholar will support the primary investigators by reviewing literature, recruiting participants, drafting and editing research documents,  helping analyze data, contributing to data dissemination, and assisting in writing a manuscript. Additional tasks may be assigned as the project develops.

Project_Title: Perceptions of Pete: Media Comparisons During the Pete Rose Scandal
Project_Description: The project examines different media interpretations of Pete Rose during his betting scandal (and the resulting lifetime ban from Major League Baseball).  Of particular interest is the press coverage of Rose’s hometown Cincinnati Enquirer versus that from other markets in an attempt to discover whether or not (and the degree to which) bias existed.  At the heart of this project are the concepts of media literacy and bias as well as the topical concern of “fake news.”
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
*Reading newspapers
*Synthesis and analysis of materials

Project_Title: Partitioning of Pharmaceuticals to Wastewater Solids
Project_Description: Municipal wastewater effluent discharges and land application of treated biosolids have been identified in the Chemicals Management Plan (CMP) as important sources of chemical substances to the environment.  Some chemical substances, including some pharmaceuticals and personal care products (PPCPs), have been identified as having the potential to cause undesirable effects to non-target organisms in the aquatic and terrestrial environments.  Municipal wastewater treatment plants (WWTPs) are not designed to remove chemical substances such as PPCPs; however previous research in Canada, the United States, and Europe has demonstrated that some substances are degraded and/or partitioned to solids during some wastewater treatment processes.  My research interest is in literature review to better understand/ predict PPCP partitioning to wastewater solids.

Project_Title: Options for a Visual Basic or Web-based Version of an Excel Spreadsheet Model
Project_Description: Most countries require that chemicals which are new to commerce be assessed for their treatability in sewage treatment plants (STPs). This applies particularly to substances that are used domestically and may enter sewer systems. Such an assessment requires a screening-level model of chemical fate in the STP. The model must be relatively simple, transparent and robust and need not simulate conditions in an actual plant in detail since this is the role of more data-intensive simulations. I have a simple Excel spreadsheet model, which is currently used by both Environment Canada and Health Canada.  I would like to explore options for development of a stand-alone Visual Basic or web-based version of the Excel spreadsheet model.

Project_Title: OCT Applications in Solid-State NMR
Project_Description: This project will investigate the spin dynamics of adiabatic pulses and their applications in acquiring spectra for solid-state compounds.  Radiofrequency pulses, which feature modulation of their phase and/or amplitude can be effective in acquiring UW NMR spectra.  The aim of this project will be to use Optimal Control Theory (OCT) to numerically optimize the amplitude and phase profiles of adiabatic rf pulses.  We will investigate their usefulness in exciting, refocusing spin polarization to obtain high-quality UW NMR spectra for both spin ½ and quadrupolar nuclei.   We will compare the effect of these optimized pulses with the WURST pulse and the WURST Echo pulse sequence.   In particular, the nuclei we will be studying are 71Ga, 119Sn and 207Pb, effectively opening up larger swaths of the periodic table for routine NMR investigation.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
•	Using Simpson and Simmol solid-state NMR simulation softwares
•	Using Python to generate Bloch Sphere visualizations that track magnetization trajectories for individual crystallites and powder averagegs
•	Running solid-state NMR experiments on a 9.4T spectrometer

Project_Title: Non-Negative Matrix Factorization Methods For Analyzing Single-Cell Data
Project_Description: Multi-omics technologies provide a unique opportunity to characterize cellular systems at both the spatial and molecular level. While each high-throughput measurement technology can resolve specic biological scales, complementary data integration techniques can reveal multi-scale interactions between modalities. While advances in multi-omics have coincided with the formation of tremendous new data resources and atlas-based initiatives to characterize biological systems, computational techniques and benchmarking strategies to integrate these datasets remains an active area of research. The long-term goal of this research is to devise new methods for integrating multiple omics data sets in order to identify the gene signatures that characterize cell lineages and cell stages, given publicly available mouse gastrulation dataset consisting of single-cell RNA expression and single-cell DNA methylation.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Student tasks consist of devising non-negative martrix factorization algorithms (1) for imputing the missing values in scRNA-seq and scDNA-seq data, and (2) for integrating these two data in order characterize cell lineages and cell stages.

Project_Title: Non-Destructive Testing System, Design Driving Circuitry PCB Board
Project_Description: Non-Destructive Testing (NDT) is a proffered method for fault detection and analysis fields ranging from automotive and welding industries to medical sciences. NDT system commonly use a driving circuitry that combines DC and AC signals to feed the ultrasonic transducers and also a receiving circuit to detect and amplify the received signal. In this project drive in and read out circuit is designed and developed.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Students will participate in design and development of the driving PCB circuit of ultrasonic transducers as well as receiver and amplifier circuit of the NDT system and electronic nose systems. The project is suitable for third or fourth year Electrical Engineering students.

Project_Title: New Organic Dyes For the Study of Biomembranes
Project_Description: Fluorescent organic dyes are widely applied as molecular tags to monitor and investigate biological process and structures down to a sub-micron length scale. They are widely used in the study of biomembranes that are not only essential to the function of cells, or life in general, but also non-living natural structures, such as viruses. Shortcomings of many commercially avaliable membrane dyes is their limited stability and differences in molecular structure to typical membrane molecules. The objective of this project is the development of the first diketopyrrolopyrrole (DPP) based fluorescent tag that mimics the structure of cholesterol, an important component of biomembranes. Studies of the dynamic biomembranes (e.g. as vesicles) by replacing some cholesterol molecules with these DPP dyes will be conducted in collaboration with the Marquardt group.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Students will be involved in the computational design, synthesis, characterization, and application of these dyes and are expected to present their findings at regional and national meetings/conferences. They will work closely together will a graduate student during their first 2 years of training.

Project_Title: Neutral Mechanisms of Tinnitus
Project_Description: Tinnitus is a phantom sensation of sounds in the absence of external acoustic stimulation. Neurobiological research has to be conducted to find the mechanisms responsible for this sensation. Students working on this project are required to locate and review literature, help graduate students in my lab analyze immunohistochemical and neurophysiological results, and help with dissemination of the results.

Project_Title: Neuropsychology-Research on Mind Wandering
Project_Description: The student will work as assistant to a MA clinical neuropsychology candidate. The student will learn about using neuropsychological devices for mind-wandering. They will observe during experiments and take notes.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
1.	Help set up neuropsych devices for experiments
2.	Be responsible for securing items during experiments 
3.	Student will keep a journal on what they have learned about brain functioning 
4.	Student will learn about the parts of the brain involved in mind-wandering 
5.	Student will perform some data entry

Project_Title: Nanadagikenim: Incorporating Indigenous Ways of Knowing (IWK} and Pedagogy
Project_Description:
The study is a pilot project that was delivered and evaluated within the SWAK 3390:
Theory and Practice of Social Work with Communities and Organizations course. The
study is a quasi-experimental design based on a two-eyed seeing approach: Students
completed a pre- and post-evaluation survey measuring student satisfaction; students
additionally completed a post-evaluation qualitative self-reflection based on the
medicine wheel model. The delivery of the course content and physical space of the
classroom was set up to reflect Indigenous values of interpersonal connection,
collaboration and interdependence. In support of fostering Indigenous Ways of Knowing,
an Elder/Knowledge Holder provided consultation and collaboration regarding the
overall design of the curriculum, as well as providing opening and closing ceremonies.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
The Outstanding Scholar will be involved in data entry, analysis and interpretation. The
Outstanding Scholar will receive ongoing mentorsh1p throughout the 2020-2021 academic
year. This position is suitable for any Outstanding Scholar, regardless of department, who is
interested in exploring the impact of incorporating Indigenous Ways of Knowing and pedagogy

Project_Title: Natural Products and Nanoemulsions
Project_Description: Natural products are often poorly soluble in water. This can be a problem when we want to use them in a pharmaceutical, food, or nutraceutical application. To overcome this challenge, we can formulate them by putting them into particles, micelles or vesicles, which, like little soap bubbles or oil droplets in salad dressing, can dissolve hydrophobic molecules while keeping everything in solution. However, unlike either of those examples, we want the droplets to be very small, so small that they are invisible. Ideally 10-200 nm. This project involves helping us formulate various natural products using new materials and using new technologies.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Analytical chemistry, synthetic chemisty, nanoformulation science. Research, writing and communication. Senior students will also be given the opportunity for supervision after a year once well established in the lab. Small team collaboration is essential.

Project_Title: Molecular mechanisms of skeletal muscle regeneration
Project_Description: Skeletal muscle has a remarkable capacity to regenerate following injury, but this process is regulated by numerous cellular and molecular mechanisms.  The lipid Sphingosine-1-Phosphate (S1P) is a positive regulator of muscle regeneration, but appears to be absent from the muscle of diabetic mice during regeneration which, incidentally, occurs very slowly in diabetic rodents.  Further studies using diabetic mice need to be performed to understand why S1P does not appear in the post-injury state in skeletal muscle.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: The appointed student will assist a graduate student in handling mice at the animal facility (Essex building), running a variety of lab experiments in the Tissue Lab (rm 221 HK building), and analysing microscope images.  Prior to those tasks, the student will be required to acquire the relevant safety training certifications (WHMIS, biosafety, animal handling training, etc).  With various covid19 related shutdowns, emphasis of tasks in the early months will be on image/data analysis that can be performed on the student’s personal computer without in-person contact.

Project_Title: Molecular Mechanism of Toxoplasma Pathogenesis
Project_Description:
Toxoplasma gondii is an obligatory intracellular parasite of mammals and birds. In 1995 the contaminated municipal water supply in British Columbia was reported to be associated with Toxoplasma, and it was the cause of an infectious outbreak. It has been estimated that approximately one third of North Americans are infected.

Our research group sets out to investigate the molecular mechanism of the parasite gene regulation network. We aim to understand how the parasite can escape an immune response and cause a recurring infection in humans. We use (i) cell culture techniques for maintaining the parasite for our study, (ii) DNA recombinant techniques to engineer various molecular tools (microRNAs, ribozymes, and plasmids) to control Toxoplasma gene expression, and (iii) pharmacological techniques to aid our search for a better anti-parasitic agent.

Project_Title: Molecular Analysis of Toxoplasma Gondii Infection
Project_Description: Toxoplasma gondii is an obligate intracellular parasite and opportunistic pathogen of the phylum Apicomplexa. This unicellular organism is capable of infecting and replicating within virtually any nucleated mammalian organism, making it an excellent model for the study of intracellular parasitism. Our research program is aimed to better understand molecular mechanism(s) critical to T. gondii infection. We utilize molecular biology, biochemistry and bioinformatic approaches to alter the parasite cell biology and infection pattern.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Students will work with graduate students to learn and use laboratory techniques (DNA cloning, plasmid construction, cell cultures, etc) in directed research projects.

Project_Title: Mitigating Distracted Driving based on Understanding of Driver Characteristics
Project_Description: This project will investigate how driver characteristics are associated with distracted driving in the events which require high level of drivers' attention. Driver distraction will be observed in virtual driving environments using a driving simulator. Characteristics of the drivers who test the driving simulator scenarios will also be collected using the survey and related to their driving performance. Based on the findings, effective strategies to mitigate distracted driving will be developed.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: review the literature on distracted driving and develop new research ideas. The student will also design the driving simulator scenarios which may cause driver distraction and identify driving performance parameters that reflect distracted driving.

Project_Title: Mental Health Outcomes of Community-based Male Survivor Services: A Comparison of Individual, Group, and Conjoint Treatment
Project_Description: Due to an historical failure to acknowledge the needs for adult male survivors of childhood sexual abuse (CSA), there is limited understanding and evidence-based knowledge of the psychosocial impact of sexual victimization of males and the therapeutic interventions that are effective for male survivors who seek help. This evaluation study investigates the effectiveness of three treatment modalities: individual, group, and a combination of individual and group treatments.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
•	Literature review/analysis
•	Tasks to find out the information related to male survivors programs in Ontario

Project_Title: Measuring and modelling pedestrian-induced vibrations
Project_Description: Vibrations due to pedestrian footfalls are often the governing source of vibration on the upper floors of structures. These vibrations may cause discomfort for occupants, or interfere with the operation of sensitive equipment such as in research and healthcare facilities. Design guidelines assist structural engineers in achieving vibration performance objectives. These guidelines, however, may be conservative and do not account for the probability that a pedestrian’s walking will generate large vibrations. Statistical analysis is to be applied to compare the long-term vibration performance of a floor against existing methods of predicting the floor response due to pedestrian-induced vibrations.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: You will be tasked with completing an experimental measurement program, modelling the measurement area using structural analysis software, and conducting a statistical analysis to determine how well the vibration predictions represent the long-term measurements.

Project_Title: Materials & Sensitive Polymers for Medical and Environmental Sensing Applications
Project_Description: Biomedical and environmental monitoring sensors often use sensitive material to various volatiles, e.g. concentration of certain element in a patient with cardiovascular disease or a certain gas release in an environment. In this project, students investigate various  sensitive materials and their deposition techniques that can be used in medical and environmental sensor systems. They will conduct literature review, study their property changes when exposed to different elements and investigate techniques to deposit them onto medical and environmental sensors. An interest in material science or chemistry is an asset.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Locate and review literature, describe and discuss sensing material properties and sensitivity for various target gases, apply the discipline’s theories, techniques and methods, contribute to the analysis of a significant research problem, and contribute to the production of a significant research finding

Project_Title: Mathematics of Signal Processing
Project_Description:
Signal Processing is a branch of electrical engineering which is connected to several areas in mathematics. 

Groups, Lie algebras, representation theory, Hilbert spaces and partial differential equations have many applications in signal procesing.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: In this project, the students will explore some of these applications with mathematical approaches mentioned above. Each student will focus on either algebraic parts: groups and Lie algebras or analysis: Hilbert spaces and differential equation. This is related to parts of my current ongoing research projects.

Project_Title: Mapping the “Right to Disconnect” Movement
Project_Description: The “right to disconnect” is a phrase being used by workers, unions, and policymakers around the world. Although causes and contexts vary, the overarching goal of this movement is to re-assert boundaries around the working day in an age defined by “always on” connectivity, including connectivity with employers. This project will map the rise of this international movement in order to better understand the disparate reasons and contexts that animate the fight for a “right to disconnect.” There is little existing critical research on the topic at the moment and we hope to get a more nuanced understanding of the movement in a broader international context.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: The research activities involve creating literature reviews about the right to disconnect movement; analyzing policy document and labour reports; managing bibliographic resources.

Project_Title: Manuscript support
Project_Description: A number of manuscript are planned for the F2020 semester covering various topics, including performance validity assessment, cross-validation of tests, and learning effects. This project provides a research-based context for learning some key concepts related to psychological testing and measurement. It also offers an opportunity to learn and participate in the manuscript writing process in preparation for writing an undergraduate thesis and preparing manuscripts for publication.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: part of a team, students will learn some of the key concepts involved in the targeted research topics. They will further learn the process involved in conducting lit reviews and populating reference sections using APA 7 formatting. Students will then prepare and submit lit reviews and reference sections for review and incorporate feedback into subsequent tasks.

Project_Title: Male Survivors of Childhood Sexual Abuse
Project_Description:
The proposed study will contribute to the advancement of knowledge both inside and outside of
academia, including understanding theories, nature/scope of childhood sexual abuse, factors
related to successful recovery, and complex issues related to treatment/intervention for male
survivors of childhood sexual abuse. The effectiveness of programs to protect survivors of
childhood sexual abuse depends on evidence-based clinical services, quality local resources, and
inter-agency relationships in the community. Along with the evaluation of the effectiveness of
the counseling modalities (individual, group, E-counseling), frontline service workers and
government agencies can better understand the need for the services and maximize their efforts
(in both practice and policies) for helping male survivors of childhood sexual abuse.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Research Assistants will conduct literature reviews on the theory and practice on the male survivors of childhood sexual abuse, and complete data analyses and entry screening. They will have the opportunity to collaborate with the Windsor-Essex Sexual Assault Crisis Centre. Additionally, they will work towards developing an application for an external research grant for the Social Science and Humanities Research Council (SSHRC) for a research outcome and knowledge transfer event.

Project_Title: Manuscript Research: Historical Dictionary of Lebanon
