Project_Description: Dr.Tom Najem and I are co-authoring a book manuscript titled Historical Dictionary of Lebanon.This is a reference book and is part of a series of such books on various countries. Although the title is broad,the focus of the book (and series) is on history and politics, but cultural topics such as music, film, literature, and art are also covered. The book has three main parts:a long "Introduction" that serves as an general overview of Lebanon's history,politics and society; a main "Dictionary" section with entries on specific topics arranged alphabetically,and a large "Bibliography" subdivided by topic. The completed book manuscript needs to be submitted by the end of the Fall 2016 term.

Project_Title: Long term trend of air quality in Canada
Project_Description:
This project will investigate the air quality and emission trends for particulate matter, volatile organic compounds, ozone and mercury in southern Ontario and other Canadian cities in the past 15 years, this study will provide useful methodology that could be used in other places to evaluate the effectiveness of emission control measures.  Such assessment will aid development of future emission reduction policy.

Requirement:The project requires students from natural sciences (e.g. environmental sciences)
or engineering programs.

Other comments:In the past 12 years, my lab has hosted 16 Outstanding Scholars, co-op students, Globalink students,and NSERC- Undergraduate Student Research Awards; and the experience has been very positive. I am looking forward to working with Outstanding Scholars in the coming terms.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Students will be working at the Air Quality Lab in CEI. Students will need to perform a literature review, operate air quality instruments, perform data collection or download data from websites, analyse data, and interpret the results. Students are required to describe their research work and results in a technical report and submit it at the end of the term.

Project_Title: Learning Effects & Experimental Malingering Study - Research Assistant (RA)
Project_Description:
Formerly conducted in-lab, this study involving administration of neuropsychological tests has
been adapted for remote on screen assessment. A second experimental condition has also
been added to compare motivational dynamics on assessment outcomes. As such, a back-up
RA is required to assure that the primary investigator conducting the assessments remains
blinded to the assignment of examinees to the control and experimental groups. However, the
OS project primarily involves assisting with scoring of tests and data entry. The RA will also
assist with manuscripts being generated from the dataset acquired from the in-lab phase of the
study.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
The student will act in a back-up capacity to another RA tasked with managing the scheduling
of appointments and awarding of compensation for examinees entering through the participant
pool and the alternate pathway. Primary duties will include assisting with scoring, data entry, lit
reviews, and populating reference sections in manuscripts.

Project_Title: Lawlessness, Treaty Amnesia, and Nêhiyaw Sovereignty: The (dis)Honour of the Crown in Treaty 6
Project_Description:
This project examines the ways in which the Crown has derrogated its duty to honour its
obligations under Treaty 6 with respect to Nêhiyaw (Cree) people in what is now called
Saskatchewan. Examining the transcripts of the trial of Sylvia and Kurtis Saysewahum,
charged with trespassing on their family’s ancestral territory, the authors explore the ways that
the trial represents larger themes of the honour (or dishonour) of the Crown, Indigenous
sovereignty, and lawlessness in the context of Indigenous and Crown treaty relationships
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
The student will be asked to conduct a comprehensive literature and case law review on the
followings issues: s.35 of the Charter, policing, and police funding, Indigenous scholarship,
and post-colonial scholarship.

Project_Title: Laser Spectroscopy Experiments for Rapid Pathogen Diagnosis
Project_Description: Our lab utilizes a technique known as “laser-induced breakdown spectroscopy” where a powerful pulse of laser light atomizes targets in a high-temperature spark and the light given off by the spark is used to measure elemental composition.  In this project, students will assist in the measurement of the elemental composition of biomedical targets (including bacterial cells) to provide ultra-rapid diagnosis of infections in human specimens.  Students will work as part of a research group, and will be each put in charge of a specific project for which they will be responsible, with Dr. Rehse as the PI. Group interaction with the other students on the project will be important and necessary.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Students will firstly learn to work safely in a laboratory environment and will be required to participate in all the necessary laser safety training and biosafety level 2 training.  Students will engage in a variety of tasks including operating the lasers and understanding their operation.  Students will be responsible for sample preparation and spectroscopic data analysis.

Project_Title: Investigating the Spin-Physics 2H SSNMR Powder Patterns
Project_Description: Solid-state NMR (SSNMR) experiments on integer spin nuclei (i.e., I = 1) yield powder patterns that often appear as “Pake doublets”. This appearance is due to (i) the dominant contributions of the first-order quadrupolar interaction, and (ii) overlapping patterns from the two single-quantum ( +1 ↔  0 and 0 ↔ −1 ) transitions. 2H SSNMR powder patterns can be as broad as 350 kHz, resulting in: (1) low signal-to-noise (S/N), (2) poor spectral resolution, and (3) spectral distortions when using conventional rectangular pulses to acquire these spectra. (1) and (2) have been addressed, in part, by using either quadrupolar-echo or CPMG pulse sequences with magic-angle spinning (MAS); however, these issues have not been soundly addressed for experiments on stationary (static) samples. This project will investigate the use of frequency-swept (FS) pulses that can uniformly excite and refocus magnetization have permitted the acquisition of ultra-wideline (UW) NMR spectra.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
-	Conduct Solid-State NMR (SSNMR) experiments
-	Utilize the open-source software SIMPSON to simulate SSNMR experiments
-	Provide data analysis and communicate findings within team environment
-	Collaborate with colleagues to apply pulse sequence designs to assist in the investigation of different solid-state compounds

Project_Title: Intelligent Computing Research
Project_Description: The goal of this project is to investigate the theories, models, algorithms, and design methodologies of intelligent computing to mimic human-like reasoning, learning, and memory. The project will require the student to investigate traditional and new intelligent computing techniques, examine practical implementations, and consider how intelligent computing principles can be used to solve real-world problems.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
-	Development understanding of intelligent computing theories and algorithms
-	Review and comprehend current research in intelligent computing
-	Use intelligent computing principles to solve practical problems

Project_Title: Individual differences and personality
Project_Description: This project is designed to allow a relative novice student (2nd year) to get an introduction to the scientific process of personality and individual differences research. The goal is to take the Outstanding Scholar from the point of understanding only the most basic and concrete information about the research process in the field of psychology to the point of actively participating in designing a study. There will also be opportunities to gather the applicable literature, assist with data collection, enter data, collaborate on ethics issues, and work on manuscripts with other students.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: In the first semester, the Outstanding Scholar will largely be engaged in the process of learning about different ongoing projects in our research group that includes a faculty member, 3 PhD students, 1 MA student, 2 post-BA students, an honours thesis student, and 2 research assistants who will be completing honours theses next year. This learning will happen largely in lab meetings and by reading circulated documents. As the Outstanding Scholar becomes more experienced, they will be asked to enter data and then assist with data collection. By the end of the second semester and into the summer (if there is interest from the student), they will be part of designing a study.

Project_Title: Income Inequality and Climate Change
Project_Description: “Income Inequality and Climate Change,” our objective is to develop the analytical framework to study the distributional effects of climate change. Our proposed framework will allow for the analysis of the effects of weather shocks and climate change among different types of agents. The first type of agent, we call the entrepreneur or capitalist, owns physical capital and combines it with labour, and natural resources to produce the economy’s final output. The second type of agent, the hand-to-mouth agent, supply labour to the production sector, do not own any physical capital. Our departure from the standard single-agent model provides the necessary structure to study the heterogeneous impacts of climate change.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
•	Review basic real business cycle model
•	Solve and check derivations
•	Literature review – Income Inequality and Climate Change
•	Writing up the model and solutions in Latex

Project_Title: Impact Assessment on Students Using the Collegiate Recovery Program at the University of Windsor
Project_Description: In recent years, the number of recovery programs on college campuses has increased dramatically in the United States, but the University of Windsor was only the second school program to be established in Canada. For students in recovery, successfully balancing the demands of school, work, social life, and a recovery program is a life-threatening issue; having a peer group to share these challenges with is critical to success. This project is the second phase in the development of a recovery program on the University of Windsor campus, assessing student needs, improving the program, and collecting data for ongoing research.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Research assistant will conduct literature reviews, assist with the collection of data, search for grant funding, review data on college students in recovery, prepare a poster for presentation at a conference, and potentially assist on a manuscript. The student will also be expected to facilitate group meetings, assist with participant acquisition, communicate with stakeholders, disseminate information to members, and manage files on Teams and website.

Project_Title: Image processing in Medical Physics
Project_Description:
Imaging processing is dealt with in Medical Physics for medical tools.

Mathematical operations such as convolutions will be investigated. This will be used in context for concepts such as convolution and correlation for images in medical tools.

Project_Title: Higher Cognitive Abilities in Rats Over Their Normal Life-Span
Project_Description: Most undergraduate students learn that so-called 'lower' animals are capable of learning very basic' simple associations' through Pavlovian or instrumental conditioning. However, they often mistakenly consider that only higher' animals (humans and maybe great apes or at least one great grey parrot, Alex) are capable of higher cognitive abilities. Current research in comparative cognition has all but destroyed this myth by showing that even rodents and other birds are capable of some remarkable cognitive abilies requiring complex human-like memory processes and reasoning. Some may argue that we primates are still superior because we can accomplish such problem solving more easily and accurately than other animals, However, those of us who study the ecology of organism are beginning to show that when we attend to an animal's appropriate ecology; that is, its species' naturally  evolved lifestyle, and attempt to approximate it in our laboratory settings and tasks, then even such animals like rats also can easily acquire complex cognitive tasks. Students on my laboratory will be trained to use these techniques to study domesticated (hooded) rats' ability to retain and retrieve information from their working memory about objects in their environment during their natural foraging activities for food located at or near them. Moreover, they will examine rats' changes in their sophistication and possible decline in processing such information over their natural life-span from an early to late adult hood (age 75 -500 days). Not only will students be introduced and become proficient in using our state of the art behavioural monitoring and analyeis techniques (e.g., Noldos, Etho-Vision digital recording program), but will be involved in formulating and testing hypotheses concerning basic underlying memory processes, prepare reports for PPT presentations at annual conferences (e.g., Tri-State, Comparative Cognition Society Conference) and for eventual publication in well respected, high impact journals in this area.  Of equal value will be their exposure to long-term humane interaction with another species without compromising rigorous scientific evaluation of its behaviour. I am appending two samples ofthe kind of research previous undergraduate students, some of whom would probably have qualified for the OS program, had conducted under my supervision and mentorship during their final two or three years of their undergraduate careers at our university.	Please note, one, Xue, obtained a doctorate at McMaster  U and is now professor  in a university China, and two, Arain and Parameswaran, received M.Sc. from U Windsor, and have become research coordinators at U of Calgary and Wisconsin, respectively.

Project_Title: High-Tech Sponges: Drug Release Materials Based on Metal Organic Frameworks
Project_Description:
Does the idea of being the first person to develop a new material with interesting properties excite you? Are you interested in material science, inorganic chemistry, pharmaceuticals, and structure-property relationships? Then this VukGroup research project may be for you! 

Metal-organic frameworks (MOFs) are a new class of porous materials which can be described as high-tech programmable sponges. MOFs are the highest surface area materials in the world, and through molecular design you can program how these materials uptake and release molecules. This research project is centred on developing MOFs which directly incorporate pharmaceuticals within the materials and monitoring their drug release rates. This work has the potential to lead to better medications and improve the delivery of chemotherapy drugs, anti-inflammatory agents, antibiotics, etc.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Students involved in this project will learn metal-coordination chemistry and how to synthesize metal-organic frameworks via solvothermal and mechanochemical (grinding) methods. Students will also learn how to properly characterize these crystalline materials via spectroscopic methods and instrumental techniques such as powder X-ray diffraction (XRD). Students will also get the opportunity to use a high-throughput solvent dispensing robot and utilize an intrinsic dissolution system commonly used to monitor the release rates of drugs in the pharmaceutical industry.

Project_Title: Human Factors of Driving
Project_Description: The purpose of this work is to examine the effect that automated driving has on driver status and cognitive load. This research falls within the field of Human Factors and Cognitive Ergonomics, and investigates the role of the human driver in their interaction with automated vehicles.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
•	Lead data collection under my supervision
•	Program driving simulator
•	Contribute to developing experimental design
•	Contribute to analyzing data
•	Contribute to writing research publication

Project_Title: Health System Performance Based on HQO Data: What is the Impact of COVID-19
Project_Description: This project will use publicly available data from Health Quality Ontario to examine the impact of COVID-19 on some of the health quality measures such as hospital-acquired infections.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
1. Extract relevant data from HQO's public databases and curate them
2. Visualize and analyze the data to examine the impact of covid-19 on system performance measures

Project_Title: Hardware Security against Side Channel Attacks
Project_Description:
The undergraduate student will participate in a project with a Post-Doctoral Fellow and another graduate student. The undergraduate student will assist in programming FPGA boards, preparing database, and test measurement of ICs under security threats.
The undergraduate student may have to go through a learning curve to become familiar with analog circuit design main features, and data encryption algorithms.

Project_Title: Head-Up Display for Automotive Operators
Project_Description: Development of “Google-like” glasses to aid in overhead work of automotive operators. Overhead work increases the risk of injury to automotive assembly operators. Attaching a small camera to a power tool will allow operators to view the target without extension of the neck. The camera view will be sent to a small view finder that is attached to normal reading-like glasses. This will allow people to see what the tool views and cause less neck extension. A laboratory study that is designed to simulate overhead work will be conducted with and without the camera-like glasses technology. EMG and whole-body kinematics will be collected to determine if less neck extension along with lower EMG activity is associated with using the camera-like glasses.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Development of process and collection of EMG and whole-body kinematics data with and without camera-like glasses.

Project_Title: Greenhouse Solar Energy Systems to Reduce Carbon and Grid Dependency
Project_Description: Examine the technical and economic feasibility of a novel solar energy system to reduce dependancy on carbon-based fuels and the grid. This project will included solar thermal and solar photovoltaic collectors, and will be based around Leamington greenhouses.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: The student will assist the professor and Gas with the project.

Project_Title: Glycosylation with Non-Protic Nucleophiles
Project_Description: Glycosylation, the chemistry of adding sugars to things, is essential for the study of glycomics. Carbohydrates play key roles in almost all biological interactions, cellular signalings, and receptor mediated processes. They are how cells talk to one another. Most sugars have oxygens attached, but in our work to develop new types of stable carbohydrates that last longer in the body, we are seeking new methods and understandings of how we can add carbon instead. The solvent used for these reactions has a huge effect on the outcome, and the result appears, from preliminary reports from our laboratory, to be flipped in the case of non-protic nucleophiles (ie carbon) compared with oxygen, sulfur and nitrogen nucleophiles. Cool eh? This project will involve the synthesis of a number of carbohydrates, and their glycosylation with a series of different nucleophiles to increase our fundamental understanding of carbohydrate chemistry.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Synthetic organic chemistry. Kinetic measurements by NMR and GC-MS, determination of energy differences. Research, writing and communication. Senior students will also be given the opportunity for supervision after a year once well established in the lab. Small team collaboration is essential.

Project_Title: Genetic Analysis of Drosophila Cell Cycle
Project_Description: Student will Maintain Lab Stocks, Set up Crosses, and Conduct Protein Biochemistry.

Project_Title: Frequency of cell phone use, driving behavior, and driving attitudes of university-aged drivers
Project_Description: This project allows  for a student to investigate cell phone use and behaviour while driving, in a university-aged population. A survey was conducted regarding frequency of cell phone use, behaviours and attitudes while driving; and the data needs to be examined. Survey questions included the estimated percentage of time engaged in distracted driving (talking on the phone, reading text messages, and sending text messages). The survey also examined 1) the circumstances surrounding use, 2) how driving ability was affected, 3) the emotions felt, and 4) the perceived danger of the participants.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: The ideal student would be capable of inputting and coding these data into excel and SPSS, running statistics on the data, sourcing current relevant scholarly articles, and aiding in manuscript writing. The ultimate goal of this project would be a manuscript submission, which would allow the student to be a named author on a publication. THESE TASKS CAN BE ACCOMPLISHED ONLINE, VIA EMAIL AND VIRTUAL MEETINGS.

Project_Title: Fluorine Chemistry is Fun!: Designer Methods for Alkane-Derived Fluroorganics
Project_Description: Fluorine is ubiquitous in the sustainability and materials sectors (as components of fuel/solar cells and in liquid crystal displays, for example). Consequently, these past few decades have witnessed an increasing number of methodologies aimed at the precise insertion of carbon-fluorine (C-F) functionality into organic substrates. However, examples of C(sp³)-H fluorination are often limited to C-H bonds adjacent electron withdrawing groups and to date, only a handful of enantioselective C(sp³)-H fluorination protocols have been realized.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
The student in this role will work alongside the primary investigator, fellow undergraduate, and graduate students to build new molecules for use in chemical reaction development (synthesis). These new species will be characterized by a suite of spectroscopic tools aimed at precisely understanding structure (how atoms are connected to one another) at the molecular level. This project targets the development of molecules that are relevant to the installation of carbon-fluorine bonds, which are present in some pharmaceuticals, for example.  

The student in this role will work in a synthetic chemistry laboratory equipped with state-of-the-art equipment and facilities – all training will be received as necessary (previous experience is not required). The student will work with air/moisture sensitive compounds employing a hands-on, problem-solving-based approach toward managing and troubleshooting problems in research and discovery. Communication skills and teamwork will be emphasized.

Project_Title: Flow Measurement Techniques in a Wind Tunnel
Project_Description: A wind tunnel is an important research tool in the field of wind engineering. It is used extensively to study various wind-related structural and nvironmental issues accurring within the atmosphereic boundary layer, of which the characteristics of the approaching wind could be considerably affected by the terrain conditions. Therefore, when studying these issues in the wind tunnel, it is of utmost importance to faithfully reproduce the terrrain conditions at the site. This is usually achieved by setting up passive devices in the upstream of the wind tunnel test section, and the spire-roughness-element technology is the most widely-used approach. In the current study, this technology will be applied to simulate the atmospheric boundary layer in a new wind tunnel facility at the University of Windsor. The student will be assisting a master’s degree student to conduct the simulation in the wind tunnel, and will help in the experimental set-up, the data collection, and the data analysis.

Project_Title: Film Festival Research and Strategy
Project_Description: The Outstanding Scholar will have the opportunity to shadow and conduct secondary research on the programming, planning and development of WIFF.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
-	Data Organizing		-  List / Chart Assembly
-	Cataloguing		-  Information Searching
-	Fact Finding

Project_Title: Floating Offshore Plant Factory
Project_Description: A floating offshore plant factory (FOPF) will be tested at model scale against wind and wave inputs at Western’s Wind EEE Dome. Collected information will be reported and synthesized for a journal manuscript.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
•	Design experiments for FOPF testing at Wind EEE Dome
•	Conduct Wind EEE Dome experiment campaign for FOPF
•	Collect and organize data for analysis
•	Synthesize and develop journal manuscript

Project_Title: Experimental techniques in hydraulic engineering
Project_Description: A position in a fluid mechanics lab is available for a third- or fourth-year undergraduate student in CEE or MAME. The specific area of research involves fluid-structure interaction with scour/erosion. Students will learn relevant experimental and measurement techniques under the guidance of experienced graduate students. The position is intended to provide students with lab experience, with the potential for extending into a Capstone project using the lab facilities.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Duties will include assisting graduate students with velocity and erosion measurements, and general lab maintenance as required.

Project_Title: Examining Indigenous women's experiences of lateral violence
Project_Description:
Lateral violence is a cycle of abuse and violence that originates from colonization, and the
resulting oppression and inter-generational trauma, as well as from ongoing experiences of
racism and discrimination (NWAC, 2015). To date, there is scant research on lateral violence
within Indigenous communities in Canada, and no known culturally informed and relevant
measure of lateral violence for use with Indigenous communities. Indigenous women attending
UWindsor will be interviewed to learn about their direct or vicarious experiences of lateral
violence. Gathering data begins by asking participants a broad question: "Tell me about your
experiences of lateral violence and what that looks like." The findings of this research will
help to identify and conceptualize incidents of lateral violence and will inform the development
of a specific measurement that is culturally informed, sensitive and relevant to Indigenous
communities working to recover from intergenerational trauma.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
The Outstanding Scholar will be involved in the interview process, data collection and
conceptualizing incidents of lateral violence. The Outstanding Scholar will receive ongoing
mentorship throughout the 2020-2021 academic year. This position is suitable for any
Outstanding Scholar, regardless of department, who is interested in exploring Indigenous
women's experiences of lateral violence.

Project_Title: Evaluation of Novel Curcumin Analogues for Anti-Cancer Activity
Project_Description:
Student will screen curcumin analogues for anti-cancer activity in leukemia cells with viability assays.
Fluorescent microscopy will be used to monitor cell death markers.
Western blot analysis will be used to monitor specific protein markers.

Project_Title: Evaluating Anti-Cancer Effects of Long Pepper
Project_Description: The project is aimed at evaluating the anticancer activities of alcohol extracts of long pepper in human neuroblastoma and glio-blastoma cells in terms of selective induction of apoptosis in cancer cells. This project will required various lab techniques including tissue culture, microscopy, biochemical and morphological and enzymatic assays, fluorescent staining and immune-fluorescent assays.

Project_Title: English Language, Literature and Creative Writing
Project_Description: ‘English Souls’ is the first chapter of a monograph, under contract with Oxford University Press, Therapy and the Passions: Rhetoric, Medicine, and Moral Philosophy in Early Modern England, in which I explore the contributions of English physicians and philosophers to contemporary conceptions of the soul. The OS student will assist with identifying, gathering, and reading relevant primary and secondary sources for this chapter and others. She/he will become familiar with the appropriate databases, early modern intellectual history, and sixteenth- and seventeenth-century medical history. Ideally, some experience in the period, or on other research projects, is preferred.

Project_Title: Embodied Humanism: Sensuous Enjoyment and the Human Good
Project_Description: The book will argue that the sensuous enjoyment of life is a fundamental aspect of good lives. We are born and die, our lives involve challenge and struggle, but we need not pine for an afterlife.  Life can be good on its own terms when we have organized our societies so as to ensure that everyone is furnished with what they need, have opportunities for meaningful contribution, and the time to enjoy the beauty of nature, mutualistic relationships with others, and the full range of sensuously enjoyable experiences the universe makes available.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: The book will argue that the sensuous enjoyment of life is a fundamental aspect of good lives. We are born and die, our lives involve challenge and struggle, but we need not pine for an afterlife.  Life can be good on its own terms when we have organized our societies so as to ensure that everyone is furnished with what they need, have opportunities for meaningful contribution, and the time to enjoy the beauty of nature, mutualistic relationships with others, and the full range of sensuously enjoyable experiences the universe makes available.

Project_Title: Electrical and Computer Engineering
Project_Description: The project will use the Simulink tool to model and implement some advanced digital communication receivers that are used in wireless systems. This will provide a useful tool that can be used by our undergraduate and graduate students.

Project_Title: Electric Vehicle Powertrain Study
Project_Description: In this project, the student will gain an understanding of electric traction motors through assisting Masters and PhD students in testing electric motors and inverters for vehicular applications. Student will assist in test data analysis obtained for different operating conditions and various cooling conditions for the motor and inverter. Project_Title: EFFECT OF COGNITIVE LOAD ON PHYSICAL PERFORMANCE IN ERGONOMICS
Project_Description: The purpose of this work is to examine the effect that cognitive load has on physical performance of manufacturing workers. Research suggests that increasing levels of physical task demand increase cognitive control required by the worker to complete a given activity. In this research, we will examine how levels of cognitive load will interact with physical task load to impact accuracy and completion time of manufacturing tasks.

Project_Title: Effects of Natural Extracts on Cancer Cells
Project_Description: My lab’s research focus is on investigating the efficacies and mechanism of action of purified natural compounds, their analogues, and several natural extracts in terms of selective induction of apoptosis in cancer cells. General techniques: cell culture, biochemical and morphological assays; microscopy; enzymetic assays; fluorescence staining; and immunoblot and immunofluorescence analysis. Other methods include mitochondrial targeting in glioblastoma cells by synthetic PST analogues; inducing cell death by combretastatin C4 analogues and by targeting metabolic and mitochondrial vulnerability of cancer cells; and apoptosis induction in human cancer cells by water and ethanol extract of Tulasi and Neem. The study focuses on the anti‐cancer properties of Dandelion root extract and long pepper extract, and the effects of natural extracts from turmeric, green tea, white tea, gooseberry on human lymphoma cells.

Project_Title: eDNA Analysis of Non-Indigenous and Indigenous Fishes in South African Rivers
Project_Description: Environmental DNA (eDNA) is a nascent, yet extremely promising, tool to monitor aquatic biodiversity with particular utility to survey rare, hard-to-find, endangered and invasive species. The coastal riverine ecosystems of South Africa have suffered negative population-level consequences to its indigenous fish fauna from repeated introductions of non-indigenous species from alien territories, resulting in the contraction of indigenous distributions. Taxon-specific genetic markers will be developed to determine the distribution of both the invading and native species in order to provide baseline distribution data upon which managers and conservationists can act.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: eDNA samples from the field have been collected. The project will provide skills in the design, testing (in silico and in situ), optimisation and analysis of PCR-based molecular genetic assays based on non-whole organism derived DNA (eDNA) extracted from suspended material located in the water column.

Project_Title: Economic Research Tools and Their Application to International Trade
Project_Description: The student will learn first how to use economic literature search engines such as EconLit and Social Science Citation Index. S/he will use them for particular literature search related to the topic of cross-border trade at urban and state/provincial level, studying the effects of trade barriers on the volume of trade between two cities or states/provinces  separated by international border ( the leading examples being Windsor and Detroit as well as Ontario and Michigan).. The second stage will involve finding general survey articles and summarizing them. Next, the student will learn how to build a simple economic model involving few equations. In order to find economic equilibrium, the use of powerful software such as Matlab and Maple is needed. The student will learn simplest commands from these tools to solve the model built. The results will be compared to the findings in the literature. Finally, the student will be asked to critically review the model in light of the findings by other scholars. If time permits, an exploration of economic data and their sources will also be made.

Project_Title: Developing Novel DC Coils for MRI
Project_Description: DC (direct current) coils are required in Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) to improve static magnetic field homogeneity and encode spatial information. Non-traditional coils will provide new opportunities for signal encoding. This project aims to design and construct novel DC coils for our portable magnetic resonance system.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Optimize the coil design through computer simulation and construct the coils.

Project_Title: Developing Psychometric Methods for the Detection of Feigned Neuropsychiatric Disorders
Project_Description: Performance on neuropsychological tests will be compared across participants randomly assigned to three different conditions: a control group instructed to demonstrate their best ability and two experimental malingering conditions. One of these groups will be instructed to feign attention deficit/hyperactivity disorder (ADHD) and one will be instructed to feign traumatic brain injury. Data will be analyzed to identify the combination of psychometric instruments that best discriminate these three groups. The clinical and forensic application of this study is far-reaching and especially relevant to higher education settings where the legitimacy of ADHD claims has been receiving increased scrutiny. Being diagnosed with ADHD can result in significant benefits (academic accommodations, stimulant medication, disability benefits). However, currently there is no empirically supported method that can reliably differentiate genuine and feigned ADHD.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Administer and score neuropsychological tests; schedule research participants; prepare test material; enter data into statistical software; prepare reviews of the scientific literature; analyze data; prepare abstracts for scientific conferences; prepare conference posters or papers

Project_Title: Developing Gold Nanoparticle Biosensors
Project_Description: In our group, we develop biosensors that can detect diseases at an early stage.  Some ways of doing this are to add a layer of gold nanoparticles to existing sensor platforms.  The Physics problem that must be studied is: What happens when light interacts with atoms and/or gold nanoparticles?  We study this problem mathematically and/or numerically by solving Maxwell’s equations or the Schrodinger equation.  Techniques used are: Solving differential equations both by hand, and numerically (writing C or Fortran code, using a software package such as DDA or Lumerical).  Computations are done on SharcNet, so students will learn to work in a parallel computing environment.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: New students will perform literature search, or test the results obtained by previous group members.  Returning students will be given increased responsibility for coding, and independence, and may be able to produce original results for participation in a conference.  Weekly responsibilities include 8-10 hours of research (time on task) including writing reports; and a weekly meeting with the advisor or oral presentation.

Project_Title: Computational Economics with Python: Optimization- Economic Models
Project_Description: The main goal of this project is to give a student an opportunity to explore economic theory using a numerical computational approach. We will use Python (open source, free and platform independent; Python has a large user base and is still growing in popularity. This project will focus on topics and knowledge appropriate to a forth-year Economics student. We will start with (1) basic Python steps; lists, tuples and dictionaries; (2)  Basic programing techniques (branching and looping) and debugging; (3) Numerical basics: numpy library, Vector, Matrices and Functions; (4) Object oriented programming; (5) Root finding (Newton Algorithm, fsolve); (6) Optimization and Constrained Optimization: Optimization library, Minimize a function; (7) Economic Model: Overlapping Generations Model (Method 1: Substitution, Method 2: Gauss-Seidl Algorithm).
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: A student involved in this research project will learn to use the methodology of numerical modeling to construct applicable theoretical framework, build an operational economic model. The student will use numerical methods and modeling software Python to find feasible solutions, interpret the results, draw conclusions. Topics and problems will be related to Economics (4th year).

Project_Title: Comparative Inner Colonizations: Ontario, Germany and the World 1870-1945
Project_Description: When most of us think of colonization, we imagine people moving to a distant place, and even in historical texts this understanding of the word dominates discussion. Throughout the period 1870-1945, however, a significant form of global colonialism was 'inner colonization', colonialism within a country, from the often urbanized centre to the usually still agrarian borderlands. Ontario (and Canada more generally) was deemed around the world to be a model of ‘inner colonialism’, and it is the purpose of this project to compare and contrast  global,  contemporaneous  moments  of  this  form  of  settler  colonialism  in  order  to uncover the legacy of such migration and land-use for our world today

Project_Title: Compliance to an Internet-Delivered Intervention
Project_Description: Compliance is defined as the extent that clients complete reccommended homework between session, and has been shown to be imprtant for therapeutic outcomes.  This study intends to investigate compliance to an Internet-delivered mindfulness interventions by comparing self-reported compliance to the actual activity of participants with the intervention online (i.e. number of log ins, number of practice sessions completeted, etc.). In addition to comparing activity and self-reported involvement, we will examine demographic and personality characteristics that might impact compliance to an Internet-delivered intervention.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: The Outstanding Scholar involved in the project will participate in data collection, management, and analysis. The Outstanding Scholar will also have additional opportunities to become involved in other aspects of the project as well as other research projects in the lab.

Project_Title: Cluster Analysis of University Students Who Engage in Non-Suicidal Self-Injury
Project_Description: This project is looking at how sub-groups of individuals who engage in non-suicidal self-injury differ in terms of known risk factors for self-harm (emotion regulation, psychological well-being, etc.). Currently, research indicates that individuals who engage in self-harm differ in terms of frequency, severity, and basic demographic descriptives. However, little else is known about individual and group differences. Understanding how these sub-groups differ in term of risk factors is important for the development of intervention programs designed to reduce suicide and self-harm. This is important because interventions to date, have little efficacy in reducing self-harm. A better understanding of the mechanisms behind self-harm should help clinicians and researchers develop more effective interventions. The project will be using the psychology participant pool volunteers. Participants will come to our lab space, complete a series of questionnaires, and a Flanker task on an iPad. (Total time ~2hrs).

Project_Title: Youth Homelessness in an International Context
Project_Description:
Detroit Public Schools (DPS) Homeless Project. The overall purpose of this project was to assist the Detroit Public School’s Homeless program in building the district’s capacity to identify and meet the needs of homeless students and families. In accordance with the McKinney-Vento act, public schools are required to provide equal educational access and facilitate the same level of educational achievement for homeless and housed schoolchildren. This project represents work with DPS to raise awareness about identifying homeless students among staff, and to track the service needs and service receipt patterns of these students over time. Services provided also involved child and parent literacy training and tutoring programs within homeless shelters around the metro Detroit area. 
Detroit “Best Practices” With Homeless Youth and Families Project
Dr. Hernandez Jozefowicz and her colleagues were able to identify and evaluate agencies in the Detroit metropolitan area that represent ‘best practices’ for serving homeless adolescents and families. A major goal of this study was to provide valuable information to policymakers, researchers, and community organizations on how to more productively use existing funds to improve and develop programs that serve the homeless youth. 
Housing, Adolescence, and Life Outcomes (HALO) Project
This is a study of the longitudinal outcomes of homelessness using a probability sample of 251 homeless and 150 matched, housed youth from the metro Detroit area. These youth have been followed over 1.5 years to document risk and resilience factors associated with negative and positive outcomes. Further, this sample has been followed at 4.5, 5.5, and 6.5 years past baseline to assess longitudinal effects. Information on the children and subsequent parenting behavior of the participants has been investigated. Aims of this project include: 1) to investigate risk and protective factors predicting longitudinal outcomes and to test theoretical models on the pathways through which such factors operate in early adulthood for an at-risk urban sample, 2) to understand the possible impact of family dynamics on outcomes in early adulthood, 3) to assess the possible impact of peer relationships on adult outcomes, 4) to assess other contextual influences on adult outcomes (e.g., housed versus homeless status, community risk, school characteristics) and 5) whether risk and resilience processes are different for African-Americans vs. European-Americans and for males vs. females
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Tasks for Outstanding Scholars include but are not limited to conducting literature reviews, quantitative data analysis, and qualitative data analysis. Outstanding Scholars will engage in creating community connections and Community Based Research. Other tasks include abstract writing and grant writing.

Project_Title: Changing the Academy: Filling and Maintaining the Pipeline- Mentorship, Training, and Leading the Next Generation of Canadian Scholars
Project_Description: This projects seeks to develop a pan-Canadian effort to re-think the way that we train and mentor students, with a focus on the needs of "non-traditional" students, in order to ensure that future generations of scholars will not be held back and limited by the institutional and systematic discrimination of today. In political science we do ourselves a disservice by replicating what we have been doing for years, and while many scholars are changing the way that they teach and mentor students, many are not. A systematic and coordinated approach across institutional environments and in collaboration across multiple fields in political science may provide additional supports to faculty members and students. We see this project, through the Partnership Development Grant opportunity as a first effort or pilot to test partnerships and a potential set of programs to build a longer term network and set of institutions in the future so that we may ensure that we are able to ensure that our discipline continues to grow and flourish.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: The assigned researcher would gain valuable experience by assisting with grant development and organizing a workshop on the future of the Canadian academy. This is the preliminary work of a larger research project.

Project_Title: C/C++ programs for computationally intensive algorithms
Project_Description: There are many application areas, such as geoscience and bioinformatics, that use computationally intensive algorithms that are prime candidates for hardware acceleration using OPUs and FPGAs. The students will focus on a specific application area and research publicly available C/C++ programs.They will download and test the programs and prepare a report describing the programs. Project_Title: Body Image Self-Enhancement Following Interpersonal Rejection
Project_Description: This project explores the topics of interpersonal rejection and body image amongst women. Previous findings revealed a self-proclaimed higher appearance satisfaction amongst women whose self-worth is hghly contigent on their body weight  following an event of interpersonal rejection. The current project explores this unexpected data in further detail through the implementaion of two related studies. Study 1 assesses whether these findings could be the result of a defensive process by which women whose self-worth is  highyl contingent on body weight engage in self-enhancement to protein their sense of global self-worth. Study 2 explores the impact of providing these women with an opportunity to self-affirm on diminishing their defensive tendency to self-enhance after experiencing interpersonal rejection.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Research assistants will be responsible for guiding particpants through the experiments and also  will debrief the participants at the end of the session. Tasks include: organizing consent forms and records, acting as a confederate when necessary,  instructing/informing participants of the study procedure, delivering the experimental manipulation, administering measures (explicit self-report, implicit, and behavioural) as well as assessing suspicion and debriefing participants.

Project_Title: Biofuels production from low-value biomass
Project_Description:
Renewable energy production from low value biomass has gained widespread attention because of dwindling fossil supplies, climate change due to increasing greenhouse gas emissions and energy security. In current research projects, undergraduate students will be able to work with a senior graduate student who will train them to use a variety of instruments and equipment relevant to renewable energy research. At the same time, undergraduate students will be involve with reviewing literature related to a specific research area related to renewable energy (Period: months one to four (Fall: September to December). They will be trained on procedures for conducting a literature review using a variety of search engines. Student will be also provided with supplementary information from relevant environmental engineering courses which will assist them in understanding the literature. This activity is expected to increase their practical application of courses in environmental engineering.
Students will meet with Dr. Lalman and senior graduate students on a regular basis to resolve issues related to their understanding of science and engineering concepts in renewable energy research. During visits to a renewable energy methane producing facility in Leamington, ON, students will be encourage to attend. This Is expected to spark their interest in connecting research with practical application in a full-scale operating facility.
Current research work in our laboratory is focused on producing hydrogen from cornstover and switchgrass using microbial cultures. This work is performed under anaerobic conditions in a N2./C0 2 atmosphere. After developing their laboratory skills over 4 months, students will again work beside senior graduate students on laboratory experiments for another 4-month period (Period: months five to eight (Winter:January to April)). If the student is a returning Outstanding Scholar, he/she will undergo a training review for one month. After onemonth, the student will be able to execute preliminary experiments to develop confidence in conducting their independent experiments. The independent experiments will be designed in parallel with work undertaken by a senior graduate student.  After the 2nd year of training, a student who has gathered sufficient data will be encourage to attend and present their work at local conferences in Ontario. An annual conference focused on energy research is held in Ottawa and to date, students from my research group have presented their work at this conference in 2013 and 2014.  Depending upon their research contribution, students will be assigned authorship to publications. This will enhance their competitiveness in applying for NSERC USRA scholarships and graduate scholarships, if they decide to proceed with a research career.

Project_Description: Different technologies have been developed for automation and security including z-wave and zigbee technologies. Among the emerging technologies for automation, Bluetooth Low Energy (BLE) is rapidly growing with promising potential for a wide range of applications. In this project, students will learn how to develop cellphone applications for both Android and IOS platforms to remotely control BLE sensors for process automation. This is a hands-on project with both hardware and software components and requires students with strong programming background.

Project_Title: Beta Crystallin Reporter Genes
Project_Description: Beta crystallin is the major protein component of vertebrate lenses, and the gene of this protein appears to be a direct target for regulation by another gene, pitx3. This has been demonstrated by various means in vitro, but still needs to be confirmed in animals (frogs). Since human and mouse pitx3 mutants experience cataracts, there is an implied link between pitx3, beta-crystallin regulation, and lens health. The project will involve re-cloning the beta crystallin gene into new configurations that will permit us to make transgenic frog embryos with glow-in-the-dark lenses: this will facilitate studies of pitx3-beta crystallin interplay and regulation.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
•	generate E coli stocks to permit DNA construct amplification
•	amplify DNA constructs
•	cut, ligate, and clone new DNA constructs
•	test constructs for correct composition and sequence
•	time permitting, generate trangenic from embryoes and document under UV microscope

Project_Title: Assessing the need for a student recovery program at the University of Windsor
Project_Description: In recent years, the number of recovery programs on college campuses has increased dramatically in the United States, but there have yet to be any such programs established in Canada. For students in recovery, successfully balancing the demands of school, work, social life, and a recovery program is a life-threatening issue; having a peer group to share these challenges with is critical to success. This project is the first phase in the development of a recovery program on the University of Windsor campus.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Assisting with obtaining data on current campus alcohol and substance use, collection of data (if necessary), literature review on collegiate recovery, search for grant funding, reviewing data on college students in recovery, preparing a poster for presentation at a conference, potential for inclusion on a manuscript if desired (and expectations for authorship are met)

Project_Title: Automotive Massage Validation
Project_Description: The OS student, will be tasked with being the research assistant to a current Master's student working on a combined physiology & biomechanics/ergonomics study. This study is propriety in nature and thus requires the involvement of mature, and trustworthy students only who can uphold the confidentiality requirements. The general overview of the project will be investigating the physiological and biomechanical responses of a car seat massage system used during prolonged periods of sitting. This project will be lead by a Master's student in part of their graduate degree requirements. All data collection will be conducted in the Department of Kinesiology at the University of Windsor.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
The OS student will be involved with such aspects as of training, participant recruitment, data collection, and any other tasks deemed relevant, applicable, and necessary.
The OS student will be potentially trained in using the following measurement instrumentation: NIRS, Dinamap, and Biofeedback. The NIRS, or Near-Infrared Spectroscopy, is a non-invasive approach to determine relative or absolutely global tissue oxygenation. The Dinamap is instrumentation used to determine blood pressure and heart rate. The biofeedback system will focus on measurements pertaining to peripheral skin temperature, skin conductance, heart rate, and blood volume pulse via photoplethysmorgraphy. Additionally, a Visual Analogue Scale (VAS) will be used to determine the ratings of perceived discomfort among participants. As all of these measurements will be taken at approximately the same time during data collection, it is advantageous to have a Primary Investigator (Master's student) and a Research Assistant (OS student) present at all times. It will also be helpful when inputting data into such logs as an excel spreadsheet.
The OS student will also receive the opportunity to gain valuable experience working with a Master's student as their direct mentor, and the faculty supervisor's involved with the project. The two main point of contacts regarding faculty supervisor's will be Dr. Paula M. van Wyk and Dr. Anthony Bain.
The OS student will enhance communication and time management skills by coordinating with the Principal Investigator and the research team to ensure availability and attendance (class schedule permitting).

Project_Title: Assessing Professional Competence: The Case of Law and Engineering in the Canadian and Global Social Contexts
Project_Description:
This project is a collaboration between Dr. Victoria Abboud (CMF/Engineering) and Maggie Liddle (Law). It is the first phase in a broader inquiry that addresses the varied competencies in each discipline and their perceived efficacy to improve student outcomes as professionals who influence global society and generate social impact. A comparative framework will exemplify three universal strands of professional education: (1) intellectual learning of academic knowledge and the capacity to think in ways that are important to the profession; (2) skills teaching and "know-how"; and (3) student ownership of the ethical standards, social roles, and responsibilities of their profession. Students often fail to grasp that professional competencies equip them to be global change-makers. This research considers how systems thinking and social impact might be incorporated into the teaching and learning of each discipline to foster graduate awareness of their potential social impact as professionals. Therefore, the first phase goal is to investigate the professional competencies through each disciplinary association to identify areas for progressing professional competence.
 	The Core Engineering Competencies, as outlined by Engineers Canada (2012), identify the seven areas in which engineers must prove competence. Among these, Competency C and E ("Protect the Public Interest" and "Communicate Engineering Information," respectively) most closely reflect the outward, globally focused need for engineering professionals to be aware of systems and social implications of their work.
Unlike Engineering, Law competencies for the Canadian Common Law JD programs are a recent development (Federation of Canadian Law Societies, 2009; 2018), Canada-centric, yet have a global reach in aligning Canadian JD's with those in other common law jurisdictions. There are three areas of competency: Skills, Knowledge, and Ethics and Professionalism. All carry a social justice component, which, like Engineering, necessitates the graduates' awareness of systems and the social impact of the law and their work as lawyers.
Questions that drive this inquiry include, but are not limited to, how might systems thinking and social impact factor into professionalization in both Engineering and Law? In what ways might systems thinking and social impact be incorporated into each discipline such that graduates will gain awareness of their potential social impact as professionals?
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
•	Conduct research through Engineering and Law academic databases, Library (possibly inter-library loan), and online (e.g., Google Scholar, association websites)
•	Collate research findings and prepare a literature review and summary reports
•	Meet with the collaborators to discuss findings and to suggest avenues for further inquiry

Project_Title: Application of Memristor-Based Imply Logic to Design Fast Adders
Project_Description: Memristors have been utilized to implement logic gates. Among the logic gates that have been targeted for implementation, Imply Logic is the most successful. In this project we will attempt to implement fast adders using Memristor-based Imply Logic.

Project_Title: Algebras and their Biduals
Project_Description:
Algebras are fundamental objects in mathematics. One can think of the product in a general  algebra as a generalization of matrix multiplication. 

Given an algebra A, its bidual carries two natural products extending the multiplication on A, called the left and right Arens products. In recent years the study of a class of bidual algebras has been a very active field in mathematics.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
In this project, the students will explore some elementary bidual algebras. The project is related 
to the preliminary part of a Fields Undergraduate Research Program, which was initiated from some of my recent research work.

Project_Title: Analysis of Driver Behavior in Anger-provoking Events using a Driving Simulator
Project_Description: Dr. Kathryn Lafreniere (Psychology) and Dr. Chris Lee (Civil Engineering) at the University of Windsor recently completed the survey for 200 participants to investigate their propensity for angry driving.  They found from the survey data that drivers’ propensity for angry driving is closely related with their socioeconomic, personality and motivational characteristics. To further investigate the survey participants’ actual driving behavior, a group of the participants will be invited to test their driving in various anger-provoking traffic events (e.g., slow-moving lead vehicle, vehicle cut-in). These events have been created in a virtual traffic scenario using the driving simulator at the Transportation System Innovation Lab. Each participant’s driving behavior will be observed based on the data from the simulator. The objective of this project is to investigate how the driver’s socioeconomic, personality and motivational characteristics are associated with their aggressiveness of driving in these anger-provoking events. It is expected that the project will inform the development of more effective driver training programs to prevent aggressive driving, which can lead to improvements in road user safety.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: The student will learn how to conduct a driving simulator experiment, and extract and process a large-scale vehicle trajectory data collected from the driving simulator. Based on the drivers’ observed behavior, the student will analyze how the driver’s socioeconomic, personality and motivational characteristics are associated with their aggressiveness of driving in different anger-provoking traffic events.

Project_Title: Alexander Pope, Animal Welfare, and Environmentalism
Project_Description: Animal subjectivity and welfare are such a strong theme in Pope's work that he could be called an eminent proponent of these concepts in this early period. Relatedly, a sense of the autonomous nature of the natural environment ("and laughing Ceres re-assumed the land") is a key element in Pope's satiric, aesthetic, and moral world view. This book will re-situate not only Pope but also the early eighteenth century in relation to the ecocritical studies and in relation to interspecies history.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Biographical research assistance both primary and secondary; searching of a range of primary documents 1600-1790 from avaliable databases. I am completing my first project on Pope and religion, as well as editing a collection, and may also require editorial assistance.

Project_Title: Alexander Pope: Imagination, Life, and Work
Project_Description: This monograph study examines the life, work, and religious imagination of Alexander Pope, from various methodological points of view, including: biographical; political; social; intellectual and religious history; textual criticism and history of the book/print marketplace; textual analysis; and theoretical applications.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: searching online databases and print collections (subject-specific searches for relevant primary and secondary materials); collation and organization of bibliographical material in various formats, including some data entry.

Project_Title: A Silent Epidemic on Women’s Health: Canadian Women’s Narrative Accounts of Their Diagnostic Journeys with Polycystic Ovary Syndrome
Project_Description:
The primary objective of the proposed project is to obtain pilot data in a sample of women diagnosed with polycystic ovary syndrome (PCOS) regarding their experiences with the diagnostic process. PCOS is the most common endocrine disorder in women of reproductive age. Prevalence rates demonstrate that it affects 10% to 25% of women, and takes, on average, five years (range = 2.5 to 16 years) and many consultations with multiple health care providers to reach a definitive diagnosis after the first presentation of symptoms, often presenting at puberty. Over the last decade, improvements in diagnostic criteria, guidelines, and assessment tools have not produced an expected reduction in the diagnostic time frame. PCOS continues to remain underdiagnosed. This substantial delay in reaching a diagnosis can exacerbate underlying health concerns, and can lead to irreversible long-term health complication (e.g., insulin-resistance, diabetes, cardiovascular disease, cancer, as well as mental health concerns such as anxiety, depression, lower quality of life, and eating disorders). We suspect that cultural attitudes toward obesity and gender discrimination in heath care contexts play an important role in the diagnostic delay, as 60% of women with PCOS are classified as overweight or obese. 
Specifically, we assess 1) the potentially negative impact of feminine body-type stereotypes on diagnostic decisions by medical professionals, and 2) the consequences of these biases on the time it takes women to receive a diagnosis and the resulting physical and psychological health outcomes of bias-related delays. This pilot data is part of a long-term research agenda to better understand the negative consequences of biased attitudes toward women in a health care context and to develop empirically grounded interventions for health care professionals to reduce these negative consequences. In short, what we are proposing is the initial step in a multiphase line of research which impacts the physical and psychological health of 1.4 million Canadian women. This baseline data on women’s experiences with the health care system from initial diagnosis to subsequent treatment of PCOS will: 1. Provide pilot data on the feasibility of the proposed methodology for recruiting a valid sample of women with a PCOS diagnosis. 2. Demonstrate the validity of the interview protocol for obtaining rigorous mixed methods data on women’s health care experiences and the qualitative links of those experiences to a wide array of physical and mental health outcomes. 3. Provide a conceptual and theoretical framework and reliable set of constructs from which to refine statistical models examining the role that negative health care experiences play in exacerbating the physical and psychological health consequences of PCOS. 4. Broaden the range of funding sources by laying the groundwork for an applied research program targeting both the basic research aims noted above but also evaluation research on the development and efficacy of educational and advocacy interventions. 5. Demonstrate the ability to become a leader in research on the interface of socialized biases in health care service delivery and women’s health.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: The student is going to assist us with several pressing tasks this semester. They will also be my lab coordinator, ensuring that all tasks and activities are completed in a timely fashion (e.g., checking in with team members on a regular basis, organizing literature reviews, and compiling transcripts). The student will also assist in conducting literature reviews, searches, and annotated bibliographies within the areas of womens’ health, PCOS, obesity stigma, and gender-based discrimination in health care, as well as with on-going transcription of qualitative interviews, and coding of qualitative data. The student will gain experience narrative coding and inter-rater reliability with other team members. The student will also help prepare new projects, consult with REB regarding mixed method studies, and learn how to integrate qualitative and quantitative methods.

Project_Title: A Regional History of National Parks in the Middle East
Project_Description: Conservation in the Middle East only began in earnest in the 1970s and 1980s driven by the establishment of National Parks. In many Middle Eastern countries their entire complement of National Parks were established at once. This project aims to tell the story of the subsequent history of these parks over the past half century through the relationship of these spaces to three groups of people: administrators and politicians who run, fund and promote the parks; scientists conducting research in the parks; and conservationists at the forefront of a very young movement.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Students will have the opportunity to work with both secondary and primary sources, and would have the opportunity to shape the contours of their research to suit their interests. This research is at an early stage and students will have the opportunity to impact the development of the research questions and perameter of the project as a whole.  I require literature reviews of environmental history writing and the publication of scholarly writing based on research conducted in these parks. This includes articles and books on the flora and fauna of the parks but also the relationship of local populations to the project of conservation. Research on the Middle East’s burgeoning environmentalist movement will be conducted in the newspapers and magazines of the Middle East, many of them available online. Every country in the Middle East has an English language local paper, but if students have language skills this research could also be conducted in Arabic, Turkish or Farsi.

Project_Title: Actuarial Exam P Materials Preperation
Project_Description: Material for Actuarial Exam P will be gathered. The student will enter the material into LaTeX. Solutions to problems will be obtained and entered into LaTeX.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Learning LaTeX and using it for problems. Web searching for Actuarial Exam P information. Hold a tutorial for Exam P. Problem Solving.

Project_Title: 3D Cartesian Mesh Generation Software
Project_Description:
In this project the student will work with an in-house C/C++ boundary code that produces a mathematical representation of geometric shapes and a Cartesian mesh generation codes that constructs meshes over complex 3D domains. Additional features will be added to the codes, such as the ability to interface with standard CAD file formats such as STL, IGES and CGNS.
The student will enhance these codes to make them more flexible and user-friendly, create a set of illustrative benchmark problems and design some self-learning tutorials.
This project requires strong C/C++ programming skills and good knowledge of vector calculus, particularly functions of several variables, surfaces and 3D geometry.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Improve the capabilities of an existing 3D mesh generator. Develop Graphical User Interfaces for the mesh generation software and related codes. Establish a set of benchmark problems to illustrate the capabilities of the code.

Project_Title: "Making Methanol Great Again": Achieving Selectivity in CO2 Reduction
Project_Description: Despite the abundance of CO₂ in Earth’s atmosphere, catalytic transformations of this molecule currently focus on reduction to formate (HCO₂⁻) or carbon monoxide (CO) - representing a bottleneck for accessing reduced C₁-equivalents such as methanol (CH₃OH) directly.  This proposal seeks to identify new iron catalysts for the selective reduction of CO₂, a waste/greenhouse gas, to CH₃OH.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
The student in this role will work alongside the primary investigator, fellow undergraduate, and graduate students to build new molecules for use in chemical reaction development (synthesis). These new species will be characterized by a suite of spectroscopic tools aimed at precisely understanding structure (how atoms are connected to one another) at the molecular level. This project targets the development of molecules that are relevant to the breakdown of carbon dioxide (CO2) – a persistent greenhouse gas, to provide fuel, which can be used in automobiles, for example. 
The student in this role will work in a synthetic chemistry laboratory equipped with state-of-the-art equipment and facilities – all training will be received as necessary (previous experience is not required). The student will work with air/moisture sensitive compounds employing a hands-on, problem-solving-based approach towards managing and troubleshooting problems in research and discovery. Communication skills and teamwork will be emphasized.
