The student will work with air/moisture sensitive compounds employing a hands-on, problem-solving-based approach towards managing and troubleshooting problems in research and discovery. Communication skills and teamwork will be emphasized.

The student in this role will work in a synthetic chemistry laboratory equipped with state-of-the-art equipment and facilities – all training will be received as necessary (previous experience is not required). The student will work with air/moisture sensitive compounds employing a hands-on, problem-solving-based approach towards managing and troubleshooting problems in research and discovery. Communication skills and teamwork will be emphasized.

Project_Title: Portable Handheld 4-Gas Monitor Calibration Test
Project_Description: In gas migration investigations testing of an abandoned well site for methane straying from production zones is often conducted using a 4-gas monitor. This monitor produces non-specific concentration measurements and detection. Our lab is checking the accuracy of the sensor with different certified methane standards to determine the % of error industry partners can expect when utilizing this tool.

Project_Title: Optimization
Project_Description: The successful student will be participate in projects that will involve the theory and application of optimization.  Level appropriate training will be provided to make this a true learning experience.  Skills learned will include the use of optimization tools available in MS Excel and / or MATLAB and LINGO; technical report writing, and advanced presentation skills. Past projects include work on "Optimal Vaccination Delivery", "Warehouse Location for a National Company", "Trade-off between Transportation an Inventory Costs for a Logistics Company",  "Emergency Waiting Room Strategies", "Software Tools for Sketching Linear Programming Problems", "Software for Extreme Point Identification".

Project_Title: New Designs For Organic Dyes as Semiconductors
Project_Description: Organic dyes are ubiquitous in nature and manufactured products. Their technological development has seen many academic and commercial success stories and is as important and vibrant today as it has been over the last 150 years. Almost every new technology requires the development of new, tailor-made dyes that may function as colourants, light emitters, stabilizers, semiconductors, initiators, recognition sites, imaging agents, etc. Both, molecular and supramolecular structures affect the properties of dyes and must be optimized for every desired application. My group presently focuses on the development of fundamentally new molecular designs for side-chain free dyes that self-assemble into mesophases that support semi-conducting properties for application in organic field effect transistors and photovoltaic devices.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: application of these dyes and are expected to present their findings at regional and national meetings/conferences. They will work closely together with a graduate student during their first 2 years of training.

Project_Title: Retrospective on the Court Challenges Program of Canada
Project_Description: Since its creation in the late 1970s, the Court Challenges Program has had an incredible history, being several times terminated and reinstated by the Government of Canada. Over the years, this program has funded numerous court cases pertaining to language and equality rights, making it a vital instrument of informal constitutional change in the country. The proposed project seeks to retrace the controversial program's history, its inner workings, and its jurisprudential impact.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: This project will give an edge to students applying for law school and help them to excel once in law school. Students will receive training in locating and analyzing primary (archival data, jurisprudence) and secondary data (literature), project management, editing (ex: using legal citations), and dissemination strategies. Mastery of Canada's two official languages is a plus, but not mandatory.

Project_Title: Feminist advocacy in decentralized federations: Canada and the United States
Project_Description: Recent research describing interest group interaction with two and sometimes three levels of government in federal stats suggests that groups can adapt well to shifting scales of government action on any given policy area. However, that same research has not been applied to social movement interaction and how social movement organizations (SMOs) navigate the various scales of government action at the municipal, state/provincial, and federal/national levels as govermnents share responsibility for particularly social program delivery. It is the purpose of this project (part of a larger edited collection on gender, diversity, and federalism for Edward Elgar publishers – 2019 publication date) to compare women’s movement advocacy in two decentralized federations since 2000 to see how they advocate in these challenging environments, and how federalism shapes or is shaped by this advocacy.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: The student will conduct backgbround research on the patterns of consultation and advocacy projects within the broader meso- and national-level women’s movement in both the US and Canada. This will be done via online (advocacy organization websites, etc.) and government document analyses. The student may also be involved in email interview question correspondence to particular groups to follow up on background research and literature review work.

Project_Title: Content analysis of course reflection papers
Project_Description: I have been doing research on my information literacy course, Information Searching and Analysis.  At the end of the semester, students submit reflection papers on the course.  These papers have proved to be a rich source of data for analyzing the effectiveness of the course.  This project will be a content analysis of those papers.  We will develop a coding scheme, code all the reflection papers and subject it to an intercoder reliability measure.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: The outstanding scholar for this project will help develop the coding scheme for the content analysis, participate with me in coding the reflection papers and write a descriptive summary of the data that emerge from the content analysis.

Project_Title: Feminizing Party Difference – book manuscript preparation
Project_Description: This book manuscript uses qualitatitive and quantitative approaches to measuring the impact of party differences, provincial socio-economic context, and the strength and influence of child care and anti-violence movements in determining differing government responses to movement demands in child care and anti-violence against women policy arenas over time.  It draws on elite-level interviews with key movement, party and government actors, archival data on parties and women’s movements in each province, statistical socio-economic data, internal party and movement documents, newspaper accounts of government and movement activity, government documents and committee submissions and secondary source material on government leaders and party performances over time.  The book argues that while left-right party differences help us understand policy diversity, partisan theory needs to measure feminist consciousness levels within the extra-parliamentary party and party leadership in order to adequately explain diverse women’s policy results.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: The research assistants employed for this project will help prepare the above manuscript for publication with UBC Press by updating literature and data on partisanship and feminist advocacy; provincial women’s movement organizing; partisan changes in governments in British Columbia and Ontario; as well as socio-economic health between 2007 and 2018.

Project_Title: Citizen engagement in municipal decision making in Canada
Project_Description: municipal policy outcomes as a result. This project will examine different case studies involving community engagement. The first is an exploration of the impact of community engagement in shaping public transportation, particularly in the context of the COVID-19 pandemic. The second case study is an examination of neighbourhood groups and their capacity to mobilize and engage with municipal policy makers.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: The student will undertake a literature review of community and individual engagement in municipal policy making in Canada and other selected states. The student will also complete an analysis of municipal government websites to examine the formal procedures that are in place for engaging with community groups.

Project_Title: Explaining the rise of Trump through Chimpanzee  politics
Project_Description:
It is easy to dismiss Donald Trump’s path to the White House as a fluke or accident of history. However, to do so would be to miss an opportunity to examine the nature of why some people are successful at gaining power, while others are not. To provide some context into the electoral success we need to go beyond public opinion polls, policy analysis, and traditional electoral politics and examine the underlining attributes of what makes a successful leader. Indeed, we should look at more primal explanations such as Chimpanzee politics 

Tom Flanagan in Winning Power describes Frans de Waal’s book Chimpanzee Politics: Power and Sex among Apes as the “most important book on politics since Machiavelli’s Prince.” Flanagan notes the similarities of chimpanzee hierarchy and human political campaigns. This study will examine the chimpanzee politics theory as applied to the 2016 U.S. Presidential campaign to explain the appeal of Donald Trump.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
. Conduct a literature review on Chimpanzee Politics, with a focus on feminist literature, and anthropology
2. Write annotated bibliographies
3. Write drafts of chapters

This work can be done remotely accessing UWindsor library resources and conducting weekly update meetings through Microsoft Teams

Project_Title: Current Issues in Eastern Religions
Project_Description:
This project involves being a research assistant for three chapters of a forthcoming book titled Wolrd Religions: Eastern Traditions, 5th ed. Edited by Roy C. Amore and Amir Hussain. New York: Oxford University Press.
The chapters are “Studying Eastern Religions,” “Buddhist Traditions,” and “Current Issues in Eastern Religions.”
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: The student will be asked to research with the goal of finding recent scholarly and other examples of new developments, trends and issues relevant to Eastern Religions. The work will mostly be done on line, but weekly meetings with the supervisor are required, plus the electronic submission of research results.

Project_Title: Structural and Functional Changes Following a Concussion
Project_Description: The goal is to review consistencies, as well as abnormalities, observed in the literature regarding the structural and functional changes following a concussive injury. In addition, it is important to describe advances and variations in neuroimaging techniques that effectively identify anatomical and physiological alterations associated with mTBI.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
-Identifying pertiant information to the particular changes that follow a concussion (mTBI)
-accurately describing situational differences that may arise 
-summarizing the general/ most common occurences of concussive injury with respects to anatomy and physiology
-effectively writing a literature review of gathered information

Project_Title: The Impact of the COVID-19 Pandemic on Nurses' Health and Well-Being in a Border City
Project_Description: The purpose of this investigation is to explore Windsor registered nurses’ (RN) experiences of working during the COVID-19 pandemic on both sides of the border, to identify mental health challenges that might cause significant distress and dysfunction and to identify approaches to support their mental health. Contributors to nurses’ stress may include concerns about their own and their families’ health and safety; high patient mortality rates; ethical challenges associated with patient care; lack of information and guidelines; perceived unsafe working conditions; financial hardship; and precautionary self-isolation. Previous research has established significant mental health impacts for nurses working during pandemics that may be associated with changes in attitude towards the profession and thoughts about leaving the job. Interviews will be conducted through an online platform with 30 RNs living in Windsor, Ontario and working in Windsor or Detroit hospitals. Interview questions will focus on experiences working in hospitals, mental health and work-related challenges and resilience and coping strategies.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
•	Literature review
•	Interview scheduling and participant communication
•	Qualitative data analysis
•	Preparation of findings for publication/presentation

Project_Title: Implementation of Algorithms for Finding Protein Isoforms in Transcriptomics Data
Project_Description:
Messenger RNA molecules (mRNAs) in eukaryotes carry the genetic information from DNA to the ribosome where they are translated into amino acid sequences (potential protein isoforms).  The potential translated frame of those continuous mRNA nucleotides, the coding sequence (CDS), derives an open reading frame (ORF).  ORFs start with a start codon AUG and terminate when reaching a stop codon UAA, UAG or UGA.
On the other hand, next generation sequencing has revolutionized the way in which genomic and transcriptomic studies are carried out. The use of RNA-seq, in particular, helps discover unexplored territories by “reading” the transcriptome very high resolution. RNA-seq studies can be used not only to simply measure gene expression, but also to reveal protein isoforms and quantitatively measure splicing and other transcriptomic activity, namely tanscripts.

An important step in understanding the role of transcript is to reconstruct protein isoforms. The benefits of this step include understanding the effects of RNA alternative splicing in diseases like cancer.  In this project a tool for extracting protein isoforms from RNA transcripts is aimed to be developed. Access to proteomics and interactomics databases will integrate the resulting protein isoforms in order to find meaningful biological information that is particularly useful in studying some diseases like prostate cancer and others. An automatic tool is desirable due to the large number of transcripts being processed.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: The main student’s tasks will involve some reading in order to become familiar with the basics of transcriptomics and protein databases. Also, the student will become familiar with languages like Python and XML. The student will also participate in the design and analysis of algorithms for integration transcriptomics and interactomics data. He will implement some of these algorithms in open platforms that include programming languages like Python, Script, and some open source packages.

Project_Title: Physics-Based Interpolation Techniques for HIgh-Fidelity CFD Simulations
Project_Description: The concept of “interpolation” is a key element underlying the meshing strategies, the quality of the results and the rate of convergence of Computational Fluid Dynamics (CFD) simulations. Traditional interpolations rely on mathematical approximations and geometrical considerations. In this project the student will develop and implement a new set of interpolation schemes that are formulated from the physics of the problem being solved. These new interpolation schemes will be more accurate than existing schemes. Applications to mesh refinement strategies, data processing and multigrid techniques will be explored.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Develop the algorithms needed to implement a physics-based interpolation scheme. Incorporate the new technique into an in-house CFD code. Design a set of appropriate benchmark problems to illustrate the improvement compared to traditional interpolation methods..

Project_Title: GPGPU Computing
Project_Description:
This project offers the opportunity to work at the cutting-edge of high-performance large-scale scientific computing. The student will explore the benefits of solving large linear systems of algebraic equations using Graphics Processing Units (GPUs) rather than Central Processing Units (CPUs). The student will develop a GPU version of an existing in-house CPU code for the numerical solution of convection-diffusion equations that model 3D physics and engineering problems. The resulting code will be verified using established benchmark problems from the open literature,
This project requires strong computer programming skills. Prior experience with GPGPU is not mandatory, but the student must have a strong desire and ability for self-learning, and willingness to work hard.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Modify a CPU-based code to run on the GPUs. Optimize the performance of the code. Search the literature for appropriate benchmark problems to illustrate the capabilities of the code.

Project_Title: Constructing Radio Frequency (RF) Coils for MRI Experiments
Project_Description: In magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), radio frequency (RF) coils are the essential component to transit and/or receive MR signal. RF engineering also has wide applications in areas such as mobile phones, radios, Wi-Fi and can be easily extended to microwave communication. In this project, students will learn the basic RF principle, design and construct coils at various frequencies and geometries for MRI experiments.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Students will work with electronics and perform MRI experiments.

Project_Title: Development of a 2D Multiblock/Multielement Cartesian Grid Generator
Project_Description:
This project offers the student the opportunity to further develop their programming skills. The student will work with an in-house 2D single block Cartesian grid generator to provide additional capabilites and a user-friendly interface. The code will then be modified to allow for highly complex domains using multiblock and multielement algorithms.

NOTE:  This project requires good knowledge of computer programming in C/C++. Candidate must demonstrate interest and desire to learn, and willingness to work hard. The mathematics involved in this project is mainly 1st-year level.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Enhance the capabilities and performance of an existing grid generation code. Design a set of appropriate benchmark problems to illustrate the capabilities of the code.

Project_Title: Characterizing Dynamic Field Change in MRI Experiements
Project_Description:
Some magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) scanners are based on permanet magnets. In such systems, the static magnetic field is temperature dependent. In addition to ambient temperature flucturation, the magnetic field gradient coils generate heat during experiments, leading to local field drifts and image artifacts. In this project, students will measure and quantitatively evaluate this effect, and develop new algorithms to incorporate it in data processing and image reconstruction. 

This project will lead to a publication in a peer-reviewed journal.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Students will design and perform MRI experiments, and process the data for advanced image reconstruction.

Project_Title: Energy Level Mass Dependence of Helium Isotopes
Project_Description: Students will use FORTRAN and graphing software (Excel, gnuplot, etc.) to create King plots in the interest of determining the dependance of energy level transitions upon the mass of isotopes of Helium. By manipulating experimental data, students will show whether a quadratic dependence could exist.

Project_Title: Design and Development of Infrared Spectrometer
Project_Description: In this project, you will design and develop and infrared spectrometer for ultrafast lasers. Intense ultrafast laser pulses require a carefully controlled spectral bandwidth, and with your instrument we will be able to characterize them. To design the spectrometer, you will need to use some basic optics principles to prototype your instrument. Prototyping will let you work with lasers, fast photodiodes, mirrors, and oscilloscopes. You will need to learn how to align lasers, and develop analog-to-digital (ADC) signal processing. Once you have this prototype working, you will use computer assisted drawing (CAD) software for 3D printing the housing of the unit. You will be able to optimize and miniaturize your design using the CAD software.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Aligning laser beams, assembling optical parts, signal processing. Designing instruments, prototyping, using CAD software, 3D printing.

Project_Title: Constructing Hybrid Deep Learning Methods for Social Network Analysis
Project_Description: The advent of deep learning (DL) methods in Machine Learning (ML) have proved success in traditionally stagnated areas such as Natural Language Processing and Image Recognition. Furthermore, DL takes advantage of massive data sets for neural network training and accuracy. Social Network Analysis is a big data problem with dynamic properties that have several sub-problems including community detection and link prediction. The recent success of evolutionary population based algorithms such as Cultural Algorithms have demonstrated promising outcomes. There remains a challenge in improving accuracty of predictions and speed. This project is aimed to investigate the feasibility and build hybrid algorithms that take advantage of DL methods and CA approaches to tackle the dynamic and large nature of SNA type problems. The premise is that knowledge based evolutionary algorithms coupled with artificial neural networks can yield to improved results. Here we shall test the algorithms on benchmark data sets as well as synthetic health-care related networks.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
-	Survey and classify existing DL methods and the various artificial neural networks types and their uses.
-	Survey and classify exisitng evolutionary algorithms, including GA, CA, DE, CO, and others.
-	Design and build a hybrid prototype and compare it to DL and EC methods on benchmark and synthetic networks to test their accuracy, performance and scalability.
-	Report the results in a technical report or research publication.
-	These activities may span multiple terms.

Project_Title: Theory of Strongly Driven Electrons in Ultrafast Fields
Project_Description: In this project,the student will apply the results of special theory of relativity to the Lorentz Lorenz shift observed in experiments  with strongly driven electrons in intense ultrafast fields.  Possible correlations to experiments  will be investigated.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Literature search, background reading,mathematical calculations, plotting on Python or C, report  writing on LaTex.

Project_Title: Deep Learning Algorithms in Document and Network Analyses
Project_Description: This project is to explore deep learning algorithms, such as word2vec and node2vec, to analyse semantic relations between documents and networks. We will learn the state-of-the-art algorithms and apply them to learn representations for academic papers, and evaluate the results in machine learning tasks such as recommending most related papers.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: You will learn machine learning algorithms especially artificial neural networks, and learn to modify the algorithms to suit better for our tasks. The programming language will be Python. You will experiment with large data sets that consists of millions of documents, hundreds of millions of citation links.

Project_Title: Study of Bioinformatics Databases and Tools to Validate Biomarkers in Lung Cell Types
Project_Description: With the aim of better understanding biological systems, single-cell RNA-seq (scRNA-seq) has been widely used in genetics studies and specifically to identify different cell types. scRNA-seq technologies enabling researchers to investigate cells individually and understand their biological mechanisms. In the recent years, some machine learning algorithms have been applied on scRNA-seq data to cluster different cell types based on similar gene expression patterns. In this project, we aim to use bioinformatics tools and databases to biologically validate molecular biomarkers in lung cell types found by clustering techniques.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: The student will write reports on biological validation of the relevant biomarkers. Additionally, the student will investigate different machine learning models and tools for dimensionality reduction and clustering.

Project_Title: Developing Low Cost NMR/MRI Devices for Novel Biomedical Applications
Project_Description: Magnetic Resonance Imaging (MRI) is probably the most flexible and powerful diagnostic imaging technique available to clinical medicine. The most significant limitation of MRI is the high cost of traditional scanners with superconducting magnet. Our group develops low cost NMR/MRI devices and explores novel applications using NMR parameters to characterize properties of biomedical systems. Students will have the opportunity to work on constructing and characterizing the devices, as well as computer simulations. Students will also be expected to learn the physics principles underlying magnetic resonance relaxations, signal excitation and detection, as well as spatial encoding and image processing techniques. Students working on this project for several years may be invited to prepare their research results for publication in a peer-reviewed journal.

Project_Title: Putnam Undergraduate Math Contest Preperation
Project_Description: Students will participate in our weekly training sessions for the Putnam Undergraduate Math Competition. The Putnam is a North America-wide competition open to all undergraduate students. Topics covered on this competition include but aren't limited to calculus, linear algebra, combinatorics, number theory, geometry, and probability. Doing well on this competition is helpful for applying to graduate school, especially if you are interested in attending a top graduate school, as you are competing against students from all schools across North America.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Attend our weekly one-hour training sessions, and write both 3 hour parts of the competitions.

Project_Title: The Effects of Fatigue on Knee Joint Landing Mechanics
Project_Description: This project involves collecting new joint landing mechanics during a submaximal fatiguing protocol on 24 athletes. Athletes visit the Lab for an orientation and testing session. Student will be involved in the data collection, data processing, and data analysis of the project. This will be a great opportunity to learn high performance research techniques.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
Student will be involved in the data collection of knee joint mechanics using motion capture, force plates, and sufacty electromyography. Student will be trained to process data and be involved in writing as it relates to the project, including let review and introduction.

This project is available only to full-time students who have qualified for the Outstanding Scholars program. Students engaged in this project will: locate and review literature; describe and discuss terminology; apply the discipline’s theories, techniques and methods; contribute to the analysis of a significant research problem; and contribute to the production of a significant research finding.

Project_Title: Creating Elite Aboriginal Athlete Entries on Wikipedia
Project_Description:
This project will create a coherent presence for elite Aboriginal athletes on Wikipedia. Currently there are a small number of Aboriginal athletes on Wikipedia and their presence is not systematically accessible. This project will enhance the public’s knowledge about Aboriginal athletes, and help Aboriginal and non-Aboriginal individuals to access information about successful Aboriginal individuals. A Strengths and Hope perspective underlies this project. Accordingly, another goal is to highlight existing strengths of Aboriginal elite athletes, the resources they have been able to draw upon, and the ways they have been a resource for others. 
The student will list existing Wikipedia entries about Aboriginal elite athletes, and learn the Wikipedia rules for posting new entries. The student will perform biographical research that will prepare her/him to edit existing entries and create new entries, under the professor’s supervision. The student will contribute to a strategy for optimizing public accessibility to entries about Aboriginal elite athletes.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: identify suitable elite Aboriginal athletes/events in conjunction with professor; and research articles/newspaper accounts and create a Wikipedia entry for each individual/event. This is part of a research project tied to Sport History and Sport Sociology, and thus students can be involved in preparing academic presentations and papers on this topic.

Project_Title: The Allrounder
Project_Description:
The Allrounder (theallrounder.com) is a cutting-edge sport studies website with an international flavour that draws on contributions from leading academics and highly regarded journalists.
This project allows for a student to be immersed in the advanced field of socio-cultural sport studies by conducting literature reviews of high-minded sport websites that will allow him/her the opportunity to co-research and co-write articles that will appear in The Allrounder.
Ultimately, I am in the market for someone with a passion for reading and writing, an insatiable sense of curiosity that will push them to learn more, and a sense of creativity that will facilitate outside-the-box story ideas, analysis, and research techniques.

Project_Title: Integrative Regulation of Cerebrovascular Function
Project_Description: The aim of the project is to understand the effects of different stressors (e.g. heat stress, sleep deprivation, hypoxia etc.) on the integrative regulation of cerebrovascular function in humans.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
-Review of literature
-Engage in journal discussions and critiques 
-Learn and apply principals of cerebrovascular research techniques, including:
	Sonography of the extra- and internal- cranial arteries
	Indirect / non-invasive measures of arterial blood gases
	LabChart® / data analysis. 
-Contribute to data collection
-Contribute to data dissemination

Project_Title: Using isometric resistance exercise to better‐manage blood pressure
Project_Description:
: Hypertension (HTN) is the leading global risk factor for death and disability. Despite past therapeutic advances, even today 50‐70% of patients receiving medical treatment worldwide have blood pressure (BP) values above clinical targets. This is of serious concern, as uncontrolled HTN greatly increases the risk for cardiovascular complications and early death. While recent trends suggest greater HTN awareness and treatment overall, many people do not have their BP adequately controlled despite optimal traditional treatments. Therefore, an adjunct novel therapy that can increase the number of people with adequately controlled HTN is of tremendous public health importance.
Isometric resistance training (IRT), most commonly performed using a programmable handgrip dynamometer (~500 USD), is recognized by the American Heart Association as a promising nonpharmacological approach to manage high BP. This is founded on the work of our research group and others. In effort to expand proof‐of‐concept evidence, this work is designed to: 1) to better understand IRT in hypertensive patients with treated yet uncontrolled high BP, and 2) to investigate less expensive IRT alternatives.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
1.   Skills development
2.   Participant recruitment
3.   Data Collection, analysis and interpretation
4.   Protocol and equipment management
5.   Scientific writing and presentation

Project_Title: Understanding TSC2 C4CBI Interaction
Project_Description: Using transection, immunofluorescence/precipitation and fluorescent microscopy to understand the interactions between TSC2 mutants and C4CBl.

Project_Title: Membership Attack Against Federated Learning
Project_Description: iven a Machine Learning (ML) model and a record, membership attacks determine whether this record was used as part of the model’s training dataset. Shokri et al. [R. Shokri, M. Stronati, and V. Shmatikov. Membership inference attacks against machine learning models. CoRR, abs/1610.05820, 2016.] have investigated this attack in the most difficult setting, where the adversary’s access to the model is limited to black-box queries that return the model’s output on a given input. Membership inference can present a risk to private datasets (e.g., healthcare datasets) if these datasets are used to train machine learning models and access to the resulting models is open to the public. For example, knowing that a certain patient’s record was used to train a model associated with a disease can reveal that the patient has this disease. This is also important for ML services such as Google Prediction, as an inability to guarantee the privacy of training data would preclude a significant number of customers from using their services.  In federated learning, multiple data owners (e.g., hospitals or government agencies) would benefit from executing a ML task on their aggregated data. However, they do not want to reveal their sensitive data to each other because of intellectual property or privacy acts. Membership ship attacks have not been investigated in the case of federated learning and privacy-preserving federated learning. We will study the effect of  the membership attack in these settings, and we will investigate the effect of different mitigation techniques against the membership attack in these settings.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
- Understand the membership attack when we have one target model.
- Implement the membership attack in the federated learning setting.
- Study and analyze the effect of the membership attack in the federated learning setting
- Study and compare the effect of the mitigation techniques (e.g., regularization and noise addition) against the membership attack in the federated learning setting.

Project_Title: Development of Advanced SAS Analysis Software
Project_Description: The treatment and analysis of small angle scattering (SAS) data is always a technical hurdle and a barrier for many groups. Through this project, we aim to develop a web based analysis package for researchers to use. The goal is to make SAS accessible to non-experts to increase the applications of the technique.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Student will implement existing SAS models into a web based application. The student will use Python to create both the user interface as well as the background computational machniery.

Project_Title: Discovery and Characterization of Novel Inhibitors for Viral Polymerases
Project_Description:
Positive-strand RNA viruses include many common and serious pathogens,
including viruses that cause respiratory diseases (COVID-19 and common colds), hepatitis (HepA and
HepC), encephalitis (polio, enterovirus and flaviviruses like Dengue and West Nile) and gastroenteritis
(norovirus). This research project builds on our previous research on the polymerase enzymes central
to the essential process of genome replication in all positive-strand RNA viruses. Our recent work
focuses primarily on exploitying key structure-function relationships in the viral polymerases of SARSCoV-2 and human norovirus to design, synthesize and test the activity of novel compounds that may
act as inhibitors of these specific enzymes, as well as a broader range of polymerase enzymes from
related viruses. The goal of this work is to discover inhibitors that are suitable as lead compounds for
further development as antiviral therapeutics. This work is urgently critical due to the lack of effective
antiviral treatments for many viral diseases that pose serious threats to public health.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
: Students will analyze the sequences and structures of viral
polymerases, as well as their interactions with inhibitors using a variety of computational tools. In
addition students will work with graduate students and post-doctoral fellows to express, purify, assay
and crystallize polymerase enzymes and accessory proteins to screen and characterize the inhibitory
effects of putative inhibitors on a variety of recombinant viral polymerases expressed in E. coli.

Project_Title: The Mathematics of GPS
Project_Description:
GPS is used daily in our lives. Its importance in today’s navigation can hardly be overestimated. In this project we use linear algebra to explore and understand how GPS works. The machanism of GPS is a variation of geodesic techique of triangulation. Signals from four different satellites  arrive simultaneously at a receiver.  The data is turned into linear equations involving the the coordinates of the receiver. The system is solved to find the location of the receiver.

In this project, we explore these ideas in more details using numerical data.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: The candidate will research the literature of the subject. Examples of this literature includes Dan Kalman’s and G. Stang’s papers. The terminology, methodology, and numerical examples of these papers will be discussed in our biweekly meetings.  At the end of the project, the candidate applies the methods to a data set and summarizes the findings in an expository article.

Project_Title: Carbohydrate Mimics as a New Paradigm for Cancer Vaccines
Project_Description: Carbohydrates play essential roles in cancer pathology. Unfortuantely, carbohydrates are readily metabolized in the body. To develop true carbohydrate vaccines, alternative strucutres are required. This project involves the synthesis of carbohydrate mimics for their eventual evaluation as cancer vaccines. The current efforts in this project are focused exclusively in synthetic organic chemistry, although in future years this project will involve molecular biology, immunology, supramolecular chemistry, and materials science research both within our lab and with our immunology, physician, and biology collaborators from around the world.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Synthetic organic chemistry. Research, writing and communication. Senior students will also be given the opportunity for supervision after a year once well established in the lab. Small team collaboration is essential.

Project_Title: Shall they Wait or Shall They Pay? Queueing and Social Choice
Project_Description:
In many situations, agents queue to get access to common resources: operating rooms, government employees, expensive lab equipment, etc. As a society, we must determine how to organize efficiently these queues, and also, we must determine how many queues to create. We face the tradeoff of increasing the number or resources (rooms, employees, equipment) or having long queues, i.e. between the cost of extra resources and the extra waiting costs.
The next task is to divide the total costs (expenditures on resources + waiting costs) among agents. This distribution is based on game-theoretic principles: we try to provide cost distributions that satisfy fairness principles, and provide agents with various incentives, like truthfully reporting their waiting costs.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
•	Literature review of Operations research literature on queueing algorithms
•	Adapting algorithms to our context
•	Building “random problem generator” to test some features
•	Proofreading manuscripts

Project_Title: Computational Development of New Auto-Immune Peptides
Project_Description: Autoimmune diseases, like multiple sclerosis, type I diabetes, rheumatoid arthritis, inflammatory bowel syndrome, celiac disease and narcolepsy are serious conditions affecting Canada. There are no cures, only symptom management therapies. All arise from the same problem: the immune system recognizes benign peptides (amino acid chains) as dangerous and mounts an immune response. We are looking to interfere with this process by creating new peptides that are rationally desinged through computational techniques to better bind to the receptors that cause all these problems. This project involves computational chemsitry, and the applicant needs to be comfortable with coding formalities. Consequently, the project ma ybe suitable for studnets outside the department of chemistry and in the departments of computer science or the faculty of engineering. However, anyone with an interest and skill in computer programming and immunology are encouraged to apply.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Computational programming and analysis of peptide-protein interactions. Research, writing and communication. Senior students will also be given the opportunity for supervision after a year once well established in the lab. Small team collaboration is essential.

Project_Title: Human anatomy instructional practices.
Project_Description: instruction, and to determine which teaching resources provide the best return (e.g., learning) on investment (e.g., capital cost) within Kinesiology programs, a national online survey of college and university programs will be conducted.  Based on the results of this study, new human anatomy labs will be developed for an existing anatomy course in Kinesiology that align with practices across the country.  In addition, a new course will be established which incorporates instructional practices and teaching resources that are consistent with the teaching and learning research on anatomy instruction, and with the best practices of comparable programs in Canada.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: The successful candidate may contribute to several ongoing research projects related to the instruction of human anatomy in higher education.  Possible contributions include analysis of data from a national survey of anatomy teaching methods and resources, and the development of laboratory activities for face-to-face and online course delivery.  The successful candidate may also contribute to the development of a new anatomy course (lectures and labs) for Kinesiology that is consistent with what is delivered in comparable programs in Canada.

Project_Title: On Wage Differentials Among Recent Graduates
Project_Description: The student will collect data on recent graduates of the Economics program at UWindsor using the departmental LinkedIn account. Comparing this with aggregate Canadian data, inference will be made regarding earnings of these graduates relative to the national distribution. A comparison among graduates of the undergraduate and graduate programs will allow to compute the earning premium associated with an advanced degree. The study will extend the analysis to an earning premium of Outstanding Scholars.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: Creating an Excel file with data. Performing statistical analysis.

Project_Title: Graph Algebras and Their Applications
Project_Description:
Graph algebras provide many important sources of important and intereting examples of both self and
non-self operator algebras. They have been attracting a lot of attention since 1980’s.
This project aims at exploring some important examples and their applications.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks: (1) Read some research papers on directed graphs, Cuntz-Krieger families, the Cuntz-Krieger uniqueness theorem and the gauge-invariant uniqueness theorem, and (2) meet me for weekly discussion.

Project_Title: Self-Immolative Polymers, Secondary Research
Project_Description:
Self-immolative polymers are materials that depolymerise when exposed to an external stimulus. This
triggered degradation makes them ideal for high-value applications such as drug delivery, where they
could be used for selective targeting of disease states through controlled release of active
pharmaceutical ingredients. This rapidly expanding field has attracted considerable multidisciplinary
attention, with contributors from across the chemical and materials disciplines. However, this broad
interest has led to the scholarship within the field lacking uniform standards when reporting
characterisation data, physical properties and synthetic (and degradative) procedures. The Trant group
seeks to add to the scholarship within this area by producing a comprehensive book to set the gold
standard within the literature. This will involve extensive review and comparison of the literature, with
sections on synthesis, characterisation and phsyico-mechanical properties. Students engaged with this
project will be involved in extensive literature research directed towards this goal and will be trained
in techniques for searching and obtaining literature data and will present their findings in regular
meetings. They will furthemore make written contributions to appropriate chapters within the book,
receiving authorship credit for their work. The study of these techniques will additionally translate in
future years towards their lab-based studies within the group.
Student_Opportunities_and_Tasks:
Research, writing and communication. Senior students will also be given the opportunity for
supervision after a year once well established in the group. Small team collaboration is essential.
